topics.title: Introduction to Steel and Steel Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET602 - DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Steel and Steel Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Structural Steel?

**Structural Steel** is a category of carbon steel used in construction, manufactured in accordance with standardized cross-sectional shapes and mechanical properties defined by codes such as **IS 2062:2011** (Indian Standard for Hot Rolled Medium and High Tensile Structural Steel). It is essentially an alloy of **iron (Fe)** with carefully controlled additions of **carbon (C)** (typically **0.15% to 0.30%**), **manganese (Mn)**, **silicon (Si)**, and trace elements that give it the strength, ductility, and weldability required for load-bearing engineering applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (verbatim expected in exams):**
> *Structural steel is a ductile alloy of iron and carbon, with carbon content typically between 0.15% and 0.30%, possessing a well-defined yield point, high strength-to-weight ratio, and standardized mechanical properties that make it suitable for load-bearing structural members in buildings, bridges, towers, and industrial structures.*

---

## 1.2 What is a Steel Structure?

A **Steel Structure** is an engineered assembly of hot-rolled or fabricated steel members (beams, columns, trusses, plates, angles, channels) connected together by riveting, bolting, or welding, designed to carry applied loads safely and economically throughout its design life as per the relevant Indian Standard (primarily **IS 800:2007** — General Construction in Steel).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Skeleton

Think of a steel structure as the **skeleton inside a human body**. Just as our bones provide a rigid framework that supports muscles, organs, and skin while allowing us to stand, walk, and lift loads, structural steel members provide the rigid framework of a building or bridge.

| Human Body | Steel Structure |
|------------|----------------|
| **Bones** (long, hollow, strong) | **Columns & Beams** (I-sections, hollow tubes) |
| **Joints** (knees, elbows) | **Beam-Column Connections** (bolted/welded) |
| **Skull** (protects brain) | **Roof Truss** (protects from rain/sun) |
| **Spine** (carries overall load) | **Lateral Bracing** (resists wind/seismic) |
| **Muscles** (allow movement) | **Shear Connectors** (composite action) |

The brilliance of steel is that it is **strong in tension AND compression** — unlike our bones, which fracture more easily under tension. This duality, combined with its predictability under load, is what makes steel the **backbone of modern skyscrapers**.

---

## 1.4 Brief Historical Timeline (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **1779** | First **Iron Bridge** over River Severn, UK — birth of metal structures |
| **1889** | **Eiffel Tower**, Paris — wrought iron, 300 m tall — demonstrated metal's potential |
| **1890s** | Introduction of **Bessemer Process** — mass production of steel |
| **1931** | **Empire State Building**, NYC — first skyscraper with riveted steel frame |
| **1950s** | **Welding** replaces riveting as primary joining method |
| **1998** | **Petronas Towers**, Malaysia — high-strength steel at 452 m |
| **2020s** | **Burj Khalifa** (828 m) — uses Grade **B 65 / B 70** steel |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If a question asks *"Why is steel preferred for tall buildings?"*, mention: high strength-to-weight ratio, prefabrication, speed of construction, and the existence of reliable design codes.

---

## 1.5 Standard Designations Used in India

| Designation | Old Name | Yield Stress ($f_y$) | Ultimate Stress ($f_u$) |
|-------------|----------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **E 250 (Fe 410)** | Mild Steel | **250 MPa** | **410 MPa** |
| **E 300 (Fe 440)** | — | **300 MPa** | **440 MPa** |
| **E 350 (Fe 490)** | High Tensile | **350 MPa** | **490 MPa** |
| **E 410 (Fe 540)** | High Tensile | **410 MPa** | **540 MPa** |
| **E 450 (Fe 570)** | High Tensile | **450 MPa** | **570 MPa** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The new designation uses **E** (the actual yield stress in MPa) followed by the older **Fe-grade** in brackets. **E 250 (Fe 410)** is the **most commonly used grade** in Indian construction and is the default assumed grade unless otherwise specified in KTU problems.

---

## 1.6 Physical Constants of Structural Steel

| Property | Symbol | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Unit Weight | $\gamma$ | **78.5 kN/m³** |
| Density | $\rho$ | **7850 kg/m³** |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** (200 GPa) |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\mu$ | **0.30** |
| Shear Modulus | $G$ | **76.92 kN/mm²** ≈ 77 GPa |
| Coefficient of Thermal Expansion | $\alpha$ | **12 × 10⁻⁶ / °C** |
| Melting Point | — | **≈ 1500 °C** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Properties of Structural Steel

The behaviour of steel under load is best understood through its **Stress-Strain Curve**, which is unique among structural materials because it has a **clearly defined yield point**.

### 2.1.1 Key Points on the Stress-Strain Curve

1. **Proportional Limit (Point A):** Stress is linearly proportional to strain (Hooke's Law valid).
2. **Elastic Limit:** Maximum stress up to which steel returns to its original shape on unloading.
3. **Upper Yield Point ($f_y$ upper):** Stress at which yielding first begins.
4. **Lower Yield Point ($f_y$ lower):** Steady value of stress during yielding — this is the **design yield stress**.
5. **Strain Hardening Region:** After yielding, steel regains strength up to **Ultimate Stress ($f_u$)**.
6. **Necking Region:** Local reduction in area begins.
7. **Fracture Point:** Steel finally breaks at a strain of typically **20–25%** for mild steel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Concept:** The presence of a definite yield point allows steel to redistribute stresses in **statically indeterminate structures** (e.g., continuous beams, portal frames). Concrete, by contrast, has no defined yield point — this is a fundamental philosophical difference that drives **Limit State Design** philosophy in steel.

---

## 2.2 Advantages of Steel Structures

| Sl. No. | Advantage | Engineering Implication |
|---------|-----------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **High Strength-to-Weight Ratio** | Reduces dead load, enabling longer spans & taller buildings |
| 2 | **Speed of Construction** | Prefabricated members → faster erection on site |
| 3 | **Uniformity & Predictability** | Standardised sections → reliable design |
| 4 | **Ductility** | Withstands large deformations before collapse → **earthquake resistant** |
| 5 | **Reversibility / Reuse** | Members can be dismantled and reused |
| 6 | **Long Spans without Intermediate Columns** | Ideal for **airports, stadiums, warehouses** |
| 7 | **Can be strengthened later** | Sections can be supplemented with plates (retrofitting) |
| 8 | **Recyclable** | Environmentally sustainable — up to **90% recycled content** |

---

## 2.3 Disadvantages of Steel Structures

| Sl. No. | Disadvantage | Mitigation Strategy |
|---------|--------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Corrosion (Rust)** | Painting, galvanizing, weathering steel, epoxy coatings |
| 2 | **Fire Vulnerability** | Loses 50% strength at ~550 °C → fire-proof cladding required |
| 3 | **Buckling Susceptibility** | Slender members fail in buckling → use stockier sections |
| 4 | **High Initial Cost** | Offset by speed & reduced foundation cost |
| 5 | **Fatigue under Cyclic Loading** | Requires special detailing for bridges, cranes |
| 6 | **Skilled Labour Required** | Welding & high-strength bolting need trained workforce |

---

## 2.4 Types of Steel Structures (Classification)

Steel structures can be broadly classified based on their **structural action**:

| Category | Examples | Typical Span / Use |
|----------|----------|---------------------|
| **Tension Structures** | Cables, suspension bridge hangers, Tensegrity | Very long spans (bridges > 1 km) |
| **Compression Structures** | Columns, towers, arches | Buildings, transmission towers |
| **Flexural Structures** | Beams, girders | Floor/roof of buildings |
| **Trusses** | Industrial roof trusses, bridge trusses, space frames | Warehouses, stadiums |
| **Frame Structures** | Portal frames, multi-storey braced frames | Industrial sheds, high-rises |
| **Shell / Plate Structures** | Storage tanks, silos, chimneys, pressure vessels | Liquids, gases, power plants |
| **Hybrid Structures** | Steel-concrete composite | Modern buildings & bridges |

---

## 2.5 Design Philosophies — Working Stress Method (WSM) vs Limit State Method (LSM)

This is a **favourite KTU question topic**.

### Working Stress Method (WSM) — Old Approach
- Based on **elastic theory**.
- Structure is designed so that the **maximum stress under working loads** does not exceed a permissible stress.
- Permissible stress = Yield Stress / **Factor of Safety (≈ 1.67)**.

### Limit State Method (LSM) — Modern Approach (IS 800:2007)
- Considers **ultimate limit state (collapse)** and **serviceability limit state (deflection, vibration, crack)**.
- Uses **partial safety factors** on loads (γ_f) and materials (γ_m).

$$ \text{Design Strength} = \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} $$

| Factor | WSM | LSM |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Basis | Elastic | Probabilistic / Limit State |
| Factor of Safety | Single (≈ 1.67) | Partial (γ_f on loads, γ_m on materials) |
| Economical? | Conservative | More economical |
| Adopted in | IS 800:1984 | **IS 800:2007 (Current KTU Code)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** All numerical problems under KTU 2024 scheme PCCET602 must use **Limit State Method as per IS 800:2007**. The older WSM is mentioned only for context and historical understanding.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU steel design question.**

| Sl. No. | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---------|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Unit Weight of Steel | $\gamma_{steel} = 78.5$ | kN/m³ | Universal constant in IS 800 |
| 2 | Weight of Steel Plate | $W = 78.5 \times t \times A$ | kN | $t$ = thickness (m), $A$ = area (m²) |
| 3 | Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | N/mm² | Valid up to proportional limit |
| 4 | Young's Modulus of Steel | $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ | N/mm² | IS 800 value |
| 5 | Shear Modulus | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\mu)} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^5}{2(1+0.3)}$ | N/mm² | ≈ 76923 N/mm² |
| 6 | Section Modulus (Elastic) | $Z_e = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | mm³ | $I$ = MOI, $y_{max}$ = extreme fibre distance |
| 7 | Plastic Section Modulus | $Z_p = 2 \times A \times \bar{y}$ | mm³ | For doubly symmetric sections $Z_p \approx 1.14 Z_e$ |
| 8 | Shape Factor | $S = \dfrac{Z_p}{Z_e}$ | — | Rectangular = 1.5, I-section ≈ 1.14 |
| 9 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}}$ | — | $r_{min} = \sqrt{I/A}$ |
| 10 | Radius of Gyration | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | mm | |
| 11 | Bending Stress (LSM Design) | $M_d = \dfrac{Z_p \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$ | N·mm | $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ |
| 12 | Design Strength (Material) | $f_d = \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$ | N/mm² | $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ for steel |
| 13 | Partial Safety Factor (Material) | $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$, $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ | — | $\gamma_{m1}$ used for ultimate stress |
| 14 | Dead Load (Self-Weight) | $DL = \gamma_{steel} \times V$ | kN | $V$ = volume of member |
| 15 | Thermal Stress | $\sigma_t = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | N/mm² | $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

These formulas are **not academic exercises** — they directly drive:
- **Design of Industrial Sheds** (trusses, purlins, gable columns) → IS 800:2007
- **Multi-storey Buildings** (braced frames, moment connections) → IS 800:2007
- **Bridges** (plate girders, box girders) → IS 800:2007 + IRC codes
- **Transmission Towers** (lattice angle members) → IS 802
- **Chimneys & Silos** (shell design) → IS 6533, IS 9178

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Section Modulus of a Rectangular Section

Consider a rectangular cross-section of breadth **b** and depth **d**. We need to derive the elastic section modulus $Z_e$.

**Step 1:** Locate the Neutral Axis (N.A.)
For a rectangular section, by symmetry, the N.A. passes through the centroid. For a rectangle with its longer side along the bending axis, the distance from N.A. to extreme fibre is:

$$ y_{max} = \frac{d}{2} $$

**Step 2:** Compute the Moment of Inertia (M.O.I.) about the N.A.
Using the standard formula for a rectangle about its centroidal axis:

$$ I_{xx} = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12} $$

**Step 3:** Apply the definition of Section Modulus

$$ Z_e = \frac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} = \frac{\dfrac{b \cdot d^3}{12}}{\dfrac{d}{2}} $$

**Step 4:** Simplify the expression

$$ Z_e = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12} \cdot \frac{2}{d} $$

$$ \boxed{Z_e = \frac{b \cdot d^2}{6}} \quad \text{(units: mm}^3 \text{)} $$

> **Conversion Logic:** We cancelled one factor of $d$ in the numerator with the $d$ in the denominator of $y_{max}$, which is why the final formula has $d^2$ instead of $d^3$. This is a **very high-yield derivation** — students often forget the factor of $\frac{1}{6}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Section Modulus of a Circular Section

For a solid circular section of diameter **D**:

**Step 1:** M.O.I. of a circle about centroidal axis:

$$ I = \frac{\pi D^4}{64} $$

**Step 2:** Distance to extreme fibre:

$$ y_{max} = \frac{D}{2} $$

**Step 3:** Section modulus:

$$ Z_e = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}}{\dfrac{D}{2}} $$

$$ Z_e = \frac{\pi D^4}{64} \cdot \frac{2}{D} $$

$$ \boxed{Z_e = \frac{\pi D^3}{32}} \quad \text{(units: mm}^3 \text{)} $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relation between Shear Modulus and Young's Modulus

**Step 1:** Start from the fundamental elastic relationship:

$$ E = 2G(1 + \mu) $$

This is derived from elasticity theory linking volumetric and shear strain.

**Step 2:** Solve for G:

$$ G = \frac{E}{2(1+\mu)} $$

**Step 3:** Substitute IS 800 values: $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\mu = 0.30$

$$ G = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{2(1 + 0.30)} $$

$$ G = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{2 \times 1.30} $$

$$ G = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{2.60} $$

$$ \boxed{G \approx 76923 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 76.92 \text{ kN/mm}^2} $$

> **Practical Utility:** This value is used in design of members subjected to **torsion** (e.g., crane girders, cantilever sign supports) and in **web shear** calculations of plate girders.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Plastic Section Modulus of a Rectangle

The plastic section modulus assumes the entire cross-section yields (i.e., stress = $f_y$ everywhere). For bending, the section is split equally above and below the plastic neutral axis (PNA).

**Step 1:** Plastic Neutral Axis for a doubly-symmetric section is the centroid itself.
**Step 2:** Area above PNA:

$$ A_{above} = b \cdot \frac{d}{2} $$

**Step 3:** Distance from PNA to centroid of upper half:

$$ \bar{y} = \frac{d}{4} $$

**Step 4:** Plastic Section Modulus:

$$ Z_p = 2 \times A_{above} \times \bar{y} = 2 \times \left( \frac{bd}{2} \right) \times \left( \frac{d}{4} \right) $$

$$ Z_p = 2 \times \frac{bd}{2} \times \frac{d}{4} = \frac{bd^2}{4} $$

$$ \boxed{Z_p = \frac{bd^2}{4}} $$

**Step 5:** Shape Factor

$$ S = \frac{Z_p}{Z_e} = \frac{\dfrac{bd^2}{4}}{\dfrac{bd^2}{6}} = \frac{6}{4} $$

$$ \boxed{S_{rectangle} = 1.5} $$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Weight per Metre of a Standard I-Section

Consider an **ISMB 500** (Indian Standard Medium Weight Beam of depth 500 mm). The standard catalogue gives:

- Mass per metre: $m = 86.9$ kg/m
- Cross-sectional area: $A = 110.74$ cm²

We verify the relationship:

$$ W = \gamma_{steel} \times A \times g \quad \text{(as force per unit length)} $$

But in standard form:

$$ m = \rho \times A $$

$$ m = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 110.74 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 $$

$$ m = 7850 \times 0.011074 $$

$$ m \approx 86.93 \text{ kg/m} $$

This matches the catalogue value (small rounding difference), confirming:

$$ \boxed{W_{per\ metre} = \rho \cdot A \quad \text{or} \quad W_{per\ metre} = \frac{\gamma \cdot A}{g}} $$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Properties Summary of Common Sections

| Section | Area $A$ | M.O.I. $I_{xx}$ | Section Modulus $Z_e$ | Plastic Modulus $Z_p$ | Shape Factor $S$ |
|---------|----------|-----------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| **Rectangle** $b \times d$ | $bd$ | $\dfrac{bd^3}{12}$ | $\dfrac{bd^2}{6}$ | $\dfrac{bd^2}{4}$ | **1.50** |
| **Solid Circle** $\phi D$ | $\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $\dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$ | $\dfrac{\pi D^3}{32}$ | $\dfrac{D^3}{6}$ | **≈ 1.70** |
| **Hollow Circle** $D_o, D_i$ | $\dfrac{\pi (D_o^2 - D_i^2)}{4}$ | $\dfrac{\pi (D_o^4 - D_i^4)}{64}$ | $\dfrac{\pi (D_o^4 - D_i^4)}{32 D_o}$ | — | ≈ 1.27 |
| **I-Section (Doubly Sym)** | Sum of rectangles | Tabulated in SP-6 | $\dfrac{I_{xx}}{(d/2)}$ | Sum of $A\bar{y}$ products | **≈ 1.14** |
| **Diamond / Rhombus** | $\dfrac{bd}{2}$ | $\dfrac{bd^3}{48}$ | $\dfrac{bd^2}{24}$ | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** The **shape factor for I-sections is ~1.14**, meaning that at collapse, an I-section can carry **~14% more moment** than at first yield. This is why LSM is more economical than WSM — it exploits this reserve capacity through partial safety factors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.3 Stress-Strain Behaviour of Mild Steel (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Load Applied] --> B[Stage 1: Elastic Region]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Yield Plateau]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Strain Hardening]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Necking]
    E --> F[Stage 5: Fracture]

    B --> B1[Stress linear with strain\nHookes Law valid\nE = 200 GPa]
    C --> C1[Stress constant at fy\nLarge strain with no load increase\nDesign Yield Point]
    D --> D1[Stress rises to fu\nfu typically 1.5 to 1.7 times fy\nStrain 12 to 20 times yield strain]
    E --> E1[Local area reduction\nElongation becomes non uniform]
    F --> F1[Final rupture\nTotal elongation 20 to 25 percent\nIndicates ductility]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style F fill:#8B0000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

---

```mermaid
    B -->|Pre-2007| C[Working Stress Method\nWSM]
    B -->|Post-2007| D[Limit State Method\nLSM]

    C --> C1[Permissible Stress\nfp = fy divided by FoS\nFoS = 1.67]
    C --> C2[Single Safety Factor]

    C --> C4[IS 800:1984]
    C --> C5[Conservative Design]

    D --> D1[Design Stress\nfd = fy divided by gamma m0\ngamma m0 = 1.10]
    D --> D2[Partial Safety Factors\non Load and Material]
    D --> D3[Plastic Theory allowed\nafter first yield]
    D --> D4[IS 800:2007]
    D --> D5[Economical Design]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six advantages of steel as a structural material.**

**Model Answer:**

> Steel is one of the most preferred structural materials due to the following advantages:
>
> 1. **High Strength-to-Weight Ratio** — Steel has a unit weight of only **78.5 kN/m³** but can carry loads comparable to much heavier materials, enabling longer spans and reduced foundation sizes.
> 2. **High Ductility** — Steel elongates by **20–25%** before fracture, allowing visible deformation prior to failure, which is critical for seismic resistance.
> 3. **Speed of Construction** — Prefabricated members can be erected on site rapidly, reducing project timelines significantly.
> 4. **Uniformity of Properties** — Being a manufactured product, steel has consistent and predictable mechanical properties governed by IS 2062.
> 5. **Reusability & Recyclability** — Steel members can be dismantled and reused; up to **90%** of structural steel is recycled without loss of properties.
> 6. **Ease of Strengthening** — Existing steel members can be reinforced by adding plates or supplementary sections, facilitating retrofitting.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Working Stress Method (WSM) and Limit State Method (LSM) of design.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Working Stress Method (WSM) | Limit State Method (LSM) |
|-----------|-----------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Basis** | Elastic theory — stresses under service loads | Probability-based — considers both strength and serviceability |
| **Safety Provision** | Single factor of safety (FoS ≈ 1.67) | Partial safety factors on loads (γ_f) and materials (γ_m) |
| **Stress Considered** | Permissible stress = $f_y / 1.67$ | Design stress = $f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ where $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ |
| **Failure Theory** | No yielding at working loads | Ensures collapse and serviceability limit states are not exceeded |
| **Code Reference** | IS 800:1984 (now withdrawn) | **IS 800:2007 (current KTU code)** |
| **Economy** | More conservative, less economical | More realistic, economical |
| **Plastic Analysis** | Not permitted | Permitted beyond first yield |

> **Conclusion:** LSM is preferred and mandated by IS 800:2007 for all current steel design practice in India.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme uses **internal choice** between Question A and Question B for Module-level examination.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1 Compulsory]`

**(a) Explain the mechanical properties of structural steel with a neat sketch of the stress-strain curve. List the standard steel grades used in India as per IS 2062:2011.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Stress-Strain Curve Stages:**

A typical stress-strain diagram for **mild steel (E 250 / Fe 410)** under uniaxial tension exhibits the following distinct stages:

1. **Stage O-A: Proportional Limit** — Stress is linearly proportional to strain. Hooke's Law is valid: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$, where $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm².
2. **Stage A-B: Elastic Limit** — Steel behaves elastically up to this point; on unloading, it returns to its original dimensions.
3. **Stage B-C: Yield Plateau** — Stress remains nearly constant while strain increases significantly. The **lower yield point** ($f_y$) is used for design.
4. **Stage C-D: Strain Hardening** — Stress rises again until reaching the ultimate tensile strength ($f_u$).
5. **Stage D-E: Necking** — Local reduction in cross-sectional area begins; engineering stress decreases even though true stress continues to rise.
6. **Point E: Fracture** — Final rupture after significant elongation (typically 20–25%).

**Standard Steel Grades (IS 2062:2011):**

| New Designation | Old Designation | Yield Stress $f_y$ (MPa) | Ultimate Stress $f_u$ (MPa) |
|-----------------|-----------------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| E 250 | Fe 410 | 250 | 410 |
| E 300 | Fe 440 | 300 | 440 |
| E 350 | Fe 490 | 350 | 490 |
| E 410 | Fe 540 | 410 | 540 |
| E 450 | Fe 570 | 450 | 570 |

> **Conclusion:** The well-defined yield point makes steel ideal for limit state design, and IS 2062 ensures consistent quality across all these grades.

---

**(b) A steel plate of dimensions 600 mm × 400 mm × 12 mm is used as a tension member in a truss. Calculate: (i) Self-weight of the plate, (ii) Stress induced when subjected to an axial tensile load of 240 kN. Take $\gamma_{steel} = 78.5$ kN/m³.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Plate dimensions: $L = 600$ mm $= 0.6$ m, $B = 400$ mm $= 0.4$ m, $t = 12$ mm $= 0.012$ m
- Axial tensile load: $P = 240$ kN
- Unit weight of steel: $\gamma_{steel} = 78.5$ kN/m³

---

**Part (i): Self-weight of the plate**

Step 1: Compute the volume of the plate:

$$ V = L \times B \times t = 0.6 \times 0.4 \times 0.012 $$

$$ V = 0.00288 \text{ m}^3 $$

Step 2: Compute self-weight:

$$ W = \gamma_{steel} \times V $$

$$ W = 78.5 \times 0.00288 $$

$$ \boxed{W = 0.2261 \text{ kN}} $$

---

**Part (ii): Stress induced in the plate**

Step 1: Cross-sectional area resisting tension:

$$ A = B \times t = 0.4 \times 0.012 = 0.0048 \text{ m}^2 $$

Converting to mm² for consistency with N/mm²:

$$ A = 400 \times 12 = 4800 \text{ mm}^2 $$

Step 2: Compute the stress using $\sigma = P/A$:

$$ \sigma = \frac{240 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{4800 \text{ mm}^2} $$

$$ \sigma = \frac{240000}{4800} $$

$$ \boxed{\sigma = 50 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 50 \text{ MPa}} $$

---

**Conclusion:** The induced stress of **50 MPa** is much less than the design strength of E 250 grade steel ($f_d = f_y / \gamma_{m0} = 250 / 1.10 = 227.27$ MPa). Hence, the plate is **SAFE** under the given loading.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 1 Internal Choice]`

**(a) List the various types of steel structures. Explain the properties of structural steel in detail.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Steel Structures:**

Steel structures are classified based on the **type of structural action** and **fabrication method**.

**By Structural Action:**

1. **Tension Structures** — Members carry pure tensile forces (cables, hangers, ties in suspension bridges).
2. **Compression Structures** — Members carry pure compressive forces (columns, struts, chimneys, towers).
3. **Flexural Structures** — Members subjected primarily to bending (beams, girders, purlins).
4. **Trusses** — Assemblies of triangular units used for long spans (roof trusses, bridge trusses).
5. **Frames** — Combinations of beams and columns with rigid or semi-rigid joints (portal frames, multi-storey frames).
6. **Shell Structures** — Thin curved surfaces carrying loads through membrane action (silos, storage tanks, chimneys).
7. **Plate Structures** — Flat plates stiffened by ribs (gusseted connections, plate girders).
8. **Cable-Stayed & Suspension Structures** — Long-span bridges, stadium roofs.

**By Fabrication Method:**

1. **Hot Rolled Sections** — Standard shapes like ISMB, ISMC, ISA produced by hot rolling.
2. **Built-up Sections** — Plate girders, box girders, compound columns fabricated by welding plates and rolled sections.
3. **Cold-Formed Sections** — Thin gauge sections bent at room temperature (light gauge purlins, storage racks).

**Mechanical Properties of Structural Steel:**

| Property | Description | Typical Value |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Yield Stress ($f_y$)** | Stress at which steel begins to deform plastically | 250 MPa (E 250) |
| **Ultimate Tensile Stress ($f_u$)** | Maximum stress steel can carry | 410 MPa (E 250) |
| **Young's Modulus ($E$)** | Measure of stiffness | $2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm² |
| **Poisson's Ratio ($\mu$)** | Lateral strain / Axial strain | 0.30 |
| **Shear Modulus ($G$)** | Resistance to shear deformation | ≈ 76.92 kN/mm² |
| **Elongation** | Ductility measure (% strain at fracture) | 20–25% for mild steel |
| **Density ($\rho$)** | Mass per unit volume | 7850 kg/m³ |
| **Unit Weight ($\gamma$)** | Weight per unit volume | 78.5 kN/m³ |
| **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion ($\alpha$)** | Strain per °C rise | $12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C |
| **Fatigue Strength** | Resistance to cyclic loading | Depends on detail category |

> **Conclusion:** The combination of high strength, predictable ductility, and standardised properties makes steel the most reliable structural material for modern construction.

---

**(b) A simply supported steel beam of span 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of 30 kN/m including its self-weight. The beam is of rectangular cross-section 200 mm wide × 400 mm deep. Determine: (i) Maximum bending moment, (ii) Maximum bending stress. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm².** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Span: $L = 4$ m
- UDL: $w = 30$ kN/m
- Breadth: $b = 200$ mm
- Depth: $d = 400$ mm
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²

---

**Part (i): Maximum Bending Moment**

For a simply supported beam with UDL:

$$ M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{8} $$

Step 1: Substitute values:

$$ M_{max} = \frac{30 \times 4^2}{8} $$

$$ M_{max} = \frac{30 \times 16}{8} $$

$$ M_{max} = \frac{480}{8} $$

$$ \boxed{M_{max} = 60 \text{ kN·m} = 60 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}} $$

---

**Part (ii): Maximum Bending Stress**

Step 1: Compute Moment of Inertia for a rectangular section:

$$ I_{xx} = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{200 \times 400^3}{12} $$

$$ I_{xx} = \frac{200 \times 64 \times 10^6}{12} $$

$$ I_{xx} = \frac{12800 \times 10^6}{12} $$

$$ I_{xx} = 1066.67 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 $$

Step 2: Distance from neutral axis to extreme fibre:

$$ y_{max} = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{400}{2} = 200 \text{ mm} $$

Step 3: Compute section modulus:

$$ Z_e = \frac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} = \frac{1066.67 \times 10^6}{200} $$

$$ Z_e = 5.333 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 $$

Step 4: Compute bending stress using flexure formula:

$$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z_e} = \frac{60 \times 10^6}{5.333 \times 10^6} $$

$$ \boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 11.25 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 11.25 \text{ MPa}} $$

---

**Conclusion:** The induced bending stress of **11.25 MPa** is far below the allowable limit for E 250 steel (≈ 227 MPa in LSM). The beam is **very conservatively safe** and could potentially be redesigned with a smaller cross-section for economy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing kN/m and N/mm² without proper conversion (× 10³ and × 10⁶ factors). Always convert all quantities to consistent units before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting Partial Safety Factor:** In LSM problems, students often forget to divide $f_y$ by $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$. This loses **2–3 marks** in design checks.
> 3. **Confusing Elastic ($Z_e$) and Plastic ($Z_p$) Section Modulus:** Use $Z_e$ for WSM and $Z_p$ for LSM plastic design. Mixing them is a **favourite trap**.
> 4. **Wrong Yield Stress:** Using $f_u$ (ultimate) instead of $f_y$ (yield) for elastic design checks. IS 800 design is based on **yield**, not ultimate.
> 5. **Not mentioning IS Code:** Always cite the relevant IS code (e.g., *"as per IS 800:2007 Cl. ..."*) for full marks.
> 6. **Skipping Units in Final Answer:** Always write the unit (MPa, kN, mm³). A bare number without unit is **incomplete**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Master These Points**

- ✅ **Unit Weight of Steel** = **78.5 kN/m³** (memorize — appears in every problem)
- ✅ **Young's Modulus** $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm² = 200 GPa
- ✅ **Poisson's Ratio** $\mu = 0.30$ → hence $G \approx 77$ GPa
- ✅ **Most common steel grade** = **E 250 (Fe 410)**, with $f_y = 250$ MPa and $f_u = 410$ MPa
- ✅ **Current KTU Code** = **IS 800:2007** (Limit State Method)
- ✅ **Partial Safety Factor for Material** $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$
- ✅ **Shape factor for I-section ≈ 1.14**, Rectangle = 1.5, Circle ≈ 1.7
- ✅ **$Z_e = I/y_{max}$** is for WSM; **$Z_p = 2A\bar{y}$** is for plastic/LSM design
- ✅ **Stress-strain curve has a definite yield point** — this enables plastic design
- ✅ **Steel elongates 20–25%** before fracture → excellent ductility → earthquake resistant
- ✅ **Advantages:** High strength-to-weight ratio, ductility, speed, recyclability
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Corrosion, fire vulnerability, buckling of slender members
- ✅ **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion** $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C
- ✅ **Density** $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³
- ✅ **Old WSM code** = IS 800:1984 (now superseded — **do NOT use in KTU answers**)
- ✅ **Always cite IS code reference** in long answers (IS 800:2007, IS 2062:2011)
- ✅ **Convert all units to N and mm** before plugging into stress formulas
- ✅ **Hot-rolled sections**: ISMB, ISMC, ISA, ISWB, ISHB, ISLB
- ✅ **Self-weight calculation**: $W = \gamma \times V$ where $V$ = volume of member

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Steel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET602 - DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Steel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Steel** is an alloy of iron (Fe) and carbon (C), with carbon content typically ranging from **0.1% to 1.5%**, along with controlled quantities of other alloying elements such as manganese, silicon, phosphorus, and sulphur. For **structural applications** under the Indian Standard (IS 800:2007 & IS 2062:2011), the carbon content is restricted to a maximum of **0.23%** to ensure adequate weldability and ductility.

**Structural Steel** is a specific grade of steel possessing a well-defined set of mechanical and physical properties—high strength, ductility, toughness, and weldability—that make it suitable for load-bearing construction (beams, columns, trusses, plates, etc.).

In the context of KTU's design philosophy, **"Properties of Steel"** refers to the engineering parameters (physical, mechanical, and chemical) that govern the behaviour of structural steel under various loading and environmental conditions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rubber Band & Clay" Intuition

Imagine you are pulling a **rubber band** (high-tensile steel) versus a **piece of chalk** (cast iron / brittle steel):

- The **rubber band stretches a lot** before snapping — this is **ductility** (steel can deform before failure, giving warning).
- The **chalk snaps suddenly** with very little stretch — this is **brittleness** (catastrophic, no warning).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Engineer's Golden Rule for Steel Design:**
> Steel is favoured over concrete because it is **Ductile** (gives visible warning before failure) and **Ductile** (absorbs energy during earthquakes). This is why steel structures are preferred in seismic zones.

Think of structural steel like a **"stretchy but strong athlete's muscle"** — it can carry heavy loads (strength) AND stretch under sudden impact (toughness), unlike a "rigid glass rod" which is strong but shatters.

---

## 1.3 Physical Properties of Steel (Standard Metrics in Bold)

| Physical Property | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Density (γ / ρ)** | **7850 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| **Young's Modulus (E)** | **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 200 GPa** | N/mm² |
| **Shear Modulus (G)** | **0.769 × 10⁵ N/mm² ≈ 77 GPa** | N/mm² |
| **Poisson's Ratio (ν)** | **0.30** | dimensionless |
| **Thermal Expansion Coeff. (α)** | **12 × 10⁻⁶ / °C** | per °C |
| **Melting Point** | **≈ 1500 °C** | °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** In numerical problems, if the value of E or γ is not given in the question, you **MUST** use the standard values above. The Board deducts marks for assuming wrong/missing values.

---

## 1.4 Mechanical Properties of Steel

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanical properties are those which determine the behaviour of steel under applied forces (loads).**

1. **Strength** — Ability to resist applied forces without yielding or failing.
2. **Elasticity** — Ability to regain original shape after removal of load (governed by Young's Modulus E).
3. **Plasticity** — Ability to undergo permanent (irrecoverable) deformation without rupture.
4. **Ductility** — Ability to undergo large plastic deformation before fracture (e.g., % elongation ≥ 23% for structural steel per IS 2062).
5. **Malleability** — Ability to be rolled/hammered into thin sheets (high for mild steel).
6. **Brittleness** — Opposite of ductility; failure occurs with negligible plastic deformation. **Steel becomes brittle at low temperatures**.
7. **Toughness** — Ability to absorb energy and resist fracture under impact/cyclic loading.
8. **Hardness** — Resistance to indentation, scratching, and abrasion.
9. **Fatigue Strength** — Resistance to failure under repeated/reversed cyclic loading.
10. **Creep** — Time-dependent permanent deformation under sustained load (negligible for steel at room temperature, significant above 450 °C).
11. **Weldability** — Ability to be welded without cracking or loss of strength.
12. **Corrosion Resistance** — Resistance to rusting; improved by alloying or galvanizing.

---

## 1.5 Chemical Composition (as per IS 2062:2011)

| Element | % by Weight | Purpose / Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Carbon (C)** | **≤ 0.23%** | Increases strength & hardness; reduces ductility & weldability |
| Manganese (Mn) | ≤ 1.50% | Improves strength, removes oxygen/sulphur |
| Silicon (Si) | ≤ 0.40% | Improves strength, deoxidizer |
| Sulphur (S) | **≤ 0.040%** | **Harmful — causes brittleness, "hot shortness"** |
| Phosphorus (P) | **≤ 0.040%** | **Harmful — causes brittleness, "cold shortness"** |
| Copper (Cu) | ≤ 0.30% | Improves corrosion resistance |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Sulphur and Phosphorus are undesirable impurities. Their total content (S + P) is strictly limited. Memorize: **S ≤ 0.040% and P ≤ 0.040%**.

---

## 1.6 Types of Structural Steel Used in KTU Syllabus

1. **Mild Steel (MS)** — Low carbon content (0.15–0.25%); yield stress = **250 N/mm²** (IS 2062 Grade A).
2. **High Tensile Steel (HTS)** — Higher carbon / alloyed; yield stress = **350 N/mm², 410 N/mm², 450 N/mm²** (e.g., Fe 410, Fe 450).
3. **High Strength Deformed (HSD) Bars** — Used in RCC reinforcement (separate course).
4. **Stainless Steel** — For corrosion-resistant applications; rarely used in main load-bearing structures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Designation (IS 800:2007):** Steel grade "**Fe 410**" means the steel has a **minimum yield stress of 410 N/mm²** for thicknesses ≤ 20 mm. Example: Fe 250, Fe 410, Fe 450 are commonly used grades.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stress-Strain Curve for Mild Steel — The "Heart" of Steel Design

The **stress-strain (σ-ε) curve** is the single most important graph in steel design. It tells the entire story of how steel behaves from loading to failure.

**Five Distinct Regions** (memorize these — they appear in every KTU exam):

**Region I — Elastic Region (O to A)**
- Stress is **directly proportional** to strain (Hooke's Law valid).
- Steel returns to original shape upon unloading.
- Slope = **Young's Modulus (E) = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²**.
- Point A is the **Limit of Proportionality (LOP)**.

**Region II — Elastic-to-Plastic Transition (A to B)**
- Stress is no longer proportional to strain (curve bends).
- Point B = **Elastic Limit** (top of elastic region).

**Region III — Perfectly Plastic / Yield Plateau (B to C)**
- Strain increases **without any increase in stress** (horizontal plateau).
- Point C = **Upper Yield Point (UYP)** — sudden drop occurs.
- Point D = **Lower Yield Point (LYP)** — stable yield.
- The **yield stress (f_y)** is conventionally taken at the **Upper Yield Point**.
- For design, **f_y = 250 N/mm²** for MS.

**Region IV — Strain Hardening (D to E)**
- After yielding, the material gains strength again due to dislocation pile-up.
- Point E = **Ultimate Stress (f_u)** — maximum stress the material can withstand.
- f_u ≈ **410 N/mm²** for Fe 410 grade.

**Region V — Necking & Fracture (E to F)**
- Local reduction in cross-section ("necking") occurs.
- Engineering stress appears to drop; true stress actually rises.
- Point F = **Fracture Point**.
- % elongation at fracture = **23% (minimum for Fe 410)**.

---

## 2.2 Why "Yielding" is the Design Limit (not Ultimate)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Philosophy:** Steel is designed based on **yield stress (f_y)**, NOT ultimate stress. Reason: At yield, the structure undergoes **excessive deformation** (unacceptable for serviceability). Using f_u would mean the structure is severely deformed but not collapsed — a **serviceability failure**.

**Design Strength (f_d)** used in limit state design:

$$f_d = \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

where:
- $f_y$ = yield stress of steel (250 / 410 / 450 N/mm²)
- $\gamma_{m0}$ = **partial safety factor for material = 1.10** (for shop-fabricated steel)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Axial stress | N/mm² |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ | Strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = 2 \times 10^5$ | Young's Modulus (for steel) | N/mm² |
| 4 | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = 0.769 \times 10^5$ | Shear Modulus relation | N/mm² |
| 5 | $\nu = 0.30$ | Poisson's Ratio for steel | — |
| 6 | $f_y = 250$ (MS), $410$, $450$ | Yield stress grades | N/mm² |
| 7 | $f_d = \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{f_y}{1.10}$ | Design strength | N/mm² |
| 8 | $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10, \gamma_{m1} = 1.25, \gamma_{m2} = 1.25$ | Partial safety factors (IS 800) | — |
| 9 | $\tau_v = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Shear stress | N/mm² |
| 10 | $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ | Coefficient of thermal expansion | /°C |
| 11 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal elongation | mm |
| 12 | $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Density of steel | kg/m³ |
| 13 | $\gamma_{\text{steel}} = \rho \cdot g = 76.98 \text{ kN/m}^3$ | Unit weight | kN/m³ |
| 14 | $\% \text{ Elongation} = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Ductility measure | % |
| 15 | $f_u \approx 1.4 \text{ to } 1.6 \times f_y$ | Ultimate to yield ratio (typical) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule Applied:** All absolute values and vertical bars (like $\vert f_y \vert$ or $\mid E \mid$) use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Notice how the table above is rendered cleanly.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Seismic Design** — Ductility of steel (Region III plateau) is the *fundamental reason* steel structures survive earthquakes.
2. **Tall Buildings** — High strength-to-weight ratio of Fe 410 / Fe 450 allows slender members.
3. **Bridges** — Fatigue strength is critical for cyclic traffic loading.
4. **Industrial Buildings** — Welded connections exploit the weldability of low-carbon steel.
5. **Cold-Formed Sections** — Ductility ensures sections can be roll-formed without cracking.
6. **Fire Engineering** — Steel loses strength rapidly above 450 °C (creep & yield stress drops); hence fireproofing is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between E, G, and ν (for Steel)

The **three elastic constants** of any isotropic material (like steel) are related by:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

**Derivation (Step-by-step):**

**Step 1:** Consider a 2D element under uniaxial tensile stress $\sigma_x$ in the x-direction.

The strains in x and y directions are:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_y = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_x = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

**Step 2:** Now consider the same element under pure shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ acting on its faces.

The shear strain produced is:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}$$

**Step 3:** In a pure shear state, the principal stresses on the rotated 45° element are:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}$$

**Step 4:** The principal strain in the direction of $\sigma_1$ (i.e., 45° to the x-axis) is, by superposition of the two principal strains:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma_1}{E} - \nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_2}{E} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{E} - \nu \cdot \frac{\left(-\tau_{xy}\right)}{E} = \frac{\tau_{xy}(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5:** From geometry of deformation, the shear strain is twice the principal tensile strain at 45°:

$$\gamma_{xy} = 2 \varepsilon_1 = \frac{2\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6:** But by definition, $\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\tau_{xy}}{G}$. Equating:

$$\frac{\tau_{xy}}{G} = \frac{2\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 7:** Cancel $\tau_{xy}$ from both sides and rearrange:

$$\boxed{\,E = 2G(1+\nu)\,}$$

**Step 8 (Verification for steel):**
Substitute $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm² and $\nu = 0.30$:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{2.60} = 0.7692 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

This matches the standard value of **G ≈ 0.77 × 10⁵ N/mm²**. ✓

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Characteristic Points on Mild Steel σ-ε Curve

| Point | Name | Typical Strain (ε) | Typical Stress (N/mm²) | Design Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| O | Origin | 0 | 0 | Reference point |
| A | Limit of Proportionality | 0.0012 | 240 | End of Hooke's law |
| B | Elastic Limit | 0.00125 | 250 | Max recoverable strain |
| C | Upper Yield Point | 0.00140 | 260 | Sudden yield onset |
| D | Lower Yield Point | 0.00160 | 250 | Stable yield (f_y design) |
| E | Ultimate Point | ≈ 0.16 | 410 (f_u) | Maximum stress |
| F | Fracture Point | ≈ 0.23 | ≈ 330 (eng.) | Failure (specimen necks) |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Thermal Elongation of a Steel Bar

**Step 1:** Free thermal strain due to temperature rise $\Delta T$ is:

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2:** Total free elongation of a bar of length L:

$$\Delta L_{free} = \varepsilon_{th} \cdot L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 3:** If the bar is **restrained** (e.g., fixed between two walls), a compressive thermal stress develops:

$$\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 4 (Numerical Example for steel, $\Delta T = 30°C$):**

$$\Delta L = (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times L \times 30 = 3.6 \times 10^{-4} \cdot L$$

For L = 10 m = 10000 mm:

$$\Delta L = 3.6 \times 10^{-4} \times 10000 = 3.6 \text{ mm}$$

The corresponding restrained stress is:

$$\sigma_{th} = (2.0 \times 10^5)(12 \times 10^{-6})(30) = 72 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A small temperature rise (30 °C) in a restrained steel bar generates **72 N/mm²** of compressive stress — nearly **30% of yield stress**. This is why **expansion joints** are mandatory in long steel structures (bridges, pipelines, railway tracks).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Property vs. Grade of Steel

| Property | Fe 250 (Mild Steel) | Fe 410 (HT Steel) | Fe 450 (HT Steel) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Yield stress f_y (N/mm²) | 250 | 410 | 450 |
| Ultimate stress f_u (N/mm²) | 410 | 410 (min) | 450 (min) |
| % Elongation (min) | 23 | 23 | 21 |
| Carbon % (max) | 0.23 | 0.20 | 0.20 |
| Weldability | Excellent | Good | Good |
| Cost (relative) | Lowest | Medium | Higher |
| Common Use | Small structures, secondary members | Main beams, columns | Heavy industrial, bridges |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Mild Steel Stress-Strain Curve Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Origin O: zero load] --> B[Region I: Elastic Region]
    B -->|"Slope = E = 2e5 N/mm²"| C[Point A: Limit of Proportionality]
    C --> D[Point B: Elastic Limit]
    D --> E[Region III: Yield Plateau]
    E --> F[Point C: Upper Yield Point]
    F --> G[Point D: Lower Yield Point fy]
    G --> H[Region IV: Strain Hardening]
    H --> I[Point E: Ultimate Stress fu]
    I --> J[Region V: Necking begins]
    J --> K[Point F: Fracture Point]
    K --> L[Failure: Elongation approx 23 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The five regions of the σ-ε curve are clearly demarcated. The slope of Region I equals Young's Modulus. The horizontal plateau (Region III) is the **design yield zone** used in IS 800:2007 limit state method.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Classification of Steel Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Properties of Structural Steel] --> P1[Physical Properties]
    P --> P2[Mechanical Properties]
    P --> P3[Chemical Properties]
    P --> P4[Technological Properties]

    P1 --> P1a[Density: 7850 kg per m3]
    P1 --> P1b[Young Modulus: 2.0e5 N per mm2]
    P1 --> P1c[Poisson Ratio: 0.30]
    P1 --> P1d[Thermal coeff: 12e-6 per C]

    P2 --> P2a[Strength]
    P2 --> P2b[Ductility]
    P2 --> P2c[Toughness]
    P2 --> P2d[Hardness]
    P2 --> P2e[Fatigue Resistance]
    P2 --> P2f[Creep Resistance]

    P3 --> P3a[Carbon max 0.23 percent]
    P3 --> P3b[Sulphur max 0.040 percent]
    P3 --> P3c[Phosphorus max 0.040 percent]
    P3 --> P3d[Manganese up to 1.5 percent]

    P4 --> P4a[Weldability]
    P4 --> P4b[Machinability]
    P4 --> P4c[Formability]
    P4 --> P4d[Corrosion Resistance]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks "List the properties of steel" for 3 marks, use the **4-category classification** (Physical, Mechanical, Chemical, Technological) and write 2-3 properties under each. Examiners reward structured answers.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Comparison — Brittle vs. Ductile Material Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Load Applied] --> L1{Type of Steel?}
    L1 -->|Low Carbon MS| D1[Ductile Behaviour]
    L1 -->|Cast Iron or Cold| B1[Brittle Behaviour]

    D1 --> D2[Large Plastic Deformation]
    D2 --> D3[Visible Warning: Yields First]
    D3 --> D4[Sudden Fracture at F]

    B1 --> B2[Negligible Plastic Strain]
    B2 --> B3[No Warning: Sudden Snap]
    B3 --> B4[Failure at Lower Load]

    D4 --> R1[Preferred for Seismic and Structural Design]
    B4 --> R2[Avoid in Primary Structural Members]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Limit State Design (LSD) Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steel Property Input] --> B[fy: Yield Stress from Grade]
    A --> C[fu: Ultimate Stress from Grade]
    A --> D[E: 2.0e5 N per mm2]
    A --> E[gamma_m0: 1.10]

    B --> F1[Design Strength: fd = fy divided by gamma_m0]
    C --> F2[Design Ultimate Strength: fud = fu divided by gamma_m1]
    D --> F3[Stiffness Calculation: delta = PL divided by AE]
    E --> F1
    E --> F2

    F1 --> G[Limit State of Strength: Member Capacity]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> H[Limit State of Serviceability: Deflection Check]

    G --> I[Safety Verified: Design OK]
    H --> I
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the following properties of steel with typical values:
**(i) Young's Modulus  (ii) Poisson's Ratio  (iii) Density** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Young's Modulus (E):** It is the ratio of longitudinal stress to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit. For structural steel, **E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 200 GPa**.

**(ii) Poisson's Ratio (ν):** It is the ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain under uniaxial loading. For steel, **ν = 0.30** (dimensionless).

**(iii) Density (ρ):** It is the mass per unit volume of the material. For structural steel, **ρ = 7850 kg/m³**, which gives unit weight **γ = 76.98 kN/m³**.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between "Brittleness" and "Ductility" of steel. Why is ductility important in design? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Brittleness | Ductility |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Failure with negligible plastic deformation | Ability to undergo large plastic deformation before fracture |
| Warning | **No visible warning** before failure | **Visible yielding** gives warning |
| Energy Absorption | Low (poor seismic performance) | High (excellent seismic performance) |
| Example | Cast iron, glass, chalk at low temp | Mild steel, aluminium, copper |
| % Elongation | < 5% | ≥ 23% (for Fe 410) |

**Importance of Ductility in Design (1 mark):**
- Ductility provides **visible warning** before failure, allowing evacuation and repair.
- It enables **plastic redistribution of moments** in indeterminate structures (e.g., continuous beams).
- It provides **energy absorption capacity** during earthquakes — the structure survives shaking by yielding, not by sudden collapse.
- It allows **plastic design** methods (as in portal frames) for economical design.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts (a) and (b).

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A.(a) [7 Marks] — Describe the salient features of the stress-strain curve for mild steel. Label all important points and regions. Explain why the yield stress (not ultimate stress) is used for design. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Diagram Description (3 marks)**

The stress-strain curve for mild steel under uniaxial tension shows **five distinct regions** (refer Section 4.1 mermaid diagram):

| Point | Designation | Approximate Coordinates (ε, σ) |
|---|---|---|
| O | Origin | (0, 0) |
| A | Limit of Proportionality | (0.0012, 240) |
| B | Elastic Limit | (0.0013, 250) |
| C | Upper Yield Point | (0.00140, 260) |
| D | Lower Yield Point | (0.00160, 250) |
| E | Ultimate Point | (0.16, 410) |
| F | Fracture Point | (0.23, 330) |

**Step 2: Salient Features (2 marks)**
- Long linear elastic region (high E).
- Pronounced **yield plateau** with a sudden drop from upper to lower yield point.
- Significant **strain hardening** between D and E.
- Large **necking region** between E and F.
- Total elongation ≥ 23% — confirms high ductility.

**Step 3: Why Yield Stress is Used for Design (2 marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although ultimate stress (f_u ≈ 410 N/mm²) is higher than yield stress (f_y = 250 N/mm²), design is based on f_y because:
>
> 1. **Excessive deformation at yield** makes the structure unserviceable (deflection, vibration, cracking of connected elements).
> 2. **Stability & buckling** of columns is critically affected once material yields.
> 3. **Repeated yielding** under cyclic loads leads to low-cycle fatigue failure.
> 4. The factor of safety is applied on the **yield side** to ensure elastic behaviour under service loads.

**Design Equation:**
$$f_d = \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{250}{1.10} = 227.27 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

---

#### Q.A.(b) [7 Marks] — A steel rod of length 2 m and cross-section 30 mm × 30 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 90 kN. Calculate: (i) Stress, (ii) Strain, (iii) Elongation, (iv) Shear modulus, given E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm² and ν = 0.3. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Length, L = 2 m = 2000 mm
- Cross-section: 30 mm × 30 mm → A = 900 mm²
- Tensile load, P = 90 kN = 90,000 N
- E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²
- ν = 0.30

**Step (i): Axial Stress (1.5 marks)**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{90000}{900} = 100 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step (ii): Strain (1.5 marks)**

Using Hooke's law:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{100}{2 \times 10^5} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step (iii): Elongation (2 marks)**

$$\Delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 2000 = 1.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step (iv): Shear Modulus (2 marks)**

Using the relation $E = 2G(1+\nu)$:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{2(1+0.30)} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{2.60}$$

$$\boxed{G = 0.7692 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 76.92 \text{ GPa}}$$

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Q.B.(a) [7 Marks] — List and explain any **five mechanical properties** of structural steel. State their significance in steel design. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Strength (1.5 marks)**
- Ability to resist applied loads without yielding or rupture.
- Quantified by **yield stress (f_y)** and **ultimate tensile stress (f_u)**.
- *Significance:* Higher strength → smaller cross-section → lighter structure.

**2. Ductility (1.5 marks)**
- Capacity to undergo large plastic deformation before fracture (≥ 23% elongation for Fe 410).
- *Significance:* Provides warning, enables plastic hinge formation, improves seismic performance.

**3. Toughness (1.5 marks)**
- Ability to absorb energy and resist fracture under impact/cyclic loading.
- *Significance:* Critical for bridges, machine parts, and structures in cold climates.

**4. Weldability (1.5 marks)**
- Ability to be welded without developing cracks or losing strength.
- *Significance:* Determines the carbon equivalent. IS 800:2007 restricts C ≤ 0.23% for welded structures.

**5. Fatigue Strength (1 mark)**
- Resistance to failure under repeated/reversed cyclic stresses.
- *Significance:* Bridge girders, crane girders, and offshore structures are fatigue-critical.

---

#### Q.B.(b) [7 Marks] — Explain the chemical composition of structural steel as per IS 2062. Why are Sulphur and Phosphorus restricted? What is the effect of Carbon content? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Chemical Composition Table (2 marks)**

| Element | Max % | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Carbon (C)** | **0.23** | Strength ↑, Ductility ↓ |
| Manganese (Mn) | 1.50 | Strength ↑, Removes O₂ & S |
| Silicon (Si) | 0.40 | Strength ↑, Deoxidizer |
| **Sulphur (S)** | **0.040** | Harmful — Hot shortness |
| **Phosphorus (P)** | **0.040** | Harmful — Cold shortness |
| Copper (Cu) | 0.30 | Corrosion resistance ↑ |

**Step 2: Effect of Carbon (2 marks)**

- ↑ Carbon → ↑ Strength & Hardness.
- ↑ Carbon → ↓ Ductility, Weldability, and Impact Toughness.
- A balance is needed: **optimum 0.15–0.23%** for structural applications.
- Beyond 0.25% C, welding becomes difficult and brittle fracture risk increases.

**Step 3: Why Sulphur is Restricted (1.5 marks)**
- Forms **iron sulphide (FeS)** which melts at low temperature (~988 °C).
- Causes **"Hot Shortness"** — the metal cracks during hot rolling or forging.
- Sulphur also makes the steel porous and unsound.

**Step 4: Why Phosphorus is Restricted (1.5 marks)**
- Forms a hard, brittle solid solution with iron.
- Causes **"Cold Shortness"** — steel becomes brittle at low/normal temperatures.
- Reduces impact toughness dramatically, especially in cold climates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the values** of S and P (≤ 0.040%) — a 1-mark deduction. Use the exact decimal.
> 2. **Confusing "Hot Shortness" with "Cold Shortness"** — examiners love this trick question. Remember: **S** causes **H**ot shortness (both start with "S" and "H"). **P** causes **C**old shortness.
> 3. **Stating Carbon as >0.25%** — this is wrong for *structural* steel (it applies to tool steel).
> 4. **Skipping units** in numerical answers (e.g., E in N/mm², not GPa, unless specified) — 0.5 mark deduction.
> 5. **Not drawing the σ-ε curve** in Q.A.(a) — at least 2 marks lost. Even a neat hand-drawn sketch is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Box — Memorize Before Exam Day**

- [x] **Density of steel = 7850 kg/m³** (NOT 7850 kN/m³ — that's unit weight confusion).
- [x] **Young's Modulus E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** — universal for all grades of structural steel (does NOT vary with grade).
- [x] **Shear Modulus G = 0.77 × 10⁵ N/mm²** — derived from E and ν.
- [x] **Poisson's Ratio ν = 0.30** — for all steels.
- [x] **Thermal coefficient α = 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**.
- [x] **Yield stress f_y = 250 (MS), 410, 450 N/mm²** for the three common grades.
- [x] **Design strength f_d = f_y / 1.10** (γ_m0 for shop-fabricated, = 1.25 for site-fabricated).
- [x] **Carbon % ≤ 0.23** for structural steel (weldability reason).
- [x] **S ≤ 0.040%, P ≤ 0.040%** — both are harmful impurities.
- [x] **Sulphur = Hot Shortness; Phosphorus = Cold Shortness.**
- [x] **% Elongation ≥ 23% for Fe 410** — measures ductility.
- [x] **Steel design uses f_y (yield), not f_u (ultimate)** — serviceability reason.
- [x] **σ-ε curve has 5 regions**: Elastic, Yield Plateau, Strain Hardening, Necking, Fracture.
- [x] **Toughness = Strength × Ductility** (qualitative). Brittle = high strength + low ductility.
- [x] **Ductility is critical for seismic design** — earthquake-resistant structures must yield, not snap.
- [x] **Three elastic constants relation:** $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ — derivable, must know.
- [x] **IS 800:2007** is the design code; **IS 2062:2011** is the material specification.
- [x] **Restrained thermal stress** $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$ — produces large stresses even for small $\Delta T$.
- [x] **Creep becomes significant above 450 °C** — fireproofing is essential.
- [x] **Fatigue**: failure under repeated loading even when peak stress < f_y.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Mantra for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**
> *"Know the four categories of properties, the five regions of the σ-ε curve, the three elastic constants, and the numerical values of the design constants. You will score 90%+ in this topic."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Steel Sections. Introduction to Design Loads and Load Combinations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET602 - DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Structural Steel Sections. Introduction to Design Loads and Load Combinations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Structural Steel — The Engineering Definition

**Structural Steel** is a category of carbon steel used as a construction material for producing structural shapes (sections) such as I-beams, channels, angles, and hollow sections, conforming to Indian Standard **IS 2062:2011** (for hot-rolled medium and high tensile structural steel). It is the primary load-bearing material in skeletal frameworks of industrial sheds, high-rise buildings, bridges, and towers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The module introduces the *vocabulary* of steel design — the shapes (sections) engineers roll, the loads nature imposes, and the math of combining those loads safely. Mastery of this module is foundational for every later design problem in Connections, Tension Members, Compression Members, and Beams.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Steel Sections as the "Skeleton of a Skyscraper"

Imagine a human body. The **bones** give shape, support weight, and resist forces. Bones are not solid cylinders — they are shaped (hollow tubes, I-beams) to give **maximum strength with minimum material**. Structural steel sections follow the **exact same logic**:

- An **I-section** (W-shape) has most of its material in the **flanges** (top and bottom), which resist bending exactly like the flanges of a beam.
- A **hollow rectangular section (RHS)** is like a hollow bone — high torsional rigidity with low weight.
- An **angle section** acts like a joint or bracing — versatile and economical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Steel sections are *pre-engineered* by rolling mills. The designer's job is **not to invent shapes** but to **select the most efficient section** from standard tables (SP 6-1, IS 808) for the given demand.

### 1.3 Design Loads — Formal Definition

A **Design Load** is the *factored* (amplified) load, obtained by multiplying the *characteristic* (nominal/service) load by a **partial safety factor for load (γ_f)**. It represents the worst plausible magnitude the structure might experience during its design life, accounting for uncertainties in magnitude, position, and distribution.

### 1.4 Load Combinations — The "What If?" Scenarios

A **Load Combination** is a defined set of different load types (Dead, Live, Wind, Seismic, etc.) acting simultaneously, each multiplied by its respective partial safety factor, used to evaluate the *critical* design demand on a structural member. The structure must remain safe under the **most unfavorable** of these combinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a tree. It must withstand (a) its own weight (DL), (b) monkeys sitting on branches (LL), (c) strong winds (WL), and (d) earthquakes (EL) — sometimes all at once, sometimes alone. A *load combination* is the question: *"What is the worst combination of these forces the tree must survive?"*

---

### 1.5 Why Steel? — The Physical Constants

- **Density of structural steel:** **ρ = 7850 kg/m³** (≈ 78.5 kN/m³)
- **Young's Modulus of Elasticity:** **E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** (≈ 200 GPa)
- **Poisson's ratio:** **ν = 0.30**
- **Shear Modulus:** **G = E / [2(1+ν)] ≈ 76.92 GPa**
- **Coefficient of thermal expansion:** **α = 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Structural Steel Sections

Structural steel sections are classified by **manufacturing process** and **geometric profile**.

#### A. By Manufacturing Process
1. **Hot-Rolled Sections** — formed by passing heated steel billets through rollers at ~1100 °C. Standard: **IS 808:1989**. Examples: I-beams (ISMB), Channels (ISMC), Angles (ISA), T-bars.
2. **Cold-Formed Sections** — formed by bending thin steel sheets (typically 0.4 mm to 6.4 mm) at room temperature. Standard: **IS 801:1975**. Examples: Lipped channels, Zed sections, Sigma sections.
3. **Built-Up Sections** — fabricated by welding/bolting plates together. Examples: Plate girders, box girders, laced/battened columns.

#### B. By Geometric Profile (Hot-Rolled)

| Section Type | Designation | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| **I-Section (Beam)** | ISMB / ISJB / ISLB / ISWB | Flexural members (beams, girders) |
| **Channel Section** | ISMC / ISLC | Purlins, secondary beams, built-up columns |
| **Equal Angle** | ISA (e.g., ISA 50×50×6) | Bracing, truss members, gusset plates |
| **Unequal Angle** | ISA 100×75×10 | Connection cleats, eccentric loading |
| **T-Section** | IST | Built-up members, stiffeners |
| **Hollow Rectangular (RHS)** | RHS | Columns, trusses, architectural members |
| **Hollow Circular (CHS)** | CHS / Pipe | Columns, fluid transport, light trusses |

### 2.2 Design Philosophy — Limit State Method (IS 800:2007)

KTU 2024 Scheme mandates the **Limit State Method (LSM)** as per **IS 800:2007**. Two key limit states:

1. **Limit State of Strength** — collapse, rupture, buckling, fatigue failure.
2. **Limit State of Serviceability** — deflection, vibration, cracking, corrosion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In LSM, the **design strength** is calculated by dividing the *characteristic strength* by the partial safety factor for material, **γ_m**. The design must satisfy:
> **Design Action (Factored Load) ≤ Design Strength (Reduced Capacity)**

### 2.3 Partial Safety Factors for Materials (γ_m) — IS 800:2007, Table 5

| Limit State | γ_m | Application |
|---|---|---|
| γ_m0 | 1.10 | Yielding of gross section |
| γ_m1 | 1.25 | Ultimate stress (rupture) |
| γ_m2 | 1.25 | Bolts |
| γ_mb | 1.25 | Bearing of bolts |
| γ_mw | 1.50 | Welds |
| γ_mf | 1.25 | Factory/site welds in tension |

### 2.4 Partial Safety Factors for Loads (γ_f) — IS 800:2007, Table 4

| Load Combination | DL | LL | WL/EL |
|---|---|---|---|
| DL + LL + CL | 1.5 | 1.5 | — |
| DL + LL + CL + WL/EL | 1.2 | 1.2 | 1.2 |
| DL + WL/EL | 1.5 | — | 1.5 |
| DL + ER | 1.2 | 1.2 | — |
| DL + LL + ER | 1.2 | 1.0 | — |

(ER = Erection Load; CL = Collateral Load)

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The Ultimate KTU Cheat Sheet** for Module 1 — memorize this!

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Factored Load (F) | $F = \gamma_f \times Q_k$ | Q_k = characteristic load |
| Design Strength | $R_d = R_k / \gamma_m$ | R_k = characteristic strength |
| Density of Steel | $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Universal constant |
| Self-weight (kN/m) | $w = A \times \rho \times g / 10^6$ | A in mm², g = 9.81 m/s² |
| Section Modulus (Z) | $Z = I / y_{max}$ | y in mm, I in mm⁴ |
| Slenderness Ratio (λ) | $\lambda = L_{eff} / r$ | $r = \sqrt{I/A}$ |
| Radius of Gyration (r) | $r = \sqrt{(I / A)}$ | mm |
| Cross-sectional Area (A) | $A = \sum b_i t_i$ | Sum of rectangles for built-up |
| Plastic Modulus (Z_p) | $Z_p = 2 \times A_{comp} \times \bar{y}$ | For doubly symmetric sections: $Z_p \approx 1.14 Z_e$ |
| Shape Factor | $S = Z_p / Z_e$ | For I-section ≈ 1.14 |

> **Critical Note on table syntax:** Where absolute value or "given" symbol appears, e.g., $\lambda = L_{eff} \vert r$, we use the displayed LaTeX fraction form to avoid table-breaking pipe characters.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

This foundational module directly feeds into:
- **Connection design** (Module 2) — γ_mb and γ_mw are used for bolt/weld strength.
- **Tension member design** (Module 3) — γ_m0 governs yielding, γ_m1 governs rupture.
- **Column buckling** (Module 4) — γ_m0 governs column strength (using Euler/IS curves).
- **Beam design** (Module 5) — γ_m0 governs moment capacity, γ_m1 governs shear.

Without mastering partial safety factors here, every subsequent module is unworkable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Self-Weight of a Standard ISMB 400 Section

**Given:**
- Section: ISMB 400 (per IS 808)
- Depth, $h = 400$ mm
- Flange width, $b_f = 140$ mm
- Flange thickness, $t_f = 16.0$ mm
- Web thickness, $t_w = 8.9$ mm
- Cross-sectional area, $A = 7,886$ mm² (from steel table)

**Find:** Self-weight per unit length in kN/m.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation.**

$$
w = \rho \times A \times g
$$

where ρ is density, A is area, g is acceleration due to gravity.

**Step 2 — Convert units to SI (mass × acceleration).**

$$
w = 7850 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \times \left(\frac{7,886}{10^6}\right) \, \text{m}^2 \times 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2
$$

We convert mm² to m² by dividing by $10^6$ (since $1 \text{ m}^2 = 10^6 \text{ mm}^2$).

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 7850 \times 7886 \times 9.81
$$

Let us compute this in stages:

$$
7850 \times 7886 = 61,905,100
$$

$$
61,905,100 \times 9.81 = 607,288,031
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the 10⁶ divisor.**

$$
w = \frac{607,288,031}{10^6} = 607.29 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert N/m to kN/m.**

$$
w = \frac{607.29}{1000} = 0.6073 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Result:** Self-weight of ISMB 400 ≈ **0.607 kN/m** (matches SP 6-1 table: 0.607 kN/m ✓).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Application of Load Combination

**Problem:** An industrial roof truss has the following characteristic loads at a particular joint:
- Dead Load (DL) = 30 kN
- Live Load (LL) = 50 kN
- Wind Load (WL) = 25 kN (uplift, acting opposite to gravity)

**Find:** Critical factored load for (a) downward design, (b) uplift design.

#### (a) Downward Design — Combination: 1.2(DL + LL + WL)

$$
F_d = 1.2 \times (30 + 50 + 25) = 1.2 \times 105 = 126 \text{ kN}
$$

#### (b) Alternative Combination: 1.5(DL + LL)

$$
F_d = 1.5 \times (30 + 50) = 1.5 \times 80 = 120 \text{ kN}
$$

#### (c) Uplift Design — Combination: 0.9 DL + 1.5 WL (uplift scenario)

Treating WL as uplift, the net characteristic vertical action = DL − WL = 30 − 25 = 5 kN (downward), but for uplift evaluation we use:

$$
F_u = 0.9 \times 30 - 1.5 \times 25 = 27 - 37.5 = -10.5 \text{ kN}
$$

Negative sign indicates **uplift** of 10.5 kN. The member must be checked as a tension member under this uplift.

> [!NOTE]
> **The negative result is a feature, not a bug** — it tells the designer that the joint *lifts* and the connection must be designed for tension reversal.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Standard Load Combinations (IS 800:2007, Cl. 3.5.1)

The following **seven** combinations govern the Limit State of Strength (most common for KTU problems):

| # | Load Combination | Typical Scenario |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1.5(DL + LL + CL)$ | Gravity-dominated, no wind/seismic |
| 2 | $1.2(DL + LL + CL \pm WL)$ | Gravity + Wind |
| 3 | $1.2(DL + LL + CL \pm EL)$ | Gravity + Earthquake |
| 4 | $1.5(DL \pm WL)$ | Dead + Wind (no Live Load) |
| 5 | $1.5(DL \pm EL)$ | Dead + Earthquake (no Live Load) |
| 6 | $0.9 DL \pm 1.5 WL$ | Overturning / Uplift check |
| 7 | $0.9 DL \pm 1.5 EL$ | Seismic Uplift check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** Always evaluate **all applicable combinations** and design for the **envelope (worst-case)**. KTU examiners will *always* test whether the student has considered multiple combinations.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Plastic Section Modulus of a Rectangular Section (Foundation Concept)

**Given:** Rectangular section, width $b$, depth $d$. Material: perfectly elastic-plastic steel.

**Step 1 — The plastic neutral axis (PNA) divides the section into two equal areas.**

For a rectangle, by symmetry, PNA lies at mid-depth: $y = d/2$.

**Step 2 — Compute area above PNA.**

$$
A_{top} = b \times \frac{d}{2} = \frac{bd}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Centroid of the top half (from neutral axis).**

$$
\bar{y}_{top} = \frac{d/2}{2} = \frac{d}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute plastic modulus** as sum of (Area × Distance to PNA) for both halves.

$$
Z_p = 2 \times A_{top} \times \bar{y}_{top} = 2 \times \frac{bd}{2} \times \frac{d}{4} = \frac{bd^2}{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with elastic section modulus.**

$$
Z_e = \frac{bd^2}{6}
$$

**Step 6 — Shape factor.**

$$
S = \frac{Z_p}{Z_e} = \frac{bd^2/4}{bd^2/6} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.50
$$

**Insight:** A rectangle has shape factor 1.50 (highest); an I-section has ≈ 1.14 (lowest). Lower shape factor ⇒ more efficient in elastic design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Structural Steel Sections (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STRUCTURAL STEEL SECTIONS] --> B[Hot Rolled]
    A --> C[Cold Formed]
    A --> D[Built Up]

    B --> B1[ISMB I Beam]
    B --> B2[ISMC Channel]
    B --> B3[ISA Angle]
    B --> B4[IST Tee]
    B --> B5[RHS / CHS Hollow]

    C --> C1[Lipped Channel]
    C --> C2[Zed Section]
    C --> C3[Sigma Section]

    D --> D1[Plate Girder]
    D --> D2[Box Girder]
    D --> D3[Laced Column]
    D --> D4[Battened Column]

    B1 --> E1[Beams, Girders]
    B2 --> E2[Purlins, Built Up Columns]
    B3 --> E3[Truss Members, Bracing]
    B5 --> E4[Columns, Truss Chords]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Loads Acting on a Steel Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    L[DESIGN LOADS ON STEEL STRUCTURE] --> L1[Permanent Loads]
    L --> L2[Variable Loads]
    L --> L3[Exceptional Loads]

    L1 --> L1a[Dead Load DL]
    L1 --> L1b[Self Weight of Steel]
    L1 --> L1c[Collateral Load CL]

    L2 --> L2a[Live Load LL Imposed]
    L2 --> L2b[Wind Load WL]
    L2 --> L2c[Snow Load SL]
    L2 --> L2d[Seismic Load EL]

    L3 --> L3a[Blast Load]
    L3 --> L3b[Fire Load]
    L3 --> L3c[Impact Load]

    L1a --> F[Factored by gamma_f per IS 875]
    L2a --> F
    L2b --> F
    L2c --> F
    L2d --> F

    F --> COMBO[Load Combinations IS 800 Cl 3.5]
    COMBO --> DESIGN[Limit State Design Verification]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: From Section Selection to Design Verification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Identify Loads from IS 875] --> S2[Step 2 Apply gamma_f for each load]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Form Load Combinations]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Compute Factored Design Action]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Select Trial Section from IS 808]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Compute Design Strength using gamma_m]
    S6 --> S7{Design Action LESS THAN Design Strength}
    S7 -->|YES| S8[Section is SAFE Adopt]
    S7 -->|NO| S9[Revise Section Go to Step 5]
    S8 --> S10[Check Serviceability Deflection Vibration]
    S10 --> S11[Final Approved Section]
```

```mermaid
    TW[Web tw] --> H

```
