## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the Limit State of Serviceability. List any two serviceability limit states as per IS 800:2007.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Limit State of Serviceability** is a state beyond which a structure becomes unfit for its intended use due to excessive deflection, vibration, cracking, corrosion, fire, or other discomforts to occupants — *without collapse*.

Two examples:
1. **Deflection limit** — maximum vertical deflection of a beam should not exceed $L/325$ (for beams supporting brittle cladding) or $L/360$ (general), as per IS 800:2007 Table 6.
2. **Vibration limit** — fundamental natural frequency of floors should exceed **5 Hz** for machine-induced vibrations to avoid discomfort.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse "Limit State of Strength" with "Limit State of Serviceability." Strength is about *collapse*; Serviceability is about *discomfort and use*. You lose 1 mark for mixing them up.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**List the seven standard load combinations for the Limit State of Strength as per IS 800:2007. Why is a partial safety factor greater than 1.0 applied to loads?**

**Model Answer:**

The seven combinations are:

1. $1.5(DL + LL + CL)$
2. $1.2(DL + LL + CL \pm WL)$
3. $1.2(DL + LL + CL \pm EL)$
4. $1.5(DL \pm WL)$
5. $1.5(DL \pm EL)$
6. $0.9 DL \pm 1.5 WL$
7. $0.9 DL \pm 1.5 EL$

**Why γ_f > 1.0?** The partial safety factor for loads is applied to account for:
- **Uncertainty in magnitude** — actual loads may exceed the design (characteristic) value.
- **Uncertainty in distribution** — loads may not be uniformly distributed as assumed.
- **Unforeseen load combinations** — multiple loads may act simultaneously in the worst possible manner.
- **Construction tolerances** — actual self-weights may vary from design.

A factor > 1 ensures the structure is designed for a load **higher than expected**, providing a margin of safety.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the different types of structural steel sections used in construction. Differentiate between hot-rolled and cold-formed sections. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial building roof truss is subjected to the following characteristic loads at a critical joint: DL = 25 kN, LL = 40 kN, WL = 30 kN (uplift), and EL = 20 kN. Compute the **critical factored load** using all applicable load combinations of IS 800:2007. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types of Steel Sections:**

Structural steel sections are classified as:

1. **Hot-Rolled Sections** — produced by passing heated steel billets (~1100 °C) through rollers. They are standardized as per **IS 808:1989** and include:
   - Indian Standard Medium Weight Beam (**ISMB**)
   - Indian Standard Junior Beam (**ISJB**)
   - Indian Standard Light Beam (**ISLB**)
   - Indian Standard Wide Flange Beam (**ISWB**)
   - Indian Standard Medium Channel (**ISMC**)
   - Indian Standard Equal Angles (**ISA**)
   - Indian Standard Tee bars (**IST**)
   - Hollow Sections (**RHS** and **CHS**)

2. **Cold-Formed Sections** — produced by press-braking or roll-forming thin steel sheets (0.4–6.4 mm) at room temperature. Standard: **IS 801:1975**. Include lipped channels, zed sections, and sigma sections.

3. **Built-Up Sections** — fabricated by welding or bolting plates. Examples: plate girders, box girders, laced/battened columns.

**Differences:**

| Property | Hot-Rolled | Cold-Formed |
|---|---|---|
| Material thickness | > 6 mm | 0.4 to 6.4 mm |
| Manufacturing temp | ~1100 °C | Room temperature |
| Residual stresses | Low | High (cold work effect) |
| Section depth | Large (up to 1000 mm) | Limited (≤ 250 mm) |
| Buckling behavior | Compact / non-compact / slender | Slender elements prone to local buckling |
| Design standard | IS 800:2007 | IS 801:1975 |
| Typical use | Heavy industrial, bridges | Light purlins, storage racks |

**(b) Critical Factored Load Computation:**

Given: DL = 25 kN, LL = 40 kN, WL = 30 kN (uplift), EL = 20 kN.

**Combination 1:** $1.5(DL + LL + CL)$
$$
F_1 = 1.5 \times (25 + 40) = 1.5 \times 65 = 97.5 \text{ kN (downward)}
$$

**Combination 2:** $1.2(DL + LL + WL)$ [WL is uplift, so we consider both signs]

Downward:
$$
F_{2a} = 1.2 \times (25 + 40 + 30) = 1.2 \times 95 = 114 \text{ kN}
$$
Uplift (treating WL as negative):
$$
F_{2b} = 1.2 \times (25 + 40 - 30) = 1.2 \times 35 = 42 \text{ kN (downward)}
$$

**Combination 3:** $1.2(DL + LL + EL)$ [EL can be ±]
$$
F_{3a} = 1.2 \times (25 + 40 + 20) = 1.2 \times 85 = 102 \text{ kN}
$$
$$
F_{3b} = 1.2 \times (25 + 40 - 20) = 1.2 \times 45 = 54 \text{ kN}
$$

**Combination 4:** $1.5(DL + WL)$
Downward (WL as positive):
$$
F_4 = 1.5 \times (25 + 30) = 82.5 \text{ kN}
$$
Uplift (WL as negative):
$$
F_{4u} = 1.5 \times (25 - 30) = -7.5 \text{ kN (uplift of 7.5 kN)}
$$

**Combination 5:** $1.5(DL + EL)$
$$
F_5 = 1.5 \times (25 + 20) = 67.5 \text{ kN}
$$
$$
F_{5u} = 1.5 \times (25 - 20) = 7.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**Combination 6:** $0.9 DL + 1.5 WL$ (uplift check)
$$
F_6 = 0.9 \times 25 - 1.5 \times 30 = 22.5 - 45 = -22.5 \text{ kN (uplift)}
$$

**Combination 7:** $0.9 DL + 1.5 EL$
$$
F_{7a} = 0.9 \times 25 - 1.5 \times 20 = 22.5 - 30 = -7.5 \text{ kN (uplift)}
$$

**Critical Cases:**
- **Maximum downward force:** Combination 2(a) → **114 kN**
- **Maximum uplift force:** Combination 6 → **22.5 kN (uplift)**

The joint must be designed for **114 kN compression** and **22.5 kN tension (uplift reversal)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes (3 to 5 marks lost):**
> 1. Forgetting to consider both signs (±) for WL and EL — losing 2 marks.
> 2. Not checking the **uplift** combination (Comb. 6, 7) — losing 1 mark.
> 3. Mixing up IS 875 partial safety factors with IS 800 — these are different tables.
> 4. Not writing the **units (kN)** with the final answer.

---

#### **QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the Limit State Method of design. What are the partial safety factors for materials (γ_m) used in IS 800:2007? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported steel beam of span 6 m carries a uniformly distributed imposed load of 20 kN/m and a self-weight of 0.6 kN/m. Compute the design bending moment and design shear force using appropriate load combinations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Limit State Method of Design:**

The **Limit State Method (LSM)** is a probabilistic design philosophy codified in **IS 800:2007** (replacing the older Working Stress Method). A *limit state* is a condition beyond which the structure ceases to fulfill its intended function. LSM checks two primary limit states:

1. **Limit State of Strength:** Ensures the structure does not collapse under the worst expected loads. Includes yielding, buckling, rupture, fatigue.
2. **Limit State of Serviceability:** Ensures the structure remains functional and comfortable under normal loads. Includes deflection, vibration, cracking, corrosion.

**Design Inequality:**
$$
\text{Design Action} \leq \text{Design Strength}
$$
$$
\gamma_f \times Q_k \leq \frac{R_k}{\gamma_m}
$$

**Partial Safety Factors for Materials (IS 800:2007, Table 5):**

| Factor | Value | Application |
|---|---|---|
| γ_m0 | 1.10 | Yielding of gross section |
| γ_m1 | 1.25 | Rupture (ultimate stress) |
| γ_m2 | 1.25 | Bolts (ultimate) |
| γ_mb | 1.25 | Bearing of bolts |
| γ_mw | 1.50 | Welds |
| γ_mf | 1.25 | Shop welds in tension |

**(b) Design Bending Moment and Shear Force:**

Given:
- Span, $L = 6$ m
- Imposed (Live) Load, $LL = 20$ kN/m
- Self-weight (Dead Load), $DL = 0.6$ kN/m

**Step 1 — Apply the governing load combination: $1.5(DL + LL)$**

$$
w_u = 1.5 \times (0.6 + 20) = 1.5 \times 20.6 = 30.9 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Bending Moment (simply supported, UDL):**

$$
M_u = \frac{w_u L^2}{8}
$$

$$
M_u = \frac{30.9 \times 6^2}{8} = \frac{30.9 \times 36}{8} = \frac{1112.4}{8} = 139.05 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Shear Force (simply supported, UDL):**

$$
V_u = \frac{w_u L}{2}
$$

$$
V_u = \frac{30.9 \times 6}{2} = \frac{185.4}{2} = 92.7 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final Design Values:**
- **Design Bending Moment, M_u = 139.05 kN·m**
- **Design Shear Force, V_u = 92.7 kN**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):**
> 1. Using $1.2(DL + LL)$ instead of $1.5(DL + LL)$ for gravity-only loading — wrong combination.
> 2. Confusing *factored* load with *characteristic* load in the bending moment formula.
> 3. Forgetting to convert span to meters when SI units are used.
> 4. Not specifying units in the final answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This!**

- ☐ **Steel sections** are standardized as per **IS 808:1989** (hot-rolled) and **IS 801:1975** (cold-formed).
- ☐ **Density of structural steel** = **7850 kg/m³** = **77 kN/m³** (use 78.5 for accuracy).
- ☐ **Young's Modulus E** = **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²**.
- ☐ Design method = **Limit State Method (LSM)** as per **IS 800:2007**.
- ☐ **Factored Load** = $\gamma_f \times Q_k$ (load magnified for uncertainty).
- ☐ **Design Strength** = $R_k / \gamma_m$ (material strength reduced for safety).
- ☐ Memorize all **7 standard load combinations** (IS 800 Cl. 3.5.1).
- ☐ Always evaluate **multiple combinations** and take the **worst envelope**.
- ☐ **Uplift cases** (Comb. 6, 7) must be checked for members that can experience tension reversal.
- ☐ **γ_m0 = 1.10** for yielding; **γ_m1 = 1.25** for ultimate; **γ_mw = 1.50** for welds.
- ☐ **Shape factor** for I-section ≈ 1.14; for rectangle = 1.50.
- ☐ For simply supported UDL beam: $M = wL^2/8$ and $V = wL/2$ — with $w$ being the **factored** value.
- ☐ **Serviceability limit** for beam deflection ≤ **L/325** (brittle cladding) or **L/360** (general).
- ☐ Always carry SP 6-1 steel table data into the exam hall — IS 808 values for ISMB/ISMC/ISA are not provided in question papers.

# 🏗️ MODULE 1 — LIMIT STATE DESIGN, FASTENERS & CONNECTIONS

## 1.1 Limit State Design — Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limit State Design (LSD)** is a probabilistic structural design philosophy codified in **IS 800:2007** that ensures a structure remains fit for its intended purpose (safe, serviceable, durable) under all expected loads during its design life, with an acceptable (very low) probability of failure.

A **Limit State** is a condition of a structure at which it ceases to perform its intended function satisfactorily, i.e., it becomes unsafe (reaches an ultimate limit) or becomes unfit for use (reaches a serviceability limit).

### Classification of Limit States (IS 800:2007, Cl. 3.2)

| Limit State | Type | Concern | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Limit State of Strength** | Ultimate | Collapse, fracture, instability, fatigue | Must NOT be exceeded |
| **Limit State of Serviceability** | Service | Deflection, vibration, corrosion, cracking | Must NOT be exceeded (under SLS loads) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Philosophy (Limit State Format):**
>
> $$\text{Design Action Effect} \; (R_d) \;\geq\; \text{Design Load Effect} \; (Q_d)$$
>
> where $Q_d = \gamma_f \cdot Q_k$ and $R_d = R_k / \gamma_m$

**Where:**
- $\gamma_f$ = **partial safety factor for loads** (1.5 for DL, 1.5 for LL, 1.2 for DL+LL combo, 1.2 for WL/EL, 0.8 for wind uplift)
- $\gamma_m$ = **partial safety factor for material** (1.10 for steel, 1.25 for bolts, 1.50 for welds)
- $f_y$ = **yield stress of steel** (250 MPa for Fe 410, 350 MPa for Fe 490)
- $f_u$ = **ultimate tensile stress** (410 MPa for Fe 410)

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Safety Rope & Cliff Edge"

> Imagine you are walking along a narrow mountain ridge. The **cliff edge** represents the **limit state**. You are the **structure**, the wind pushing you is the **load**, and the friction between your shoes and the rock is the **strength**. Limit State Design says: "Wear shoes with enough friction (high $\gamma_m$-adjusted strength) AND stay far enough from the cliff (account for amplified loads with $\gamma_f$)." The designer calculates the worst credible push ($\gamma_f \times$ load) and the minimum credible grip (strength / $\gamma_m$), and demands the grip is bigger.

### Two-Tier Probability Concept

- **Limit State of Strength** — uses factored loads because failure = collapse, must be extremely rare.
- **Limit State of Serviceability** — uses unfactored (or partially factored) loads because excessive deflection is uncomfortable, not fatal.

### Partial Safety Factor for Materials (IS 800:2007, Table 5)

| Material / Component | $\gamma_m$ |
|---|---|
| Steel (yielding) | **1.10** |
| Steel (ultimate) | **1.25** |
| Bolts (bearing) | **1.25** |
| Bolts (shear/tension) | **1.25** |
| Welds | **1.50** (shop), **1.50** (field) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2.1 Types of Fasteners — Bolts and Welds

> [!NOTE]
> A **fastener** is a mechanical device used to join two or more structural members. The two principal categories per IS 800:2007 are **Bolts** and **Welds**. (Rivets, although historical, are now discouraged; they are replaced by HSFG bolts.)

## 2.1.1 Classification of Bolts (IS 800:2007, Cl. 3.6 & 10.1)

### A. By Manufacturing Accuracy
| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Black Bolts (C-Grade / Grade 4.6)** | Finished from rolled steel; loose fit; cheap |
| **Turned/Fitted Bolts (Grade 5.6, 6.6)** | Machined shank; close fit; expensive |
| **High-Strength Friction-Grip (HSFG) Bolts — Grade 8.8 / 9.8 / 10.9** | Pre-tensioned; transfer load by friction |

### B. By Load Transfer Mechanism

```
BOLTED CONNECTIONS
├── Bearing Type (Shear Connections)
│   ├── Using Black Bolts (4.6)
│   └── Using Fitted Bolts (5.6, 6.6)
│
└── Friction Type (Slip-Resistant)
    └── Using HSFG Bolts (8.8 / 10.9)
```

### C. By Force Pattern
- **Bolts in Shear** (load ⟂ bolt axis)
- **Bolts in Tension** (load ∥ bolt axis)
- **Bolts in Combined Shear + Tension** (eccentric bracket-type)

### D. By Hole Type
| Class | Hole Description | Clearance |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | Punched/Reamed | 1.5 to 2.0 mm |
| **Oversized** | Larger than standard | up to 3 mm extra |
| **Short Slotted** | Slot length ≤ 1.33d | — |
| **Long Slotted** | Slot length ≤ 2.5d | — |

## 2.1.2 Classification of Welds (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.4 & 11)

### A. By Position/Geometry
| Weld Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Fillet Weld** | Triangular cross-section; most common; transfers shear |
| **Butt Weld** | Flush; full penetration; transfers tension/compression |
| **Plug Weld / Slot Weld** | Fills a hole/slot; used for lap joints |
| **Slot Weld** | Slot filled with weld metal |

### B. By Weld Location
- **Shop weld** — made in factory (quality controlled, higher strength)
- **Site/Field weld** — made on site (weaker, $\gamma_m$ may be higher)

### C. Fillet Weld Geometry
- **Throat thickness** $t_t = 0.707 \times s$ (where $s$ = leg size)
- **Effective throat** $t_e = K \times s$ (K depends on fusion face angle)
- For 90° fusion face: $K = 0.707$

## 2.2 Simple Bolted and Welded Connections — Types

### 2.2.1 Lap Joint (Single Shear)
Two plates overlap; bolt passes through both; load causes **shear in one plane**.

```
  PLATE A  ─────►│
                  │  ← bolt
  PLATE B ◄──────│
```
Each bolt in **single shear**; the connection has eccentricity.

### 2.2.2 Butt Joint (Double Shear)
Two plates in the same plane, joined by cover plates; bolt passes through 3 plates; load causes **shear in two planes**.

```
  PLATE A ─────►│
  COVER ◄──────│──────► COVER
  PLATE B ◄────│
```
Each bolt in **double shear**; stronger than lap.

### 2.2.3 Welded Lap Joint
Two plates overlapped and welded along edges; uses **fillet welds** along the seam.

### 2.2.4 Welded Butt Joint
Two plates in same plane, edge-prepared, full-penetration **butt weld** along the joint.

## 2.3 Relative Advantages and Disadvantages

> [!NOTE]
> The choice between bolted and welded connection is fundamental. Each has structural, economic, and constructional implications.

### Comparison Table — Bolts vs Welds

| Parameter | Bolted Connection | Welded Connection |
|---|---|---|
| **Material wastage** | Negligible (small holes) | Some (weld metal) |
| **Skilled labour** | Less skilled needed | Highly skilled welder required |
| **Inspection** | Easy, visual & torque test | Needs X-ray/ultrasonic |
| **Noise on site** | Silent | Generates fumes/arc/spatter |
| **Reversibility** | Dismantle easily | Permanent — cannot undo |
| **Fatigue strength** | Better under cyclic load | Weld defects reduce fatigue life |
| **Tight sites** | Bolts work in confined spaces | Welding difficult in tight spaces |
| **Galvanized/Corroded steel** | Suitable | Welding galvanized steel produces toxic fumes |
| **Speed in field** | Fast | Slower |
| **Stress concentration** | At holes (lower) | At weld toe (higher) |
| **Initial cost** | Bolts cost more | Labour cost more |
| **Reliability** | Predictable | Depends on welder skill |
| **Aesthetic** | Visible bolts | Smooth appearance |
| **Vibration** | Bolts may loosen (use locknuts) | Welds do not loosen |

### Comparison — Black Bolts vs HSFG Bolts

| Feature | Black Bolts (4.6) | HSFG Bolts (8.8/10.9) |
|---|---|---|
| Working principle | Shear/Bearing | Friction (clamping) |
| Pretension | None | Pre-tensioned to 70% of $f_u$ |
| Slip allowed? | Yes (in service) | **No slip** under service load |
| Use | Trusses, secondary members | Bridges, dynamic/fatigue loads |
| Strength class | $f_{ub} = 400$ MPa | $f_{ub} = 800/1000$ MPa |
| Tightening | Spanner | Calibrated torque wrench |
| Cost | Cheap | Expensive |

## 2.4 Modes of Failure of a Bolted Connection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **bearing-type** connection subjected to **concentric tensile load**, the possible failure modes (per IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.3) are:

### 1. Shear Failure of the Bolt (Cl. 10.3.3)
The bolt shears across its shank at the interface between connected plates.

### 2. Bearing Failure of the Plate or the Bolt (Cl. 10.3.4)
The bolt crushes the plate material (or vice-versa) at the contact face.

### 3. Tension Failure of the Plate (Tearing of Net Section) (Cl. 10.3.1)
Plate tears along the line of bolt holes due to reduced cross-section.

### 4. Shear Failure of the Plate (Shear Tearing / Block Shear) (Cl. 10.3.1)
Plate shears out at the edge along two parallel lines of bolts — a combination of tension and shear.

### 5. Tension Failure of the Bolt (Cl. 10.3.5)
Bolt fails by tension at the root of the thread (relevant for connections with tensile load on bolt).

### 6. Crushing of the Plate Edge / Edge Shear
If edge distance too small, plate edge shears out ahead of the bolt.

### Schematic — Failure Modes

```
   ┌────────────────────────┐
   │ PLATE (Failure modes)  │
   └────────────────────────┘
   ⓐ Tearing of plate (net section)  ──► across hole line
   ⓑ Bearing of plate behind bolt    ──► at contact face
   ⓒ Shearing of plate edge          ──► along edge
   ⓓ Shear of bolt shank             ──► across bolt
   ⓔ Tension of bolt (at threads)    ──► along bolt axis
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Note for KTU Exams:** In a multi-bolt connection, you must check **all five** failure modes and design for the **minimum** of the strengths.

## 2.5 Design of Bearing-Type Connection (Concentric Load)

### Steps (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.3)
1. Compute **factored load** $T_u = 1.5 \times T$ (or use load combination)
2. Assume bolt diameter $d$
3. Compute **nominal shear strength** $V_{nsb}$
4. Compute **nominal bearing strength** $V_{npb}$
5. Compute **bolt value** = minimum of shear and bearing strengths
6. Compute **plate tear strength** = $T_{dn} = 0.9 \times f_u \times A_n / \gamma_{mb}$
7. Provide required number of bolts: $n = T_u / V_{db,\min}$
8. Check **edge distance, end distance, pitch, gauge** (Cl. 10.2)

## 2.6 Design of Friction (HSFG) Connection

### Working Principle
The bolt is **pre-tensioned**, clamping the plates together. Load transfer occurs via the **frictional resistance** between contact surfaces — the bolt shank does NOT come into contact with the hole wall (no bearing).

### Design Strength (Cl. 10.4.5)

$$V_{dsf} = V_{nsf} / \gamma_{mf}$$

where

$$V_{nsf} = \mu_f \cdot n_e \cdot K_h \cdot F_0$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\mu_f$ | **Coefficient of friction** (= 0.55 for clean surfaces, 0.30 for galvanized, 0.20 for grit-blasted) |
| $n_e$ | Number of effective friction surfaces (1 for lap, 2 for butt-cover) |
| $K_h$ | Hole factor (1.0 for standard, 0.85 for oversized/long-slot) |
| $F_0$ | **Proof load** = $0.7 f_{ub} A_{nb}$ |
| $A_{nb}$ | Net tensile stress area of bolt |
| $f_{ub}$ | Ultimate tensile strength of bolt material |
| $\gamma_{mf}$ | Partial safety factor for HSFG = **1.25** (service), **1.10** (ultimate) |

> [!NOTE]
> The slip resistance of an HSFG bolt is **independent of bolt shear strength** and **bearing area**. Hence, HSFG bolts are not designed to act in shear/bearing — only in friction.

## 2.7 Prying Forces

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prying forces** are additional tensile forces induced in bolts of **flexible end-plate (or thin flange) connections** due to deformation of the flange. The bolt "pry open" the flange, increasing the tension in the bolt.

### Mechanism
Consider a T-stub or bracket flange connecting to a column. When the bolt pulls the flange, the flange tries to bend outward at its edges. This bending is restrained by the bolt, adding a **prying force $Q$** on the bolt.

### Design Equation (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.4.7)

The design tensile force on a bolt in a connection subject to prying is:

$$B = T / n + Q$$

The **bolt tension capacity** $T_{db}$ must satisfy:

$$B \;\leq\; T_{db}$$

### Conservative Approach (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.4.7.1)
- Take **$Q = 0$** and design the flange as a **rigid** element (no prying).
- **OR** use the **factor $\beta = 2$** method: $T_e = 2 \times$ external tension, then design bolt for $2T_e/n$.

> [!WARNING]
> The "$\beta = 2$" method is **conservative** but very commonly used in KTU examinations for quick design. Real design uses Q-factor formulation.

### Prying Force Magnitude (Eurocode-style)
$Q = \frac{w}{2} \cdot \left[ \frac{\delta \cdot \alpha - 0.5 \cdot t^2}{\alpha \cdot (1+\delta) - 0.5 \cdot t^2} \right]$
where parameters depend on flange geometry, bolt pitch, and edge distance.

## 2.8 Design of Bracket Connection (Eccentric Shear + Tension)

> [!NOTE]
> A **bracket connection** is a beam-to-column bracket where the load is applied with **eccentricity** from the bolt group centroid. The bolts experience **direct shear** plus **torsional shear**, and the **top bolts** experience **additional tension** due to moment.

### Bracket Bolt Subjected to:
- **Direct shear** $V = P / n$
- **Torsional moment** $M = P \cdot e$ (eccentricity $e$ from CG of bolt group)
- **Tension in top bolts** $T = M \cdot y_{\max} / \sum y_i^2$

### Failure Checks for Each Bolt:
1. **Resultant shear** $R = \sqrt{V^2 + V_T^2} \leq V_{db}$ (bolt shear strength)
2. **Bolt tension** $T \leq T_{db}$ (bolt tension strength)
3. **Combined shear and tension** — Interaction equation:

$$\left(\frac{V}{V_{db}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{T}{T_{db}}\right)^2 \;\leq\; 1.0$$

(This is the IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.3.6 interaction check.)

# 3.1 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas per **IS 800:2007**, with $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ (bolt), $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$ (weld), $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ (ultimate steel), $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ (yielding steel).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Cl. Ref. |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shear strength of bolt | $V_{dsb} = \dfrac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \dfrac{(n_n A_{nb} + n_s A_{sb})}{\gamma_{mb}}$ | 10.3.3 |
| 2 | Bearing strength of bolt | $V_{dpb} = \dfrac{2.5 \, k_b \, d \, t \, f_u}{\gamma_{mb}}$ | 10.3.4 |
| 3 | Bearing factor $k_b$ | $k_b = \min\left(1.0, \dfrac{e}{3d_0}, \dfrac{p}{3d_0} - 0.25, \dfrac{f_{ub}}{f_u}\right)$ | 10.3.4 |
| 4 | Tension strength of bolt | $T_{db} = \dfrac{0.90 \, f_{ub} \, A_n}{\gamma_{mb}}$ | 10.3.5 |
| 5 | HSFG bolt slip strength | $V_{dsf} = \dfrac{\mu_f \, n_e \, K_h \, F_0}{\gamma_{mf}}$ | 10.4.5 |
| 6 | Proof load | $F_0 = 0.70 \, f_{ub} \, A_{nb}$ | 10.4.5 |
| 7 | Net section rupture (plate) | $T_{dn} = \dfrac{0.90 \, f_u \, A_n}{\gamma_{m1}}$ | 6.3.1 / 10.3.1 |
| 8 | Block shear strength | $T_{db} = \dfrac{A_{vg} f_y}{\sqrt{3} \gamma_{m0}} + \dfrac{0.9 A_{tn} f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$ (if $A_{vn} \geq A_{vn,lim}$) | 6.4.1 |
| 9 | Edge distance (min) | $1.5 \times$ hole dia (cut) / $1.7 \times$ hole dia (rolled) | 10.2.4.2 |
| 10 | Pitch (min) | $2.5 \times$ bolt dia | 10.2.3.1 |
| 11 | Fillet weld strength | $f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$ | 11.4.3 |
| 12 | Throat thickness (90°) | $t_t = 0.707 s$ | 11.4.1 |
| 13 | Effective length of weld | $l_w = L - 2 \times s$ (min) | 11.4.1 |
| 14 | Bolt value (bearing-type) | $V_{db} = \min(V_{dsb}, V_{dpb})$ | — |
| 15 | Combined shear + tension | $\left(\dfrac{V_{sb}}{V_{db}}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{T_b}{T_{db}}\right)^2 \leq 1.0$ | 10.3.6 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Glossary:**
> - $A_{nb}$ = net tensile area (thread root), $A_{sb}$ = shank (nominal) area = $\pi d^2/4$
> - $A_n$ = net cross-section area of plate
> - $n_n$ = number of shear planes with threads
> - $n_s$ = number of shear planes without threads (i.e., on shank)
> - $d_0$ = hole diameter, $d$ = bolt shank diameter
> - $f_{ub}$ = ultimate tensile stress of **bolt** material
> - $f_u$ = ultimate tensile stress of **plate** material

---

# 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Shear Strength of a Bolt

The derivation follows basic mechanics of materials combined with IS 800:2007 safety factors.

### Step 1 — Average Shear Stress at Bolt Failure
When a bolt is sheared across one cross-section, the **average shear stress** required for failure is given by the **maximum shear stress theory** (Tresca):

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{f_y}{2} \quad \text{(for ductile steel, at yield)}$$

However, for **bolt steel** which is harder, the shear failure occurs at the **ultimate** stress level:

$$\tau_{fail} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

The factor $\sqrt{3}$ arises from the **von Mises equivalent stress** relation: $\tau_{max} = f_u / \sqrt{3}$ in uniaxial tension. (This uses the maximum distortion energy theory.)

### Step 2 — Nominal Shear Capacity
The nominal (un-factored) shear capacity of **one shear plane** through the threaded portion is:

$$V_{nsb,\text{thread}} = \tau_{fail} \cdot A_{nb} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} A_{nb}$$

For a **shank portion** (no threads):

$$V_{nsb,\text{shank}} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} A_{sb} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Multiple Shear Planes
If a bolt is in **single shear** ($n_n = 1, n_s = 0$) → 1 shear plane:
$$V_{nsb} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} A_{nb}$$

If in **double shear** with thread in both planes ($n_n = 2, n_s = 0$):
$$V_{nsb} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot 2 A_{nb}$$

Generalized:
$$V_{nsb} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} (n_n A_{nb} + n_s A_{sb})$$

### Step 4 — Apply Partial Safety Factor
$$\boxed{\,V_{dsb} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{(n_n A_{nb} + n_s A_{sb})}{\gamma_{mb}}\,}$$

with $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$.

### Step 5 — Standard Strength Values
Substituting $f_{ub} = 400$ MPa (Grade 4.6), $A_{nb} \approx 0.78 A_{sb}$ (typical thread reduction), $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$:

$$V_{dsb} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 1.25} \cdot 0.78 \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \approx 90.3 \, d^2 \, (\text{N, with } d \text{ in mm})$$

This is often tabulated as $\approx 90 d^2$ for **4.6 grade bolts in single shear** (threaded plane).

---

# 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Bearing Strength of Bolt on Plate

### Step 1 — Crushing Mechanism
When a bolt presses on the edge of a hole, the contact area is a **rectangle** of dimensions $d \times t$ (bolt diameter $\times$ plate thickness). The **bearing pressure** causing failure is empirically related to the plate's ultimate tensile strength.

### Step 2 — Empirical Formula (AISC / IS 800)
From experimental data on bolted plates, the bearing stress at failure is:

$$\sigma_{b,\text{fail}} = 2.5 \cdot f_u$$

The factor **2.5** accounts for the redistribution of stress beyond elastic limit (work hardening) and triaxial constraint.

### Step 3 — Bearing Capacity
Projected bearing area $= d \cdot t$
$$V_{npb} = 2.5 \cdot d \cdot t \cdot f_u$$

### Step 4 — Modifying Factor $k_b$
For edge distance $e$, pitch $p$, and hole diameter $d_0$, the bearing strength is reduced if the plate edge fails by shear. The factor $k_b$ accounts for these edge/pitch effects:

$$k_b = \min\left(1.0,\; \frac{e}{3d_0},\; \frac{p}{3d_0} - 0.25,\; \frac{f_{ub}}{f_u}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Design Bearing Strength
$$\boxed{\,V_{dpb} = \frac{2.5 \, k_b \, d \, t \, f_u}{\gamma_{mb}}\,}$$

with $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$.

### Step 6 — Numerical Example Standard
For $f_u = 410$ MPa, $d = 20$ mm, $t = 12$ mm, $k_b = 1.0$:

$$V_{dpb} = \frac{2.5 \times 1.0 \times 20 \times 12 \times 410}{1.25} = \frac{246{,}000}{1.25} = 196{,}800 \; \text{N} = 196.8 \; \text{kN}$$

---

# 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation — HSFG Bolt Slip Strength

### Step 1 — Pre-tensioning
HSFG bolts are tightened so that the bolt tension reaches the **proof load**:

$$F_0 = 0.7 \, f_{ub} \, A_{nb}$$

The factor 0.7 ensures the bolt operates below its proportional limit and avoids yielding during installation.

### Step 2 — Frictional Force on Interface
By Coulomb friction, the shear force that can be transmitted across one friction surface is:

$$F = \mu_f \cdot F_0$$

### Step 3 — Multiple Surfaces & Hole Factor
For a butt joint with 2 cover plates, there are **2 friction surfaces** ($n_e = 2$):
$$V_{nsf} = \mu_f \cdot n_e \cdot K_h \cdot F_0$$

### Step 4 — Apply Partial Safety Factor
$$\boxed{\,V_{dsf} = \frac{\mu_f \, n_e \, K_h \, F_0}{\gamma_{mf}}\,}$$

For HSFG at **service limit state**, $\gamma_{mf} = 1.25$; at **ultimate**, $\gamma_{mf} = 1.10$.

### Step 5 — Standard Numerical
For 10.9 grade M20 bolt: $f_{ub} = 1040$ MPa, $A_{nb} = 245$ mm², $\mu_f = 0.55$, $n_e = 1$, $K_h = 1.0$:

$$F_0 = 0.7 \times 1040 \times 245 = 178{,}360 \; \text{N} = 178.36 \; \text{kN}$$

$$V_{dsf} = \frac{0.55 \times 1 \times 1.0 \times 178.36}{1.10} = 89.18 \; \text{kN per bolt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **service slip strength**. The ultimate strength is $V_{dsf} / \gamma_{mf}$ (different factor). For HSFG connections, **slip must not occur under service loads** — that is the design intent.

---

# 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation — Prying Force in T-Stub Flange

> [!NOTE]
> Prying analysis is typically done using the **T-stub model** (Eurocode 3 / IS 800 Annex G equivalent).

### Step 1 — Geometry & Force Diagram
Consider a T-stub flange of thickness $T_f$ connected by 2 bolts to a column flange. A tensile force $B$ acts on each bolt.

**Forces on the flange:**
- Bolt tension $B$
- Reaction from column flange $F/2$ at each bolt
- Prying force $Q$ at the flange edge (deformation-induced)
- Prying force $B - Q$ transmitted to bolt (since $Q$ reduces bolt tension)

### Step 2 — Equilibrium & Plastic Hinge Concept
A plastic hinge forms in the flange at the bolt line and at the edge. The **yield line mechanism** gives:

$$Q = \frac{w}{2} \cdot \left[ \frac{4 \, B \, e_1 - w \, T_f^2 \, f_y / 2}{2 \, n \, e_1 - w}\right]$$

where:
- $w$ = length of the T-stub (out-of-plane)
- $e_1$ = distance from bolt centre to flange edge
- $n$ = distance from bolt to web

### Step 3 — Conservative IS 800 Approach
IS 800:2007 (Cl. 10.4.7) simplifies by recommending:
- **Option 1**: Take $Q = 0$ but design bolt for $T_{eff} = 1.4 \times T_{external}$ (i.e., $\beta = 1.4$)
- **Option 2**: Take $\beta = 2$ for simplicity in design office

### Step 4 — Interaction Check
The bolt in a prying connection must satisfy both:
$$T_{db} \geq B \quad \text{(tensile capacity)}$$
$$V_{db} \geq V \quad \text{(if shear also present)}$$

For combined:
$$\left(\frac{V}{V_{db}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{B}{T_{db}}\right)^2 \leq 1.0$$

# 4.1 Flow Diagram — Limit State Design Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1: Identify Loads DL LL WL EL] --> B[STEP 2: Apply Load Combinations with gamma_f]
    B --> C[STEP 3: Compute Factored Load Effect Q_d]
    C --> D[STEP 4: Select Material f_y and f_u]
    D --> E[STEP 5: Compute Design Strength R_d = R_k / gamma_m]
    E --> F[STEP 6: Check R_d greater than or equal to Q_d]
    F --> G{Is Check Satisfied?}
    G -- YES --> H[Design is SAFE Provide Section Details]
    G -- NO --> I[Revise Section: Increase Size or Change Material]
    I --> E
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```

# 4.4 Block Diagram — Bracket Connection (Eccentric Load) Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Load P at Eccentricity e from Bolt Group CG] --> S1[Direct Shear per bolt: V_d = P / n]
    P --> S2[Torsional Moment: M = P × e]
    S2 --> S3[Torsional Shear on bolt i: V_ti = M × r_i / sum r_i^2]
    S1 --> S4[Resultant Shear on bolt i: R_i = sqrt V_d^2 + V_ti^2]
    S3 --> S4
    P --> S5[Tension in topmost bolt: T_max = M × y_max / sum y_i^2]
    S4 --> S6[Check: R_i less than or equal to V_db]
    S5 --> S7[Check: T_max less than or equal to T_db]
    S6 --> S8{Combined Check}
    S7 --> S8
    S8 -->|Satisfied| OK[Bracket SAFE]
    S8 -->|Not Satisfied| REV[Increase Bolt Size or Reduce Eccentricity]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!NOTE]
> All questions modelled on KTU 2024 ESE pattern. Tag with simulated past-year question identity.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Limit State. List the various limit states to be considered in the design of steel structures as per IS 800:2007.**

#### Model Answer:
A **Limit State** is a condition of a structure at which it becomes unsafe (reaches ultimate capacity) or unfit for use (reaches a serviceability limit). As per **IS 800:2007**, the design must satisfy:

1. **Limit State of Strength (Ultimate)** — collapse, fracture, buckling, yielding, fatigue
2. **Limit State of Serviceability** — deflection, vibration, durability, fire resistance

The design equation is:
$$Q_d \leq R_d$$
where $Q_d = \gamma_f \cdot Q_k$ (factored load) and $R_d = R_k / \gamma_m$ (design strength).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Distinguish between bearing type and friction type bolted connections.**

#### Model Answer:

| Aspect | Bearing Type | Friction Type (HSFG) |
|---|---|---|
| Load transfer | Shear + Bearing on hole wall | Friction at plate interface |
| Pre-tensioning | None | Pre-tensioned to proof load |
| Slip | Slip permitted in service | **No slip** in service |
| Bolt grade | 4.6 (mild) | 8.8 / 10.9 (high-strength) |
| Application | Buildings, trusses | Bridges, dynamic/fatigue |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Tightening | Spanner | Calibrated wrench |

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the various **modes of failure** of a bolted connection. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A tie member 80 mm wide × 12 mm thick is to be connected to a 12 mm thick gusset plate using 6 mm diameter bolts of grade 4.6. The connection has 2 bolts in a row, in **single shear**. Determine the **strength of the connection** and the **safe load** it can carry. Take $f_u = 410$ MPa, hole diameter = 17 mm, edge distance = 30 mm. (7 Marks)

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — Modes of Failure

The five primary modes of failure of a bearing-type bolted connection are:

1. **Shear failure of the bolt** — bolt shears at the interface between connected plates; resisted by $V_{dsb} = (f_{ub}/\sqrt{3}) \cdot A_{nb} / \gamma_{mb}$.

2. **Bearing failure of plate or bolt** — material at hole contact area crushes; resisted by $V_{dpb} = 2.5 k_b d t f_u / \gamma_{mb}$.

3. **Tension failure of plate (Tearing at net section)** — plate tears across the line of holes due to reduced area; resisted by $T_{dn} = 0.9 f_u A_n / \gamma_{m1}$.

4. **Shear failure of plate at edge (Block shear)** — edge of plate shears along two parallel bolt lines; combination of shear + tension failure.

5. **Tension failure of bolt at thread root** — when bolt is loaded in tension; resisted by $T_{db} = 0.9 f_{ub} A_n / \gamma_{mb}$.

The design strength is the **minimum of all these**.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — Strength of Bolted Connection

**Given:**
- Main plate: 80 × 12 mm
- Gusset plate: 12 mm thick
- Bolts: **6 mm diameter** (likely typo for **16 mm**; using 16 mm as standard for KTU problems)
- Bolt grade 4.6, $f_{ub} = 400$ MPa
- Plate $f_u = 410$ MPa, $f_y = 250$ MPa
- $d_0 = 17$ mm (assumed; will compute as $d + 1.5$ mm)
- Edge distance $e = 30$ mm

**Step 1 — Bolt Properties (M16, Grade 4.6)**
- $d = 16$ mm
- $A_{sb} = \pi \times 16^2 / 4 = 201.06$ mm²
- $A_{nb} = 157$ mm² (standard value for M16)
- Single shear: $n_n = 1$, $n_s = 0$

**Step 2 — Shear Strength of Bolt**
$$V_{dsb} = \frac{f_{ub}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{A_{nb}}{\gamma_{mb}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{157}{1.25}$$

$$V_{dsb} = \frac{400 \times 157}{1.732 \times 1.25} = \frac{62{,}800}{2.165} = 29{,}007 \; \text{N} \approx 29.0 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Bearing Strength of Bolt**
$$k_b = \min\left(1.0,\; \frac{e}{3d_0},\; \frac{p}{3d_0} - 0.25,\; \frac{f_{ub}}{f_u}\right)$$

Assuming pitch $p = 2.5d = 40$ mm:
- $e/(3d_0) = 30/(3 \times 17) = 0.588$
- $p/(3d_0) - 0.25 = 40/(3 \times 17) - 0.25 = 0.784 - 0.25 = 0.534$
- $f_{ub}/f_u = 400/410 = 0.976$
- $k_b = \min(1.0, 0.588, 0.534, 0.976) = 0.534$

$$V_{dpb} = \frac{2.5 \times k_b \times d \times t \times f_u}{\gamma_{mb}} = \frac{2.5 \times 0.534 \times 16 \times 12 \times 410}{1.25}$$

$$V_{dpb} = \frac{105{,}081}{1.25} = 84{,}065 \; \text{N} \approx 84.07 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Bolt Value (minimum)**
$$V_{db} = \min(V_{dsb}, V_{dpb}) = 29.0 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 5 — Net Section Strength of Plate (2 bolts in line, deduction of 1 hole for tearing check)**
Wait — 2 bolts in a **row** (perpendicular to load) means tearing is across the **line of bolts**; for tearing strength:

For tearing of plate through **one line** of bolts:
$$A_n = (B - n_h \cdot d_0) \cdot t = (80 - 1 \times 17) \times 12 = 756 \; \text{mm}^2$$

Wait, for **2 bolts side-by-side in a row perpendicular to load**, the critical net section is across the **first bolt** (tearing is checked at the row, deducting 1 hole):
$$A_n = (80 - 1 \times 17) \times 12 = 63 \times 12 = 756 \; \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Tearing Strength**
$$T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \times f_u \times A_n}{\gamma_{m1}} = \frac{0.9 \times 410 \times 756}{1.25} = 223{,}133 \; \text{N} \approx 223.1 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 7 — Connection Strength**

The connection has **2 bolts**:
- Bolt strength: $2 \times 29.0 = 58.0$ kN
- Plate tearing: 223.1 kN
- Connection strength = **58.0 kN** (governed by bolt shear)

**Step 8 — Safe Load (working load)**
Using partial safety factor for load $\gamma_f = 1.5$:
$$T_{safe} = \frac{T_{u,\text{conn}}}{1.5} = \frac{58.0}{1.5} \approx 38.7 \; \text{kN}$$

> **Final Answer:** Connection strength $T_u = 58.0$ kN; Safe working load = **38.7 kN**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the design philosophy of **HSFG (High-Strength Friction-Grip) bolts**. Derive the expression for **slip resistance** of an HSFG bolt. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An ISMB 300 beam is to be connected to a column flange using a **bracket connection** with 4 bolts of 16 mm diameter (Grade 4.6) arranged in 2 rows × 2 columns. The bracket is welded to the beam flange. The factored load is **80 kN** at an eccentricity of **150 mm** from the centroid of the bolt group. Check whether the bolts are safe. Take $f_{ub} = 400$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa. (7 Marks)

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a) — HSFG Bolts

**Working Principle:**
HSFG bolts are pre-tensioned to a high stress, clamping the connected plates together with substantial contact pressure. The external load is transferred across the joint by the **frictional resistance** generated at the plate interface, **NOT** by shear in the bolt shank or bearing on the hole wall. The bolt shank never comes into contact with the hole.

**Design Philosophy:**
- Slip must NOT occur under **service loads** (limit state of serviceability).
- The connection is checked at **service** for slip and at **ultimate** for bolt strength.

**Derivation of Slip Resistance:**

**Step 1 — Bolt Proof Load** (pre-tensioning force)
The bolt is tightened to its **proof load** $F_0$:
$$F_0 = 0.7 \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A_{nb}$$

The factor 0.7 ensures the bolt is below the proportional limit (so it does not yield or relax).

**Step 2 — Friction Force at Plate Interface**
By Coulomb's law, the maximum frictional force across one friction surface is:
$$F = \mu_f \cdot F_0$$

**Step 3 — Multiple Surfaces & Hole Adjustment**
For a **butt-cum-cover joint**, there are 2 friction surfaces ($n_e = 2$). The hole factor $K_h$ accounts for reduced clamping due to oversized/slotted holes.

**Step 4 — Slip Resistance**
$$\boxed{\,V_{dsf} = \frac{\mu_f \cdot n_e \cdot K_h \cdot F_0}{\gamma_{mf}} = \frac{\mu_f \cdot n_e \cdot K_h \cdot 0.7 \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A_{nb}}{\gamma_{mf}}\,}$$

For service: $\gamma_{mf} = 1.25$; For ultimate: $\gamma_{mf} = 1.10$.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b) — Bracket Connection Check

**Given:**
- 4 bolts, 16 mm diameter, Grade 4.6, $f_{ub} = 400$ MPa
- Arrangement: 2 rows × 2 columns
- Pitch $p = 50$ mm (assumed), gauge $g = 50$ mm
- Factored load $P = 80$ kN at eccentricity $e = 150$ mm
- $A_{nb} = 157$ mm², $A_{sb} = 201$ mm²

**Step 1 — Geometry**
Distance of extreme bolt from CG:
$$r_{max} = \sqrt{25^2 + 25^2} = 35.36 \; \text{mm}$$
$$\sum r_i^2 = 4 \times 35.36^2 = 4 \times 1250 = 5000 \; \text{mm}^2$$
(All 4 bolts at same distance from CG)

**Step 2 — Direct Shear on Each Bolt**
$$V_d = \frac{P}{n} = \frac{80}{4} = 20 \; \text{kN per bolt}$$

**Step 3 — Torsional Shear on Each Bolt**
$$M = P \cdot e = 80 \times 0.15 = 12 \; \text{kN·m} = 12{,}000 \; \text{kN·mm} = 12 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm}$$
$$V_t = \frac{M \cdot r_{max}}{\sum r_i^2} = \frac{12 \times 10^6 \times 35.36}{5000} = 84{,}864 \; \text{N} = 84.86 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Resultant Shear on Each Bolt**
$$R = \sqrt{V_d^2 + V_t^2} = \sqrt{20^2 + 84.86^2} = \sqrt{400 + 7201.5} = 87.2 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 5 — Bolt Shear Capacity**
$$V_{dsb} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{157}{1.25} = 29.0 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 6 — Shear Check**
$R = 87.2$ kN $> V_{dsb} = 29.0$ kN → **NOT SAFE** ❌

> **Conclusion:** The bolts are **NOT safe** in shear. To make the connection safe, either:
> - Increase bolt size to M20 ($V_{dsb} \approx 45$ kN) — still not enough, need M24 (≈ 65 kN)
> - Reduce eccentricity $e$ (move load closer to bolt group)
> - Use HSFG bolts or increase number of bolts

**Suggested redesign:** Use 6 bolts (3 rows × 2 columns) at M20: $V_{dsb} = 45.3$ kN, then $V_t$ reduces to $\approx 47$ kN, $R \approx 51$ kN, **SAFE**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU steel design exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factors** — using $f_u$ directly without $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ or $f_y$ without $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$. Marks deducted = 1 to 2.
> 2. **Wrong area** — using $A_{sb}$ (shank) instead of $A_{nb}$ (net thread area) for bolt shear. Always use $A_{nb}$ in shear.
> 3. **Confusing load factors** — applying 1.5 to already-factored load, or using 1.2 when 1.5 is required.
> 4. **Skipping modes of failure** — checking only bolt shear and forgetting bearing, tearing, and block shear.
> 5. **Forgetting 0.9 factor** in tension and tearing capacity (accounts for uneven stress distribution).
> 6. **Bracket connections** — students often compute torsional shear but forget direct shear and the **combined interaction** check $\left(\frac{V}{V_{db}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{T}{T_{db}}\right)^2 \leq 1.0$.
> 7. **HSFG** — students use the **bolt shear capacity** $V_{dsb}$ for HSFG; the correct check is **slip strength** $V_{dsf}$, not bolt shear.
> 8. **Units** — writing $f_u$ in MPa but computing area in cm². Always be consistent: use **N and mm**.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Limit State Design** = strength + serviceability; check $Q_d \leq R_d$ with $\gamma_f$ on load side and $\gamma_m$ on resistance side.
- ✅ **Limit States per IS 800:2007**: Ultimate (collapse/fatigue) + Serviceability (deflection/vibration).
- ✅ **Partial Safety Factors**: $\gamma_{m0}=1.10$ (yielding), $\gamma_{m1}=1.25$ (ultimate), $\gamma_{mb}=1.25$ (bolt), $\gamma_{mw}=1.50$ (weld).
- ✅ **Fastener types**: Rivets (obsolete) → Bolts (bearing/friction) → Welds (fillet/butt).
- ✅ **Bolt classification**: 4.6 (black), 5.6/6.6 (fitted), 8.8/10.9 (HSFG).
- ✅ **HSFG working principle**: Friction transfer, no shear/bearing. Slip must not occur in service.
- ✅ **Lap joint** = single shear (eccentric); **Butt joint** = double shear (concentric).
- ✅ **Bolts vs Welds**: Bolts — reversible, less skill, fatigue-friendly. Welds — permanent, smoother, site-fume issues.
- ✅ **Modes of failure (5)**: Shear of bolt, Bearing of plate, Tearing of net section, Block shear, Tension of bolt.
- ✅ **Shear strength**: $V_{dsb} = (f_{ub}/\sqrt{3}) \cdot A_{nb} / \gamma_{mb}$ (use $A_{nb}$, not $A_{sb}$).
- ✅ **Bearing strength**: $V_{dpb} = 2.5 k_b d t f_u / \gamma_{mb}$; $k_b = \min(1.0, e/3d_0, p/3d_0-0.25, f_{ub}/f_u)$.
- ✅ **Tearing of plate**: $T_{dn} = 0.9 f_u A_n / \gamma_{m1}$.
- ✅ **HSFG slip strength**: $V_{dsf} = \mu_f n_e K_h F_0 / \gamma_{mf}$; $F_0 = 0.7 f_{ub} A_{nb}$.
- ✅ **Prying forces**: Additional tension in bolt due to flange bending. Conservative design uses $\beta = 2$.
- ✅ **Bracket connection**: Direct shear + Torsional shear + Tension in top bolts. Combined interaction check: $\left(\frac{V}{V_{db}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{T}{T_{db}}\right)^2 \leq 1.0$.
- ✅ **Pitch min** = $2.5d$; **Edge distance min** = $1.5 d_0$ (cut) / $1.7 d_0$ (rolled/hand-cut).
- ✅ **Bolt value** = minimum of (shear capacity, bearing capacity); **Number of bolts** = $T_u / \text{bolt value}$.
- ✅ **Block shear** = combined shear + tension at plate end (governs when 2 bolts are in line with edge).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welds - Specifications and Effective Area of Welds - Fillet and Butt Connections - Axially Loaded Connections for Plate and Angle Truss Members - Design of Bracket Connections. Tension Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET602 - DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Welds - Specifications and Effective Area of Welds - Fillet and Butt Connections - Axially Loaded Connections for Plate and Angle Truss Members - Design of Bracket Connections. Tension Members

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Welds – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding** is a process of joining two pieces of metal by raising the temperature of the metal to a plastic or fluid state at the joint interface, with or without the application of pressure and with or without the use of a filler metal, so that the two pieces become one continuous, monolithic structure. (Ref: IS 800:2007, Cl. 9.1.1)

In the context of **structural steel design** as per **IS 800:2007 (General Construction in Steel – Code of Practice)**, welding is the most common method of fabricating steel connections in trusses, beams, columns, and brackets.

### 1.2 Types of Welds Relevant to KTU Syllabus

| Type of Weld | Description | Application |
|--------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Fillet Weld** | Triangular cross-section deposited in the corner formed by two plates | Most common for lap, T, and corner joints |
| **Butt Weld** | Weld deposited in a gap between two butting plates | Used where full strength of plate is required |
| **Slot / Plug Weld** | Weld in a slot or hole in one plate | Used to supplement fillet welds or for shear transfer |
| **Spot Weld** | Small circular weld | For thin sheets |

> [!NOTE]
> For the **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2**, the focus is strictly on **Fillet Welds** and **Butt Welds** and their applications in **axially loaded connections, truss members, and bracket connections**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy – The "Metal Glue" Concept:**
> Think of welding as **"metallic glue that becomes the metal itself."** Unlike bolts (which are like rivets holding two pieces of paper together), welds fuse the two steel plates into a single piece. Imagine melting two ice cubes together at their contact face — once refrozen, you cannot tell where one ended and the other began. A **fillet weld** is like the small triangular bead of glue at the corner of a wooden frame, while a **butt weld** is like two boards butted end-to-end with glue filling the entire joint.

- A **fillet weld** is generally the **economical** choice for transferring **shear forces** between plates.
- A **butt weld** is used when the **full tensile strength** of the plate must be developed across the joint.

### 1.4 Standard Specifications (As per IS 800:2007 & AWS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Specifications (Cl. 9.5, IS 800:2007):**
> - **Minimum size of fillet weld:** Based on the **thicker of the two plates** being joined.
> - **Maximum size of fillet weld:** Limited to **thinner plate thickness minus 1.5 mm** (for edges) or **thinner plate thickness** (for rounded edges).
> - **Effective length of fillet weld:** Not less than **4 × size of weld** (minimum), or **40 mm** whichever is greater.
> - **Effective throat thickness:**
>   - Fillet weld (90° between fusion faces): $t_t = 0.7 s$
>   - Butt weld (full penetration): $t_t = t$ (thickness of thinner plate)
> - **Partial safety factor for welds ($\gamma_{mw}$):**
>   - **Shop weld:** $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$
>   - **Field weld:** $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$

### 1.5 Effective Area of Welds – Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective Area ($A_e$)** of a weld is the area upon which the design stress is calculated. It is the product of the **effective throat thickness ($t_t$)** and the **effective length ($L_e$)** of the weld.
> 
> $$A_e = t_t \times L_e$$
> 
> For a fillet weld: $A_e = 0.7s \times L_e$
> For a butt weld: $A_e = t \times L_e$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Fillet Welds

#### 2.1.1 Geometry of a Fillet Weld

A **fillet weld** is approximately triangular in cross-section. The three sides are:
1. **Fusion face (root)** – the face between the two plates
2. **Two legs (sides)** – the outer faces

For a **90° fillet weld** (most common case):
- **Size of weld (s):** Leg length of the triangle
- **Effective throat thickness ($t_t$):** The perpendicular distance from the root to the hypotenuse

$$t_t = 0.7 \times s \quad \text{(for 90° angle between fusion faces)}$$

#### 2.1.2 Why $0.7 s$?

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **0.7** comes from $\sin 45° = \cos 45° = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$, which is the perpendicular height of a right-angled isosceles triangle with leg $s$.

#### 2.1.3 Size Limitations (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.5.3)

| Parameter | Specification | Purpose |
|-----------|---------------|---------|
| **Minimum size ($s_{min}$)** | Based on thicker plate, see table below | Prevents rapid cooling, ensures adequate strength |
| **Maximum size ($s_{max}$)** | $t - 1.5$ mm (along edge) | Prevents melting of thinner plate |

| Thickness of Thicker Plate (mm) | Minimum Size of Fillet Weld (mm) |
|--------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| ≤ 10 | 3 |
| 10–20 | 5 |
| 20–32 | 6 |
| 32–50 | 8 (first run); 10 (min. size after first run) |
| > 50 | 10 |

### 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Butt Welds

A **butt weld** is made by preparing the edges of the two plates (square, V, U, or J preparation), placing them together with a small gap, and depositing filler metal.

- **Full penetration butt weld:** Effective throat = thickness of **thinner plate**.
- **Partial penetration butt weld:** Effective throat = depth of penetration, which is less than the plate thickness (cl. 9.6.3.2 of IS 800:2007).

> [!TIP]
> For design purposes, **transverse butt welds** (weld axis perpendicular to the line of force) develop the **full strength of the parent metal**, while **longitudinal butt welds** have lower efficiency (≈ 80–100% of the plate strength).

### 2.3 Design Strength of Welds (IS 800:2007, Cl. 9.6)

#### 2.3.1 Design Strength of Fillet Weld

The design strength of a fillet weld is governed by the **lower** of:

**(a) Shear strength of weld metal:**
$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_{uw}}{\gamma_{mw}}$$

**(b) Shear strength of parent metal (in the fusion zone):**
$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$

The design strength of a fillet weld per unit length is:
$$f_{w} = 0.7 s \times f_{wd}$$

**Total design strength of fillet weld:**
$$R_{dw} = 0.7 s \cdot L_e \cdot f_{wd}$$

where:
- $f_{uw}$ = ultimate strength of weld metal (E250 electrodes: $f_{uw} = 410$ MPa)
- $f_u$ = ultimate strength of parent metal
- $\gamma_{mw}$ = partial safety factor (1.25 shop, 1.50 field)

#### 2.3.2 Design Strength of Butt Weld

For a full penetration butt weld, the joint is as strong as the parent metal:
$$R_{db} = t \cdot b \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$$

where $b$ is the width of plate, $f_y$ is yield strength, $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$.

### 2.4 Axially Loaded Connections for Plate and Angle Truss Members

#### 2.4.1 Plate Truss Member Connection

For an **axially loaded plate** connected to a gusset plate:
- Weld is provided on **both sides** of the plate.
- Each face carries **half the load**.
- **Strength of weld = 2 × 0.7s × L_e × f_{wd} ≥ Axial Load**

#### 2.4.2 Angle Truss Member Connection

For an **angle member** connected to a gusset plate, the load is **eccentric** relative to the centroid of the angle. IS 800:2007 provides a simplified method (Cl. 10.3.5):

- **Heel side (longer leg) carries $k_1$ of the load**
- **Toe side (shorter leg) carries $k_2$ of the load**
- $k_1 + k_2 = 1.0$

| Connection Type | $k_1$ | $k_2$ |
|-----------------|-------|-------|
| Equal-leg angle welded on both sides | 0.7 | 0.3 |
| Unequal-leg angle (longer leg welded) | 0.7 | 0.3 |
| Unequal-leg angle (shorter leg welded) | 0.75 | 0.25 |
| Equal-leg angle welded on one side only | 0.5 | 0.5 |

**Total weld length required on each side:**
$$L_e = \frac{k_i \times P}{0.7 s \cdot f_{wd}}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Effective throat (90° fillet) | $t_t = 0.7 s$ | mm |
| 2 | Effective area of fillet weld | $A_e = 0.7 s \times L_e$ | mm² |
| 3 | Effective throat of butt weld | $t_t = t$ (thinner plate) | mm |
| 4 | Design strength of fillet weld | $R_{dw} = 0.7 s \cdot L_e \cdot \frac{f_{uw}}{\gamma_{mw}}$ | N |
| 5 | Design strength of butt weld | $R_{db} = t \cdot b \cdot \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$ | N |
| 6 | Partial safety factor (shop) | $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ | – |
| 7 | Partial safety factor (field) | $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$ | – |
| 8 | Min. weld size (10<t≤20) | 5 mm | mm |
| 9 | Max. weld size (along edge) | $t - 1.5$ mm | mm |
| 10 | Min. effective length | $4s$ or 40 mm | mm |
| 11 | Tension member (gross yield) | $T_{dg} = \frac{A_g \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$ | N |
| 12 | Tension member (rupture) | $T_{dn} = \frac{\alpha \cdot A_n \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$ | N |
| 13 | Block shear strength | $T_{db} = \frac{A_{vg} \cdot f_y}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} + \frac{0.9 A_{tn} \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$ | N |
| 14 | Bracket weld (combined) | $P = 0.7 s \cdot L_e \cdot f_{wd}$ (shear) + moment effect | N |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Trusses in industrial buildings, bridges, transmission towers:** Axially loaded angle members connected via gusset plates using fillet welds.
> - **Roof purlins, bracing systems:** Plate and angle connections.
> - **Crane brackets, machine supports:** Bracket-type connections with eccentric loading.
> - **Building columns to base plates:** Butt-welded column splices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Throat Thickness of Fillet Weld

**Given:** Two plates of thickness $t_1$ and $t_2$ joined at a right angle with a fillet weld of leg size $s$.

**Objective:** Derive the effective throat thickness $t_t$.

Consider the right-angled isosceles triangle formed by the fillet weld. The two equal sides are the legs of length $s$. The throat is the perpendicular from the right-angle vertex to the hypotenuse.

Using the Pythagoras theorem:
$$(\text{hypotenuse})^2 = s^2 + s^2 = 2s^2$$

The hypotenuse length is:
$$\text{hypotenuse} = s\sqrt{2}$$

The area of the triangle in two ways:
$$A = \frac{1}{2} \times s \times s = \frac{1}{2} \times s\sqrt{2} \times t_t$$

Equating:
$$\frac{s^2}{2} = \frac{s\sqrt{2} \cdot t_t}{2}$$

Solving for $t_t$:
$$s = s\sqrt{2} \cdot t_t \cdot \frac{1}{s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_t = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s}{1.414}$$

$$\boxed{t_t = 0.7 s}$$

This derivation confirms the KTU formula. The factor **0.7** is an approximation of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Design Strength of Fillet Weld per Unit Length

**Given:** Fillet weld with size $s$, electrode E250 ($f_{uw} = 410$ MPa), shop welding ($\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$).

**Step 1:** Effective throat thickness:
$$t_t = 0.7 s$$

**Step 2:** Ultimate shear strength of weld metal:
$$\tau_{uw} = \frac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

This accounts for the fact that weld metal fails in shear (von Mises yield criterion for pure shear, $\tau_y = f_y / \sqrt{3}$).

**Step 3:** Design shear strength of weld metal:
$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$

**Step 4:** Design strength per unit length of weld:
$$f_w = t_t \times f_{wd} = 0.7 s \times \frac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$$

**Step 5:** For shop welds with E250 electrodes:
$$f_w = 0.7 s \times \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 0.7 s \times 189.4 = 132.6 s \text{ N/mm}$$

$$\boxed{f_w \approx 132.6 \text{ N/mm per mm of weld size (shop, E250)}}$$

This is the most-cited value in KTU exams.

### 3.3 Worked Example 1: Axially Loaded Plate Connection

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 12 mm thick plate of Fe 410 grade is connected to a 10 mm thick gusset plate by fillet welds. The plate carries an axial tensile force of 350 kN. Design the weld (shop welded, E250 electrodes). Assume both sides of the plate are welded.

**Given:**
- Plate thickness, $t = 12$ mm
- Gusset thickness, $t_g = 10$ mm
- Axial load, $P = 350$ kN = 350,000 N
- $f_{uw} = 410$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa, $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$

**Step 1: Design shear strength of weld metal:**
$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Design strength per mm length of weld (per side):**
$$f_w = 0.7 s \times 189.4 = 132.6 s \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 3: Adopt weld size:**
- Thicker plate = 12 mm → from IS code table, $s_{min} = 5$ mm
- Thinner plate = 10 mm → $s_{max} = 10 - 1.5 = 8.5$ mm (along edge)
- **Adopt $s = 6$ mm**

**Step 4: Strength per mm of weld (per side):**
$$f_w = 132.6 \times 6 = 795.6 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 5: Total strength (two sides):**
$$R_{dw} = 2 \times 795.6 \times L_e$$

**Step 6: Equating to load:**
$$2 \times 795.6 \times L_e = 350,000$$
$$L_e = \frac{350,000}{2 \times 795.6} = 220 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7: Provided effective length:**
- Effective length = actual length (if no end returns) = 220 mm
- Actual length to be provided = $L_e + 2s$ (to account for craters) = $220 + 12 = 232$ mm
- **Adopt 232 mm or round up to 240 mm**

**Verification:** Min. effective length = $4s = 24$ mm < 220 mm ✓

**Result:** Provide **6 mm fillet weld of length 240 mm on both sides** of the plate.

### 3.4 Worked Example 2: Axially Loaded Angle Truss Member

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An equal-leg angle 80 × 80 × 10 mm of Fe 410 grade is connected to a 10 mm gusset plate to carry an axial force of 300 kN. Design the weld. The angle is welded on both sides. (Shop welding, E250 electrodes.)

**Given:**
- Angle: 80 × 80 × 10 mm (equal legs, both welded to gusset)
- $P = 300$ kN

**Step 1: Adopt weld size:**
- $s_{min} = 6$ mm (thicker plate 10 mm)
- $s_{max} = 10 - 1.5 = 8.5$ mm
- **Adopt $s = 6$ mm**

**Step 2: Distribution of load (equal-leg angle, both sides welded):**
- Heel side: $k_1 = 0.7$, so $P_1 = 0.7 \times 300 = 210$ kN
- Toe side: $k_2 = 0.3$, so $P_2 = 0.3 \times 300 = 90$ kN

**Step 3: Strength of weld per mm:**
$$f_w = 132.6 \times 6 = 795.6 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 4: Required weld lengths:**
$$L_1 = \frac{210,000}{795.6} = 264 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(heel side)}$$
$$L_2 = \frac{90,000}{795.6} = 113 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(toe side)}$$

**Step 5: Provided lengths (actual):**
$$L_{1,actual} = 264 + 12 = 276 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(heel side)}$$
$$L_{2,actual} = 113 + 12 = 125 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(toe side)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Important Constraint:** The provided weld length on the toe side must **not exceed** the available length of the toe-side leg minus deductions for top and bottom craters. Also, the longer leg side (heel) is the one farther from the gusset edge.

**Result:** Provide **6 mm fillet weld of length 280 mm on the heel side and 130 mm on the toe side.**

### 3.5 Worked Example 3: Design of Bracket Connection

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A bracket is welded to the flange of an I-section column as shown. The bracket carries an eccentric load of $P = 200$ kN at an eccentricity of $e = 300$ mm from the face of the column flange. Design the weld. (Column flange thickness = 16 mm, bracket plate thickness = 12 mm, $f_u = 410$ MPa, shop welding.)

**Solution Approach (Elastic Method per IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.4.7):**

For a bracket with two vertical welds (one on each side of the bracket plate):

**Step 1: Adopt weld size:**
- $s_{min} = 6$ mm, $s_{max} = 12 - 1.5 = 10.5$ mm
- **Adopt $s = 8$ mm**

**Step 2: Direct shear stress (uniform):**
$$\tau_1 = \frac{P}{2 \times 0.7 s \cdot L} = \frac{200,000}{2 \times 0.7 \times 8 \times L} = \frac{200,000}{11.2 L} \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Bending stress (linear, max at top/bottom):**
$$M = P \times e = 200 \times 0.3 = 60 \text{ kN·m} = 60 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

For two vertical welds (length L, spaced at distance 2h apart, where h is half the spacing):
$$\sigma_{bx} = \frac{M \cdot h}{2 \times I_x} = \frac{6M}{2 \cdot L^2} = \frac{3M}{L^2} \text{ (for unit spacing)}$$

Wait — more carefully, for **two parallel vertical welds** of length $L$ separated by distance $b$ (face-to-face):
$$I_{xx} = 2 \times \frac{0.7 s \cdot L^3}{12} + 2 \times 0.7 s \cdot L \times \left(\frac{b}{2}\right)^2$$

But for a simpler analysis, often the welds are taken as two lines of length $L$ each.

**Step 4: Combined stress check:**
$$f = \sqrt{\tau_1^2 + \tau_2^2 + \sigma_{bx}^2} \leq f_{wd}$$

where $f_{wd} = 189.4$ MPa.

**Step 5: Solve for required L:**

For a quick KTU-style approach, we balance:
$$\frac{P}{A_w} + \frac{6 M}{Z_w} \leq f_{wd}$$

For a bracket with **horizontal weld (top) + two vertical welds (sides):**

$$Z_w = \frac{0.7 s \cdot L^2}{2} \text{ (for pair of vertical welds)}$$

After algebraic manipulation, the required length is determined.

$$\boxed{L = \sqrt{\frac{6 M}{2 \times 0.7 s \cdot f_{wd}}} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 60 \times 10^6}{2 \times 0.7 \times 8 \times 189.4}} = 412 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 6: Provided length:**
Adopt **L = 420 mm** with 8 mm fillet weld on both sides of the bracket plate.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step Decision Flow

| Step | Decision | KTU Reference |
|------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Identify weld type (fillet / butt) | Cl. 9.1, 9.5 |
| 2 | Compute $s_{min}$ and $s_{max}$ | Cl. 10.5.3 |
| 3 | Adopt $s$ between min and max | – |
| 4 | Compute $f_{wd}$ | Cl. 9.6.1.2 |
| 5 | Compute load distribution ($k_1$, $k_2$) for angle | Cl. 10.3.5 |
| 6 | Compute required effective length $L_e$ | – |
| 7 | Add 2s for craters, check $L_e \geq 4s$ or 40 mm | Cl. 10.5.4.1 |
| 8 | Verify strength against applied load | – |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the terms: (a) Effective throat thickness, (b) Effective length of weld, (c) Effective area of weld.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Effective throat thickness ($t_t$):** The minimum thickness of the weld measured along a line perpendicular to the surface of the parent metal at the joint. For a 90° fillet weld, $t_t = 0.7s$ where $s$ is the size of weld. For a full penetration butt weld, $t_t$ = thickness of the thinner plate being joined.

(b) **Effective length of weld ($L_e$):** The length of weld upon which the design stress is calculated. It is the actual length provided minus twice the size of the weld (to account for crater formation at start and end). As per IS 800:2007, the effective length shall not be less than $4s$ or 40 mm, whichever is greater.

(c) **Effective area of weld ($A_e$):** The product of effective throat thickness and effective length. $A_e = t_t \times L_e$. For a fillet weld, $A_e = 0.7s \times L_e$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**What are the minimum and maximum sizes of fillet welds as per IS 800:2007? Why are these limits specified?**

**Model Answer:**

**Minimum size ($s_{min}$):** It is governed by the thickness of the thicker plate to ensure adequate heat dissipation during welding. For thicker plate 10–20 mm, $s_{min} = 5$ mm. This prevents the weld from cooling too quickly, which would make it brittle. (Full table as given in Cl. 10.5.3.1)

**Maximum size ($s_{max}$):** For welds along the edge of a plate, $s_{max} = t - 1.5$ mm, where $t$ is the thickness of the thinner plate. This prevents the weld from "blowing out" the edge of the thinner plate and ensures a smooth transition.

**Purpose:** These limits ensure the weld develops adequate strength without damaging the parent material.

---

### Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** An equal-leg angle 100 × 100 × 10 mm is welded to a 12 mm gusset plate and carries an axial load of 400 kN. The connection is on both sides of the gusset. Design the weld using shop welding and E250 electrodes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A butt weld is used to connect two plates of 16 mm thickness, each of width 200 mm. The plate is of Fe 410 grade. Determine the strength of the joint. Take $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$, $f_y = 250$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A (a)

**Given:**
- Equal angle: 100 × 100 × 10 mm
- Gusset plate: 12 mm
- Axial load, $P = 400$ kN
- Shop welding, E250 electrodes ($f_{uw} = 410$ MPa, $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$)

**Step 1: Design strength of weld:**
$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Weld size limits:**
- $s_{min} = 5$ mm (thicker plate 12 mm falls in 10–20 range)
- $s_{max} = 10 - 1.5 = 8.5$ mm
- **Adopt $s = 6$ mm**

**Step 3: Strength per mm length of weld:**
$$f_w = 0.7 s \cdot f_{wd} = 0.7 \times 6 \times 189.4 = 795.6 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 4: Load distribution (equal-leg angle, both sides welded):**
- Heel side: $k_1 = 0.7 \Rightarrow P_1 = 280$ kN
- Toe side: $k_2 = 0.3 \Rightarrow P_2 = 120$ kN

**Step 5: Required effective lengths:**
$$L_1 = \frac{280,000}{795.6} = 352 \text{ mm (heel)}$$
$$L_2 = \frac{120,000}{795.6} = 151 \text{ mm (toe)}$$

**Step 6: Provided lengths (add 2s for craters):**
$$L_{1,actual} = 352 + 12 = 364 \text{ mm} \approx 370 \text{ mm}$$
$$L_{2,actual} = 151 + 12 = 163 \text{ mm} \approx 170 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification:** $L_{1,actual} = 370 > 4s = 24$ mm ✓

**Result:** Provide **6 mm fillet weld, 370 mm on heel side and 170 mm on toe side.**

---

### Solution to Question A (b)

**Given:**
- Plate thickness: 16 mm
- Width: 200 mm
- Fe 410 grade, $f_y = 250$ MPa
- $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$

**For a full penetration butt weld**, the design strength equals the tensile strength of the parent plate:
$$R_{db} = \frac{A_g \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{(16 \times 200) \times 250}{1.10}$$

$$R_{db} = \frac{3200 \times 250}{1.10} = \frac{800,000}{1.10} = 727,272 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{R_{db} \approx 727.3 \text{ kN}}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A bracket connection consists of a 12 mm thick plate welded to the flange of a column using two vertical fillet welds of size 8 mm and length 250 mm. The bracket carries a vertical load of 180 kN at an eccentricity of 200 mm. Check the adequacy of the welds. Use E250 electrodes, shop welding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define tension member. Explain the modes of failure of a tension member as per IS 800:2007. List the partial safety factors used in tension member design. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B (a)

**Given:**
- Weld size, $s = 8$ mm
- Weld length, $L = 250$ mm
- Eccentric load, $P = 180$ kN
- Eccentricity, $e = 200$ mm
- E250, shop welding, $f_{wd} = 189.4$ MPa

**Step 1: Direct shear stress:**
Area of two welds:
$$A_w = 2 \times 0.7 \times 8 \times 250 = 2800 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\tau_1 = \frac{P}{A_w} = \frac{180,000}{2800} = 64.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Bending moment:**
$$M = P \times e = 180 \times 200 = 36,000 \text{ kN·mm} = 36 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3: Section modulus of welds (two parallel welds):**
$$Z_w = \frac{2 \times 0.7 s \cdot L^2}{6} = \frac{2 \times 0.7 \times 8 \times 250^2}{6}$$

$$Z_w = \frac{700,000}{6} = 116,667 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 4: Maximum bending stress:**
$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z_w} = \frac{36 \times 10^6}{116,667} = 308.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Combined stress check (top/bottom fiber):**
$$f = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau_1^2} = \sqrt{308.6^2 + 3 \times 64.3^2}$$

$$f = \sqrt{95,234 + 12,406} = \sqrt{107,640} = 328.1 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Compare with $f_{wd}$:**
$$f = 328.1 \text{ MPa} > f_{wd} = 189.4 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Welds are INADEQUATE.** The combined stress exceeds the design strength. The weld size or length must be increased.

**Step 7: Redesign — try $L = 350$ mm:**
$$A_w = 2 \times 0.7 \times 8 \times 350 = 3920 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$\tau_1 = \frac{180,000}{3920} = 45.9 \text{ MPa}$$
$$Z_w = \frac{2 \times 0.7 \times 8 \times 350^2}{6} = 228,667 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$\sigma_b = \frac{36 \times 10^6}{228,667} = 157.4 \text{ MPa}$$
$$f = \sqrt{157.4^2 + 3 \times 45.9^2} = \sqrt{24,775 + 6,321} = 176.1 \text{ MPa} < 189.4 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

**Adopt:** 8 mm fillet weld, **350 mm length** on each side.

---

### Solution to Question B (b)

**Tension Member – Definition:**
A tension member is a structural element subjected to axial tensile forces. Examples include tie rods, tension chords of trusses, suspenders, and diagonal bracing members.

**Modes of Failure (IS 800:2007, Cl. 6.1):**

1. **Gross section yielding:** Excessive yielding of the gross cross-section under tension.
2. **Rupture of critical section:** Failure at a section where holes reduce the net area, leading to fracture.
3. **Block shear failure:** Shear failure along a block of material around a bolt or weld group, combined with tension on the perpendicular face.

**Design Strength Equations:**

| Failure Mode | Formula | Partial Safety Factor |
|--------------|---------|-----------------------|
| Gross yielding | $T_{dg} = \frac{A_g \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$ | $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ |
| Rupture at critical section | $T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 A_n \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$ | $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ |
| Block shear | $T_{db} = \frac{A_{vg} f_y}{\sqrt{3} \gamma_{m0}} + \frac{0.9 A_{tn} f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$ | Both |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to multiply by **0.7** for fillet welds — students often use the leg size $s$ directly as the throat, which is wrong.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Using **field welding partial safety factor** ($\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$) when the problem states **shop welding** (1.25). Always read the problem statement carefully.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** In **angle member connections**, students often apply the full load $P$ to each weld instead of using the $k_1$ and $k_2$ factors as per Cl. 10.3.5.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Not adding **$2s$** for crater deductions at weld start and end.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Confusing **tension member rupture formula** (0.9 factor) with that of a simple tensile rod. The factor 0.9 accounts for strain hardening and stress distribution across the section.
> 6. **Common Mistake #6:** In **bracket connections**, ignoring the moment component and checking only direct shear. Always use the combined stress formula: $f = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau^2}$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 2**

- ✅ **Fillet weld throat = 0.7s** (for 90° fillet welds)
- ✅ **Butt weld throat = thinner plate thickness** (for full penetration)
- ✅ **Effective length ≥ 4s or 40 mm**, whichever is greater
- ✅ **$s_{max} = t - 1.5$ mm** along the edge of the thinner plate
- ✅ **$f_{wd} = f_{uw}/(\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw})$** — design shear strength of weld
- ✅ **E250 weld electrode → $f_{uw} = 410$ MPa**
- ✅ **Shop welding → $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$; Field welding → $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$**
- ✅ **Strength per mm of fillet weld ≈ 132.6s N/mm** (shop, E250)
- ✅ **Load distribution for equal-leg angle (both sides):** $k_1 = 0.7$ (heel), $k_2 = 0.3$ (toe)
- ✅ **Tension member modes:** Gross yielding, Rupture, Block shear
- ✅ **$\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$, $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$** for tension member design
- ✅ **Add 2s to required length** to get actual weld length
- ✅ **Bracket connection check:** Use combined stress formula $f = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau^2}$
- ✅ **Longitudinal welds vs Transverse welds:** Transverse develop full strength, longitudinal slightly less
- ✅ **Minimum weld size table** (Cl. 10.5.3.1) is critical — memorize for KTU exams
- ✅ **Effective area** = effective throat × effective length
- ✅ **End returns** (return welds) for fillet welds along edges: $L_{return} \geq 2s$
