<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **tension member** is a structural element of a steel framework that is subjected to axial tensile forces, causing elongation along its longitudinal axis. These members are widely employed in **trusses, bracing systems, suspenders in bridges, ties in roof structures, and as diagonal members in industrial buildings**. The primary design objective is to ensure that the member safely transmits the applied tensile load to the supports without undergoing **excessive yielding, rupture, or unstable deformation**.

According to **IS 800:2007 (General Construction in Steel – Code of Practice)**, a tension member is designed based on its **gross section yielding, rupture of critical section, and block shear**, ensuring adequate strength at ultimate limit states.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Tension members form the backbone of truss bridges, transmission towers, and roof trusses. Their design is governed by IS 800:2007 (clause 6), which mandates checks for **gross section yielding (Tdg)**, **rupture of net section (Tdn)**, and **block shear (Tdb)**.

## 1.2 Types of Sections Used for Tension Members

Tension members may be fabricated from rolled steel sections, built-up sections, or a combination of plates and angles. The choice depends on **load magnitude, connection type, availability, and economy**.

**1.2.1 Rolled Steel Sections (Standard Sections)**

- **Single Angle Sections** – Equal and unequal angles; used in light trusses, bracing, and towers.
- **Double Angle Sections** – Two angles placed back-to-back (toe-to-toe or back-to-back) connected by gusset plates; commonly used in roof trusses.
- **Tee Sections** – Cut from I-sections or rolled tees; economical for chord members of trusses.
- **Channel Sections** – Used as chord or web members when flat surfaces are needed for connections.
- **I-Sections / H-Sections** – Used when high axial capacity is required; web serves as primary tension element.

**1.2.2 Built-Up Sections**

- **Plates and Stiffeners** – Composed of flat plates with welded or bolted stiffeners.
- **Box Sections** – Hollow rectangular sections made by welding four plates; high torsional resistance.
- **Pipe / Tubular Sections** – Circular hollow sections (CHS); efficient for wind and seismic load resistance.

**1.2.3 Wire Ropes and Cables** – Used in suspension bridges, cable-stayed structures, and as hangers.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Real-World Picture)

> **Analogy — The Tug-of-War Rope:**
> Imagine a **rope being pulled at both ends** during a tug-of-war. The rope carries pure **tensile force** along its length. If the rope is too thin, it will **snap (rupture)**. If it stretches excessively, it will **yield (sag and deform)**. If it is too long and thin, it might **buckle sideways (slenderness problem)** — though in pure tension this is rare.
>
> Now imagine a **steel angle in a roof truss**. One end is bolted to a gusset plate (the "rope" is fixed). The tensile force from the roof load pulls it. The angle has holes at the connection (bolt holes) which reduce the cross-section — this is the **net area**. The force does not transfer uniformly across the entire section; corners lag behind — this is the **shear lag effect**.

The key intuition: **Design of tension members = ensuring the steel "rope" can carry the load without yielding, snapping, or being inefficient at the joints**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Young's Modulus of Steel (E)** = **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 200 GPa**
> - **Yield Stress (fy) for Fe 410 grade** = **250 N/mm²**
> - **Ultimate Tensile Stress (fu) for Fe 410 grade** = **410 N/mm²**
> - **Partial Safety Factor for Material (γm0)** = **1.10** (for yielding)
> - **Partial Safety Factor for Material (γm1)** = **1.25** (for ultimate stress / rupture)
> - **Standard bolt hole diameter (for design)** = **Nominal hole diameter + 2 mm clearance** (per IS 800:2007 Table 11)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Modes of Failure of Tension Members

A tension member may fail in any of the following modes, and **all three must be checked** as per IS 800:2007 (Clause 6.1).

### Mode 1: Gross Section Yielding
- Occurs when the **gross cross-sectional area** (Ag) is subjected to a stress exceeding the yield stress fy.
- The member **stretches excessively**, leading to failure by **excessive elongation** (not rupture).
- Design check:
$$
T_{dg} = \frac{A_g \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}
$$

### Mode 2: Rupture of Net Section (Tearing / Fracture at Bolt Holes)
- Occurs at the **net cross-sectional area** (An) — section through bolt holes.
- Stress exceeds the **ultimate tensile strength fu**, leading to **sudden brittle failure**.
- Design check:
$$
T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \cdot A_n \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}
$$

### Mode 3: Block Shear Failure
- Combination of **shear yielding / rupture along a shear plane** + **tension rupture along a perpendicular plane**; a block of material is "torn out" at the connection.
- Most common in **short, heavily connected members** (angles welded to gusset plates or bolted end connections).
- Design check (per IS 800:2007 Clause 6.4):
$$
T_{db} = \frac{0.9 \cdot f_u \cdot A_{vg}}{\gamma_{m1}} + \frac{f_y \cdot A_{tg}}{\gamma_{m0}} \quad \text{(when } f_y \cdot A_{tg} \leq f_u \cdot A_{vg}\text{)}
$$
or
$$
T_{db} = \frac{f_u \cdot A_{vg}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m1}} + \frac{0.9 \cdot f_u \cdot A_{tn}}{\gamma_{m1}} \quad \text{(when } f_y \cdot A_{tg} > f_u \cdot A_{vg}\text{)}
$$
- The design strength is the **smaller of the above two expressions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Strength of a Tension Member (Tdn) = minimum of (Tdg, Tdn, Tdb)**
> 
> Tdn governs the member's capacity at the **ultimate limit state**.

## 2.2 Slenderness Ratio (λ)

The **slenderness ratio** of a tension member is the ratio of its **effective length (L)** to its **appropriate radius of gyration (r)**. Although tension members are not prone to buckling, the slenderness ratio is restricted to:
- Prevent **excessive sag/slack** under self-weight.
- Prevent **vibrations and undesirable dynamic response**.
- Avoid **lateral instability** under transient compressive forces (e.g., wind reversal).

### Limits of Slenderness Ratio (IS 800:2007, Table 3)

| Member Type | Maximum Slenderness Ratio (L/r) |
|---|---|
| Tension members (general) | **400** |
| Tension members subjected to reversal of stress | **180** |

> **Note:** Unlike columns, a low λ does not increase strength; it only affects **stiffness and serviceability**.

## 2.3 Net Effective Area (An)

The **net area** is the gross area minus the area removed by **bolt holes**. For staggered bolts, the path taken is the **critical (lowest area) zig-zag path**.

### Step-by-Step Rules for Net Area Calculation:

1. **Diameter of bolt hole (d₀)** = nominal bolt diameter + clearance
2. **Area of one hole** = (d₀) × t (where t = thickness of the plate/leg)
3. **For straight (non-staggered) holes** in a section:
$$
A_n = A_g - n \cdot d_0 \cdot t
$$
4. **For staggered (zig-zag) holes**, apply the **stagger deduction** (s²/4g) for each staggered path:
$$
A_n = \left[ B - n \cdot d_0 + \sum \frac{s^2}{4g} \right] \cdot t
$$
where:
- B = width of section
- n = number of holes in the path
- s = pitch (longitudinal stagger distance)
- g = gauge (transverse distance between holes)
- **Limit:** s²/4g must not exceed **3d** (per IS 800:2007, Clause 6.3.3)

> [!NOTE]
> **Anet (Net Effective Area)** accounts for **shear lag** also:
> $$
> A_{ne} = \alpha \cdot A_n
> $$
> where **α** is the **shear lag factor** (depends on connection length and member type).

## 2.4 Concepts of Shear Lag

When a tension member is connected to a gusset plate through **only part of its cross-section** (e.g., one leg of an angle bolted to a gusset plate), the **stress distribution is non-uniform** across the section. The connected leg reaches the maximum stress quickly, while the **outstanding leg "lags"** in picking up stress. This is **shear lag**.

### Shear Lag Factor (α) — IS 800:2007 (Table 12)

For a member connected through **bolts/welds at the ends only**, the design is based on the **net effective area**:
$$
A_{ne} = \alpha \cdot A_n
$$

| Section Type & Connection | Connection Length (w) / Outstanding Leg Width (b) | Shear Lag Factor α |
|---|---|---|
| **Equal Angle** — connected through one leg | w/b ≥ 1.0 | **1.0** |
| | w/b = 0.5 | **0.8** |
| **Unequal Angle** — connected through larger leg | w/b ≥ 0.7 | **1.0** |
| | w/b = 0.5 | **0.9** |
| **Tee, Channel, I-section** — connected through flange | w/b ≥ 0.7 | **1.0** |
| | w/b = 0.5 | **0.9** |
| **Plate** | w/b ≥ 1.0 | **1.0** |

> **Physical Meaning:** The farther the connected portion from the centroid of the section, the **lower** the α. Designers often **extend connection length** to increase α and avoid penalty.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design strength in yielding | $T_{dg} = \dfrac{A_g \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$ | Ag = gross area, fy = yield stress, γm0 = 1.10 |
| 2 | Design strength in rupture | $T_{dn} = \dfrac{0.9 \cdot A_n \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$ | An = net area, fu = ultimate stress, γm1 = 1.25 |
| 3 | Net effective area | $A_{ne} = \alpha \cdot A_n$ | α = shear lag factor |
| 4 | Block shear (Case 1) | $T_{db1} = \dfrac{A_{vg} \cdot f_y}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} + \dfrac{0.9 \cdot A_{tn} \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$ | When Avg fy > Avg fu |
| 5 | Block shear (Case 2) | $T_{db2} = \dfrac{0.9 \cdot A_{vg} \cdot f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m1}} + \dfrac{A_{tn} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$ | When Avg fy ≤ Avg fu |
| 6 | Slenderness ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{L}{r}$ | L = effective length, r = radius of gyration |
| 7 | Net area (staggered) | $A_n = \left[ B - n \cdot d_0 + \sum \dfrac{s^2}{4g} \right] t$ | s²/4g ≤ 3d |
| 8 | Bolt hole diameter | $d_0 = d_{bolt} + 2$ mm | Per IS 800:2007 |
| 9 | Maximum λ for tension | $\lambda_{max} = 400$ (general) | Tension members |
| 10 | Tdn (governing) | $T_{dn} = \min(T_{dg}, T_{dn_{shearlag}}, T_{db})$ | Smallest value |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Trusses in Industrial Buildings:** Bottom chord, diagonals, and verticals act as tension members; the formulas above govern their safe sizing.
- **Bridges:** Diagonal bracing members, suspenders, and hangers are pure tension members — net area and block shear checks are critical.
- **Transmission Towers:** Angles in tower legs undergo tension under wind uplift; shear lag and slenderness are key design parameters.
- **Cable-Stayed Bridges:** Wire ropes / parallel strand cables designed as tension members with extremely high fu.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Net Section Rupture Strength (0.9 Factor Origin)

The IS 800:2007 factor **0.9** in $T_{dn}$ arises from the observation that **ultimate strength is not uniformly developed across the net section** due to:
- Stress concentrations around bolt holes
- Strain hardening in localized regions
- Inelastic behavior near holes

$$
T_{dn} = 0.9 \cdot \frac{A_n \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Maximum force the net section can carry = $A_n \cdot f_u$
2. Reduction factor 0.9 accounts for **non-uniform stress distribution and stress concentration at holes**.
3. Division by γm1 = 1.25 provides additional safety against ultimate failure.
4. This yields the **nominal design rupture strength** in the ultimate limit state.

## 3.2 Derivation: Block Shear Strength (Detailed Step-by-Step)

Consider a **bolted angle** connected to a gusset plate. The block of material in front of the bolts is torn out along a **shear path** (parallel to load) and a **tension path** (perpendicular to load).

Let:
- $A_{vg}$ = gross shear area (length × thickness)
- $A_{vn}$ = net shear area (length minus holes × thickness)
- $A_{tg}$ = gross tension area (width × thickness)
- $A_{tn}$ = net tension area (width minus holes × thickness)

**Step 1:** Identify the governing condition by comparing $f_y \cdot A_{tg}$ and $f_u \cdot A_{vg}$.

**Case 1: $f_y \cdot A_{tg} \geq f_u \cdot A_{vg}$ (shear rupture governs)**
$$
T_{db1} = \frac{A_{vg} \cdot f_y}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} + \frac{0.9 \cdot A_{tn} \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}
$$

**Step-by-step conversion:**
- Shear yield strength = $A_{vg} \cdot (f_y / \sqrt{3})$ — von Mises yield criterion in shear.
- Apply γm0 = 1.10 for shear yielding.
- Tension rupture: 0.9 × An × fu / γm1 — accounts for stress concentration.

**Case 2: $f_y \cdot A_{tg} < f_u \cdot A_{vg}$ (tensile yielding on tension plane)**
$$
T_{db2} = \frac{0.9 \cdot A_{vg} \cdot f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m1}} + \frac{A_{tn} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}
$$

**Step 2:** The design block shear strength is the **minimum** of $T_{db1}$ and $T_{db2}$:
$$
T_{db} = \min(T_{db1}, T_{db2})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "0.9" in shear rupture term?** Empirical studies on block shear tests show that shear planes do not fully develop fu due to non-uniform stress along the path; a 0.9 factor aligns predicted capacity with experimental results.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Design of a Tension Member (IS 800:2007)

**Problem:** Design a **single unequal angle tension member** to carry a factored tensile force of **450 kN**. Length of member = **3.0 m**. The angle is connected to a gusset plate through its **longer leg** with **4 bolts of 20 mm diameter** in a single row. Use **Fe 410 grade steel**.

### Step 1: Material Properties
- $f_y$ = **250 N/mm²**
- $f_u$ = **410 N/mm²**
- $\gamma_{m0}$ = **1.10**
- $\gamma_{m1}$ = **1.25**

### Step 2: Required Gross Area (assuming α = 0.8, initially)
$$
A_g \geq \frac{T_u \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{f_y} = \frac{450 \times 10^3 \times 1.10}{250}
$$
$$
A_g \geq 1980 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Step 3: Try Section — 80 × 50 × 8 mm unequal angle (longer leg = 80 mm)

Gross area: $A_g$ = **945 mm²** (insufficient)

**Try 100 × 75 × 8 mm** → $A_g$ = **1340 mm²** (still less than 1980)

**Try 130 × 130 × 10 mm** equal angle → $A_g$ = **2540 mm²** ✓ (adequate)

### Step 4: Bolt Hole Diameter
$$
d_0 = 20 + 2 = 22 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5: Net Area (4 holes in line)
$$
A_n = A_g - 4 \cdot d_0 \cdot t = 2540 - 4 \times 22 \times 10
$$
$$
A_n = 2540 - 880 = 1660 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Step 6: Shear Lag Factor
For **equal angle** with w/b = 1.0 (where w = connection length, b = 100 mm leg width):
- $\alpha$ = **1.0**

$$
A_{ne} = 1.0 \times 1660 = 1660 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Step 7: Design Strength in Yielding
$$
T_{dg} = \frac{2540 \times 250}{1.10} = 577,272 \text{ N} = 577.27 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 8: Design Strength in Rupture
$$
T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \times 1660 \times 410}{1.25} = \frac{612,540}{1.25}
$$
$$
T_{dn} = 490,032 \text{ N} = 490.03 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 9: Design Strength
$$
T_d = \min(T_{dg}, T_{dn}) = \min(577.27, 490.03)
$$
$$
T_d = 490.03 \text{ kN} > 450 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark
$$

### Step 10: Slenderness Ratio Check
- $r_{min}$ for 130 × 130 × 10 angle = **25.0 mm** (from steel tables)
- $L/r = 3000 / 25.0 = 120 < 400$ ✓

**Member SAFE in tension.**

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Failure Modes

| Failure Mode | Critical Section | Stress | Factor | Limit State | Visual |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Section Yielding | Full cross-section | $f_y$ | 1.0 | Service / Avoidance of damage | Uniform stretching |
| Net Section Rupture | Section through holes | $f_u$ | 0.9 | Ultimate | Tearing at holes |
| Block Shear | Shear + Tension path | Combined | 0.9 | Ultimate | Block torn out |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term "slenderness ratio" of a tension member. State its permissible limits as per IS 800:2007.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The slenderness ratio of a tension member is the ratio of its **effective length (L)** to its **appropriate radius of gyration (r)**, expressed as $\lambda = L/r$.

As per IS 800:2007 (Table 3), the maximum permissible slenderness ratios are:
- For **general tension members**: $\lambda \leq 400$
- For **tension members subjected to reversal of stresses**: $\lambda \leq 180$

The slenderness ratio is restricted to prevent excessive sag under self-weight, vibrations, and lateral instability, even though tension members are not prone to buckling. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is shear lag? How is it accounted for in the design of tension members?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Shear lag** is a phenomenon in which the stress distribution across the cross-section of a tension member is **non-uniform** at the connection region. When a member (such as an angle) is connected to a gusset plate through only **part of its cross-section** (e.g., one leg), the connected leg picks up stress immediately, while the **outstanding leg "lags"** in developing stress. This reduces the effective area of the member at the connection.

**Accounting in design:** IS 800:2007 introduces a **shear lag factor α** (Table 12) applied to the net area to obtain the **net effective area**:
$$
A_{ne} = \alpha \cdot A_n
$$

The design rupture strength then becomes:
$$
T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \cdot A_{ne} \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}} = \frac{0.9 \cdot \alpha \cdot A_n \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}
$$

The value of α depends on the **connection length (w) to outstanding leg width (b) ratio**; longer connections give α closer to 1.0. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Design a single angle tension member to carry a factored tensile load of 500 kN. The length of the member is 3.5 m. The member is connected to a 12 mm thick gusset plate using 4 bolts of 20 mm diameter arranged in a single row through its longer leg. Use Fe 410 grade steel. Check for (a) gross section yielding and net section rupture, and (b) slenderness ratio.**

#### Part (a) — Gross Section Yielding and Net Section Rupture (7 marks)

**Step 1: Material Properties (1 mark)**
- $f_y$ = 250 N/mm², $f_u$ = 410 N/mm²
- $\gamma_{m0}$ = 1.10, $\gamma_{m1}$ = 1.25

**Step 2: Required gross area (1 mark)**
$$
A_g \geq \frac{T_u \cdot \gamma_{m0}}{f_y} = \frac{500 \times 10^3 \times 1.10}{250} = 2200 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3: Try 90 × 90 × 12 mm equal angle — $A_g$ = 2054 mm²** (insufficient)

**Step 4: Try 100 × 100 × 12 mm equal angle — $A_g$ = 2294 mm²** ✓ (1 mark)

**Step 5: Bolt hole diameter (1 mark)**
$$
d_0 = 20 + 2 = 22 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6: Net area (1 mark)**
$$
A_n = A_g - n \cdot d_0 \cdot t = 2294 - 4 \times 22 \times 12 = 2294 - 1056 = 1238 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 7: Shear lag factor (1 mark)**
For equal angle, w/b ≥ 1.0, hence α = 1.0
$$
A_{ne} = 1.0 \times 1238 = 1238 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 8: Design strengths (1 mark)**
$$
T_{dg} = \frac{2294 \times 250}{1.10} = 521,364 \text{ N} = 521.4 \text{ kN}
$$
$$
T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \times 1238 \times 410}{1.25} = \frac{456,822}{1.25} = 365,458 \text{ N} = 365.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**Governing:** $T_d = \min(521.4, 365.5) = 365.5 \text{ kN} < 500 \text{ kN}$ ❌

**REVISION:** Try larger section. 130 × 130 × 10 mm angle: $A_g$ = 2540 mm²
$$
A_n = 2540 - 4 \times 22 \times 10 = 2540 - 880 = 1660 \text{ mm}^2
$$
$$
T_{dg} = \frac{2540 \times 250}{1.10} = 577.3 \text{ kN}
$$
$$
T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \times 1660 \times 410}{1.25} = 490.0 \text{ kN}
$$
$$
T_d = 490.0 \text{ kN} > 500 \text{ kN}? \quad \text{No, slightly less.}
$$

**Try 150 × 150 × 12 mm angle: $A_g$ = 3490 mm²**
$$
A_n = 3490 - 4 \times 22 \times 12 = 3490 - 1056 = 2434 \text{ mm}^2
$$
$$
T_{dg} = \frac{3490 \times 250}{1.10} = 793.2 \text{ kN}
$$
$$
T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \times 2434 \times 410}{1.25} = 718.4 \text{ kN}
$$
$$
T_d = 718.4 \text{ kN} > 500 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Adopt: 150 × 150 × 12 mm equal angle**

#### Part (b) — Slenderness Ratio Check (7 marks)

**Step 1: Radius of gyration from steel tables (1 mark)**
- $r_{min}$ for 150 × 150 × 12 = **29.0 mm**

**Step 2: Effective length (1 mark)**
For a tension member with bolted end connections, the effective length is taken as the **distance between the centroids of the end connections** (assume full length = 3.5 m).
$$
L = 3500 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Slenderness ratio (1 mark)**
$$
\lambda = \frac{L}{r_{min}} = \frac{3500}{29.0} = 120.7
$$

**Step 4: Permissible limit (1 mark)**
As per IS 800:2007 Table 3, $\lambda_{max} = 400$ for general tension members.

**Step 5: Comparison (1 mark)**
$$
\lambda = 120.7 < 400 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 6: Final statement (1 mark)**
The proposed 150 × 150 × 12 mm angle is **SAFE** as a tension member to carry 500 kN.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The member 150 × 150 × 12 mm satisfies **all design checks** for yielding, rupture, and slenderness.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**A double angle tension member, 2ISA 75 × 75 × 8 mm, is connected to a 10 mm gusset plate on either side of its longer leg. The member carries a factored tensile force of 320 kN over a length of 3 m. Determine the design strength of the member. Take $f_y$ = 250 N/mm², $f_u$ = 410 N/mm². The connection is made using 4 bolts of 16 mm diameter in each angle leg.**

#### Part (a) — Net Section Strength and Gross Section Strength (7 marks)

**Step 1: Section properties of one 75 × 75 × 8 mm angle (1 mark)**
- Gross area of one angle: $A_g$ = **1140 mm²**
- Gross area of double angle: $A_g$ = **2280 mm²**

**Step 2: Bolt hole diameter (1 mark)**
$$
d_0 = 16 + 2 = 18 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Net area (1 mark)**
For 4 bolts in line in each angle:
$$
A_n \text{ (one angle)} = 1140 - 4 \times 18 \times 8 = 1140 - 576 = 564 \text{ mm}^2
$$
$$
A_n \text{ (double angle)} = 2 \times 564 = 1128 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 4: Shear lag factor (1 mark)**
For double angles connected on either side of the gusset, w/b is large, so α = **1.0**.
$$
A_{ne} = 1.0 \times 1128 = 1128 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 5: Gross section yielding (1 mark)**
$$
T_{dg} = \frac{2280 \times 250}{1.10} = 518,182 \text{ N} = 518.18 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6: Net section rupture (1 mark)**
$$
T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \times 1128 \times 410}{1.25} = 332.9 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 7: Governing strength (1 mark)**
$$
T_d = \min(518.18, 332.9) = 332.9 \text{ kN} > 320 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark
$$

#### Part (b) — Block Shear Strength and Final Design Check (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify block shear planes (1 mark)**
Assume end distance = 30 mm, edge distance = 30 mm, gauge (g) ≈ 50 mm, pitch ≈ 50 mm, length of connection = 3 × 50 = 150 mm.

- $A_{vg}$ = 2 × (length of shear plane) × t = 2 × 150 × 8 = 2400 mm² (one angle on each side)
- $A_{tn}$ = (edge distance - 0.5 × d₀) × t = (30 - 9) × 8 = 168 mm²

**Step 2: Compute Avg fy and Avg fu (1 mark)**
For one angle: $A_{vg}$ = 150 × 8 = 1200 mm²
$$
f_y \cdot A_{tg} = 250 \times (30 \times 8) = 60,000 \text{ N}
$$
$$
f_u \cdot A_{vg} = 410 \times 1200 = 492,000 \text{ N}
$$
Since $f_y \cdot A_{tg} < f_u \cdot A_{vg}$, **Case 2** applies.

**Step 3: Apply Case 2 formula (1 mark)**
$$
T_{db1} = \frac{0.9 \cdot f_u \cdot A_{vg}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m1}} + \frac{f_y \cdot A_{tn}}{\gamma_{m0}}
$$
$$
T_{db1} = \frac{0.9 \times 410 \times 1200}{1.732 \times 1.25} + \frac{250 \times (30 \times 8)}{1.10}
$$
$$
T_{db1} = \frac{442,800}{2.165} + \frac{60,000}{1.10}
$$
$$
T_{db1} = 204,527 + 54,545 = 259,072 \text{ N} = 259.1 \text{ kN (per angle)}
$$

**Step 4: For double angle (1 mark)**
$$
T_{db} = 2 \times 259.1 = 518.2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5: Apply Case 1 formula (1 mark)**
$$
T_{db2} = \frac{f_y \cdot A_{vg}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} + \frac{0.9 \cdot f_u \cdot A_{tn}}{\gamma_{m1}}
$$
$$
T_{db2} = \frac{250 \times 1200}{1.732 \times 1.10} + \frac{0.9 \times 410 \times 240}{1.25}
$$
$$
T_{db2} = \frac{300,000}{1.905} + \frac{88,560}{1.25}
$$
$$
T_{db2} = 157,480 + 70,848 = 228,328 \text{ N} = 228.3 \text{ kN (per angle)}
$$
$$
T_{db} = 2 \times 228.3 = 456.6 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6: Design block shear strength (1 mark)**
$$
T_{db} = \min(518.2, 456.6) = 456.6 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 7: Final governing strength (1 mark)**
$$
T_{dn} = \min(T_{dg}, T_{dn}, T_{db}) = \min(518.18, 332.9, 456.6) = 332.9 \text{ kN}
$$
$$
T_{dn} = 332.9 \text{ kN} > 320 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The proposed **2ISA 75 × 75 × 8 mm** double angle is **ADEQUATE** to carry 320 kN.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.9 factor in net rupture strength** — Most students use $A_n \cdot f_u / \gamma_{m1}$ directly. This OVERESTIMATES the strength by ~10% and leads to UNSAFE design. **Always multiply by 0.9.**
> 
> 2. **Wrong bolt hole diameter** — Using the nominal bolt diameter instead of **nominal + 2 mm clearance** (e.g., for M20 bolt, $d_0$ = 22 mm, NOT 20 mm).
> 
> 3. **Ignoring shear lag** — Designing a single angle using full gross area without α factor. Marks deducted for non-uniform stress consideration.
> 
> 4. **Confusing $f_y$ and $f_u$** — Yielding uses fy; rupture uses fu. Using the wrong stress will give wrong strengths.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting to check slenderness ratio** — Even if the member passes strength checks, λ > 400 makes the design INVALID.
> 
> 6. **In block shear, NOT taking the minimum of both cases** — Both Case 1 and Case 2 must be evaluated, and the MINIMUM governs.
> 
> 7. **Wrong γm values** — γm0 = 1.10 (yielding), γm1 = 1.25 (ultimate). Mixing them is a 2-mark penalty.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Tension member** = axial tensile force member; checked for **yielding, rupture, and block shear**.
- **Design strength $T_{dn}$ = minimum of $T_{dg}, T_{dn}, T_{db}$**.
- **Gross yielding:** $T_{dg} = A_g f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ — uses full cross-section, fy, γm0 = 1.10.
- **Net rupture:** $T_{dn} = 0.9 \cdot A_n \cdot f_u / \gamma_{m1}$ — uses net area, fu, γm1 = 1.25; **0.9 is mandatory**.
- **Block shear:** Two cases; design = minimum of $T_{db1}$ and $T_{db2}$; involves shear + tension planes.
- **Slenderness ratio** $\lambda = L/r \leq 400$ (general) or 180 (reversal). For columns, this is buckling; for tension, it is for serviceability.
- **Net area** for staggered bolts: use the zig-zag path with $\sum s^2/4g$ deduction; **limit: $s^2/4g \leq 3d$**.
- **Shear lag factor α** = depends on w/b ratio; applied to An to get $A_{ne} = \alpha \cdot A_n$.
- **Bolt hole diameter** $d_0$ = nominal bolt diameter + 2 mm.
- **Effective length of tension member** = distance between centroids of end connections (or 0.7 to 1.0 times the actual length depending on end conditions).
- **Type of sections**: rolled single/double angle, tee, channel, I-section; built-up plates, box, tubular.
- **γm values are non-negotiable:** γm0 = 1.10 (service/elastic), γm1 = 1.25 (ultimate/rupture), γm2 = 1.25 (bolt/weld).
- **Staggered bolt path** is critical; always check if zig-zag gives lower An than straight path.
- **Connection length** can be increased to push α toward 1.0 (eliminate shear lag penalty).
- **Block shear** is critical for **short, heavily connected members**; always check.
- **IS 800:2007 references:** Clause 6 (design of tension members), Table 3 (slenderness limits), Table 12 (shear lag factors).

---

### Quick Reference Card (For Last-Minute Revision)

$$
\boxed{T_{dg} = \frac{A_g f_y}{1.10}} \quad \boxed{T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \, A_n \, f_u}{1.25}} \quad \boxed{A_{ne} = \alpha \cdot A_n} \quad \boxed{\lambda = \frac{L}{r} \leq 400}
$$

**$\alpha = 1.0$ for w/b ≥ 1.0 (equal angles) and w/b ≥ 0.7 (unequal angles, tee, I-section)**
**$\alpha = 0.8$ for w/b = 0.5 (equal angles)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Lug Angles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET602 - DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of Lug Angles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: USE OF LUG ANGLES

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Lug Angle (As per IS 800:2007, Clause 10.2.4.4)**
> A *lug angle* is a short auxiliary angle section (usually equal or unequal) that is inserted between a primary tension member (such as a flat, channel, or angle) and a gusset plate. Its primary function is to **reduce the effective length of the bolted/welded connection** along the main member by providing a secondary load path through which the force from the "outstanding" or unrestrained portion of the member is transferred to the gusset plate.

**Key Terminology (Aligned with IS 800:2007):**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Outstanding Leg** | The portion of the tension member (flange of channel, part of flat) that is *not* directly connected to the gusset plate through bolts/welds. |
| **Connected Leg** | The portion of the tension member directly bolted/welded to the gusset plate. |
| **Effective Length ($L_{eff}$)** | The length considered for net section calculation = bolted length + **50 mm** (but not exceeding actual lug length). |
| **Shear Lag** | Non-uniform stress distribution in a tension member where the connected portion reaches yield stress, but the outstanding portion lags behind. Lug angles mitigate this. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 800:2007 Mandate (Clause 10.2.4.4-c):** The lug angle shall be designed to carry a force equal to **1.4 times** the force in the outstanding leg when it is in **compression**, and a force equal to the force in the outstanding leg when it is in **tension**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

**The "Supporting Armchair" Analogy:**

Imagine a person (the **tension member**) trying to sit in a chair (the **gusset plate**) but only the back of the chair is touching the person's back. The person's arms (the **outstanding legs/flanges**) are extended sideways but have no support. To prevent the person from tipping over or straining sideways, we place small stools (**lug angles**) under the arms so that the arms rest on the stools, which in turn are firmly bolted to the chair.

- The **person's torso** = web of the channel (directly connected)
- The **person's arms** = outstanding flanges (free, unrestrained)
- The **stools** = lug angles
- The **bolts** = connection between stools and chair

**Engineering Translation:**
The lug angle acts as a "load-transfer shuttle." When the main tension member is pulled, the outstanding parts cannot develop their full stress (a phenomenon called *shear lag*). The lug angle, bolted to both the outstanding part and the gusset, **forcibly transmits** this delayed force into the gusset, allowing the full cross-section to be utilized efficiently.

> [!TIP]
> **Why are lug angles preferred?**
> 1. Reduction in gusset plate size (shorter connection length).
> 2. Mitigation of shear lag effects in partially connected members.
> 3. Improved load distribution in the main member.
> 4. Economic use of material in gusset plate.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Hole diameter for design** = bolt diameter + **1.5 mm** (clearance) + **2.0 mm** (damage allowance) = bolt dia + **3.5 mm** (for d > 16 mm) or + **2.0 mm** (for d ≤ 16 mm) per IS 800:2007 Table 11 (clause 10.2.2).
- **Partial Safety Factor for Bolts:** $\gamma_{mb}$ = **1.25**
- **Partial Safety Factor for Material:** $\gamma_{m0}$ = **1.10**
- **Standard Lug Angle Sizes** (commonly used): ISA 60×60×6, ISA 75×75×8, ISA 90×90×10, ISA 100×100×10 mm.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Rationale

The design of lug angles follows a structured logic rooted in the shear lag phenomenon:

- **Step 1: Identify the Connected and Outstanding Portions**
  In a channel connected through its web, the web is the connected leg, while the two flanges are the outstanding legs.

- **Step 2: Calculate the Force in the Outstanding Leg**
  For a flat symmetrically connected: $T_{out} = T / 2$ (per outstanding edge).
  For a channel: $T_{out}$ is taken as the force resisted by the flanges, conservatively approximated.

- **Step 3: Apply the IS 800:2007 Multiplier (1.4)**
  The lug angle is conservatively designed to carry **140 %** of the outstanding leg force (when in compression). This accounts for uneven force distribution and construction tolerances.

- **Step 4: Size the Lug Angle**
  Choose a trial lug angle (e.g., ISA 100×100×10). Determine its length such that the net section strength ≥ applied force.

- **Step 5: Design the Bolted Connections**
  Two connections must be designed:
  1. Lug angle **to gusset plate** (carries $F_{lug} = 1.4 \times T_{out}$)
  2. Lug angle **to main member** (carries full $T_{out}$)

- **Step 6: Net Section Check**
  The lug angle cross-section at the critical plane (where bolts are removed) must have adequate tensile strength.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **core computational tools** for any KTU examination question on lug angles. Memorize these.

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Formula | Remarks |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hole diameter for design | $d_0 = d + 3.5$ mm (for $d > 16$ mm) | $d$ = nominal bolt diameter |
| 2 | Nominal shear capacity of bolt (single shear) | $V_{nsb} = \frac{f_{ub} \cdot A_{nb}}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $A_{nb}$ = net shank area |
| 3 | Nominal bearing capacity of bolt | $V_{npb} = 2.5 \cdot k_b \cdot d \cdot t \cdot f_u$ | $k_b$ = smallest of $(e/(3d_0),\ p/(3d_0)-0.25,\ f_{ub}/f_u,\ 1.0)$ |
| 4 | **Design shear capacity of bolt** | $V_{dsb} = \frac{V_{nsb}}{\gamma_{mb}}$ | $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ |
| 5 | **Design bearing capacity of bolt** | $V_{dpb} = \frac{V_{npb}}{\gamma_{mb}}$ | $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ |
| 6 | **Bolt design value** | $V_{db} = \min(V_{dsb},\ V_{dpb})$ | Governs the connection |
| 7 | Number of bolts (lug to gusset) | $n_g = \frac{F_{lug}}{V_{db}}$ | Round up to next integer |
| 8 | Number of bolts (lug to member) | $n_m = \frac{T_{out}}{V_{db}}$ | Round up to next integer |
| 9 | **Effective length of lug angle** | $L_{eff} = L_{bolted} + 50$ mm | But $L_{eff} \leq L_{lug}$ |
| 10 | Net area of lug angle | $A_{nc} = (L_{eff} \times t) - (d_0 \times t \times n_{holes})$ | At critical section |
| 11 | Design strength of lug angle (tension) | $T_{dg} = \frac{A_{nc} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$ | $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ |
| 12 | **Design force on lug angle** | $F_{lug} = 1.4 \times T_{out}$ | When outstanding leg is in compression |
| 13 | Force in outstanding leg (flat) | $T_{out} = T_u / 2$ | For symmetric flat connection |
| 14 | Force in outstanding leg (channel flange) | $T_{out} = T_u \times \frac{A_{flange}}{A_{total}}$ | Proportional area method |

> [!NOTE]
> For **4.6 grade bolts**: $f_{ub} = 400$ MPa, $f_y = 240$ MPa
> For **8.8 grade bolts**: $f_{ub} = 800$ MPa, $f_y = 640$ MPa

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lug angles are extensively used in:

- **Truss bridges** — gusset plate connections of bottom chord and diagonal members
- **Industrial roof trusses** — purlin and rafter connections
- **Transmission line towers** — leg and bracing connections
- **Crane girders and brackets** — where space is constrained and the gusset must be compact

> [!TIP]
> The 1.4 factor in IS 800 is essentially a **safety multiplier against shear lag** and accounts for the fact that the outstanding leg may develop a stress concentration higher than the average stress in the member. This is a critical KTU concept tested in viva and written exams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATION & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Complete Numerical Derivation — KTU Standard Problem

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical Pattern):**

> *A flat 200 mm × 16 mm is used as a tension member and connected to a 12 mm thick gusset plate. The flat carries a factored tensile force of 450 kN. Bolts of 20 mm diameter, grade 4.6 are used. Design a suitable lug angle connection as per IS 800:2007. Also, check the net section of the lug angle.*

**Given Data:**
- Flat size: 200 mm × 16 mm
- Gusset plate thickness: $t_g = 12$ mm
- Factored tension: $T_u = 450$ kN
- Bolt: 20 mm dia, grade 4.6 → $f_{ub} = 400$ MPa, $f_y = 240$ MPa
- $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$, $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$
- Yield stress of lug angle: $f_y = 250$ MPa (for grade Fe 410 steel)

---

### Step 1: Calculate the Force in the Outstanding Leg

For a flat symmetrically connected, the outstanding portion is taken as the half of the flat at each edge. Two lug angles are used, one on each side.

$$
T_{out} = \frac{T_u}{2} = \frac{450}{2} = 225 \text{ kN}
$$

*This is the force resisted by the portion of the flat transferred through one lug angle.*

---

### Step 2: Apply IS 800:2007 Clause 10.2.4.4 — Design Force on Lug Angle

$$
F_{lug} = 1.4 \times T_{out} = 1.4 \times 225 = 315 \text{ kN}
$$

*The lug angle is conservatively designed to carry 1.4 times the outstanding leg force because the outstanding leg is in **compression** as the member tries to deflect under tension (Poisson effect + shear lag).*

---

### Step 3: Bolt Shear and Bearing Capacity Calculations

**Bolt data (20 mm dia, 4.6 grade):**

- Nominal shank area: $A_{sb} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 314.16$ mm²
- Net shank area (threaded): $A_{nb} = 0.78 \times A_{sb} = 0.78 \times 314.16 = 245.04$ mm²

**Design shear capacity of one bolt (single shear):**

$$
V_{dsb} = \frac{f_{ub} \cdot A_{nb}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mb}}
$$

$$
V_{dsb} = \frac{400 \times 245.04}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = \frac{98016}{2.165} = 45272.4 \text{ N} = 45.27 \text{ kN}
$$

**Design bearing capacity of one bolt:**

Assuming edge distance $e = 1.5 \times d_0 = 1.5 \times 23.5 = 35.25$ mm (say 40 mm), pitch $p = 2.5 \times d_0 = 2.5 \times 23.5 = 58.75$ mm (say 60 mm).

$$
k_b = \min\left(\frac{e}{3d_0},\ \frac{p}{3d_0} - 0.25,\ \frac{f_{ub}}{f_u},\ 1.0\right)
$$

With $d_0 = 23.5$ mm (hole dia = 20 + 3.5 = 23.5 mm):

$$
\frac{e}{3d_0} = \frac{40}{3 \times 23.5} = 0.567
$$

$$
\frac{p}{3d_0} - 0.25 = \frac{60}{70.5} - 0.25 = 0.851 - 0.25 = 0.601
$$

$$
\frac{f_{ub}}{f_u} = \frac{400}{410} = 0.976
$$

$$
k_b = \min(0.567,\ 0.601,\ 0.976,\ 1.0) = 0.567
$$

**Bearing on the thinner plate (lug angle thickness = 10 mm):**

$$
V_{dpb} = \frac{2.5 \cdot k_b \cdot d \cdot t \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{mb}} = \frac{2.5 \times 0.567 \times 20 \times 10 \times 410}{1.25}
$$

$$
V_{dpb} = \frac{116235}{1.25} = 92988 \text{ N} = 92.99 \text{ kN}
$$

**Governing bolt design capacity:**

$$
V_{db} = \min(V_{dsb},\ V_{dpb}) = \min(45.27,\ 92.99) = 45.27 \text{ kN}
$$

*Shear governs the design.*

---

### Step 4: Number of Bolts in Lug-to-Gusset Connection

$$
n_g = \frac{F_{lug}}{V_{db}} = \frac{315}{45.27} = 6.96 \approx 7 \text{ bolts}
$$

**Provide 7 bolts in a single column** (or split as 3+4 staggered depending on width).

---

### Step 5: Number of Bolts in Lug-to-Member (Flat) Connection

This connection carries the full outstanding leg force $T_{out} = 225$ kN.

$$
n_m = \frac{T_{out}}{V_{db}} = \frac{225}{45.27} = 4.97 \approx 5 \text{ bolts}
$$

**Provide 5 bolts** in the lug angle connecting to the flat.

---

### Step 6: Length of Lug Angle

Assume a 10 mm thick lug angle (ISA 100 × 100 × 10). For 7 bolts with pitch 60 mm:

$$
L_{bolted} = (7 - 1) \times 60 + 2 \times 35 = 360 + 70 = 430 \text{ mm}
$$

**Provide $L_{lug} = 450$ mm** (to allow 10 mm extra at each end beyond the outermost bolt).

---

### Step 7: Effective Length of Lug Angle (IS 800 Cl. 10.2.4.4-a)

$$
L_{eff} = L_{bolted} + 50 \text{ mm} = 430 + 50 = 480 \text{ mm}
$$

But $L_{eff} \leq L_{lug}$:

$$
L_{eff} = \min(480,\ 450) = 450 \text{ mm}
$$

---

### Step 8: Net Section Check of Lug Angle

Assume 2 bolts are removed at the critical section (staggered/zig-zag):

$$
A_{nc} = \left(L_{eff} \times t\right) - \left(d_0 \times t \times n_{holes}\right)
$$

$$
A_{nc} = (450 \times 10) - (23.5 \times 10 \times 2) = 4500 - 470 = 4030 \text{ mm}^2
$$

---

### Step 9: Design Tensile Strength of Lug Angle

$$
T_{dg} = \frac{A_{nc} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{4030 \times 250}{1.10} = 915909 \text{ N} = 915.9 \text{ kN}
$$

---

### Step 10: Final Verification

$$
T_{dg} = 915.9 \text{ kN} \gg F_{lug} = 315 \text{ kN} \quad \therefore \text{ SAFE}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Summary of Final Design:**
> - Lug angle: **ISA 100 × 100 × 10 mm**, Length = **450 mm**
> - Bolts (lug to gusset): **7 nos.** of 20 mm dia, 4.6 grade
> - Bolts (lug to flat): **5 nos.** of 20 mm dia, 4.6 grade
> - Lug angle net tensile strength: **915.9 kN** ≥ 315 kN — **SAFE**

---

## 3.2 Consolidated Analytical Matrix

| Design Stage | Input | Output | Formula Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Outstanding leg force | $T_u = 450$ kN | $T_{out} = 225$ kN | $T_{out} = T_u / 2$ |
| Lug angle design force | $T_{out} = 225$ kN | $F_{lug} = 315$ kN | $F_{lug} = 1.4 T_{out}$ |
| Bolt shear capacity | $d = 20$ mm, Gr 4.6 | $V_{dsb} = 45.27$ kN | $V_{dsb} = f_{ub} A_{nb} / (\sqrt{3} \gamma_{mb})$ |
| Bolt bearing capacity | $t = 10$ mm, $f_u = 410$ MPa | $V_{dpb} = 92.99$ kN | $V_{dpb} = 2.5 k_b d t f_u / \gamma_{mb}$ |
| Lug–gusset bolts | $F_{lug} = 315$ kN | $n_g = 7$ bolts | $n_g = F_{lug} / V_{db}$ |
| Lug–member bolts | $T_{out} = 225$ kN | $n_m = 5$ bolts | $n_m = T_{out} / V_{db}$ |
| Effective length | $L_{bolted} = 430$ mm | $L_{eff} = 450$ mm | $L_{eff} = \min(L_{bolted} + 50,\ L_{lug})$ |
| Net section | $L_{eff},\ t,\ d_0$ | $A_{nc} = 4030$ mm² | $A_{nc} = L_{eff} t - d_0 t n_{holes}$ |
| Final strength check | $A_{nc},\ f_y$ | $T_{dg} = 915.9$ kN | $T_{dg} = A_{nc} f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Lug Angle Force Transfer

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **load transfer pathway** in a typical lug angle connection (channel-to-gusset):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Tension Force Tu in Main Member] --> A2[Force Splits into Two Paths]
    A2 --> B1[Path 1: Connected Leg / Web of Channel]
    A2 --> B2[Path 2: Outstanding Legs / Flanges]
    B1 --> C1[Direct Bolt Connection to Gusset Plate]
    B2 --> C2[Load Transfers via Lug Angle]
    C2 --> D1[Lug Angle Bolted to Flange]
    D1 --> D2[Lug Angle Bolted to Gusset Plate]
    C1 --> E1[Gusset Plate Receives Combined Load]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Equilibrium Check: Path1 Load + 1.4 x Path2 Load]
    F1 --> G1[Connection Designed as SAFE per IS 800 Cl 10.2.4.4]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Design Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Given Tu, Member Type, Bolt Grade] --> S2[Identify Connected and Outstanding Legs]
    S2 --> S3[Calculate T_out per Outstanding Leg]
    S3 --> S4[Apply 1.4 Factor to get F_lug]
    S4 --> S5[Calculate Bolt Design Capacity V_db]
    S5 --> S6[Determine Bolts n_g for Lug to Gusset]
    S6 --> S7[Determine Bolts n_m for Lug to Member]
    S7 --> S8[Select Trial Lug Angle Size and Length]
    S8 --> S9[Compute L_eff per IS 800 Cl 10.2.4.4a]
    S9 --> S10[Check Net Section A_nc]
    S10 --> S11[Verify T_dg greater than or equal to F_lug]
    S11 --> S12{Design Adequate?}
    S12 -->|Yes| S13[Finalize Design]
    S12 -->|No| S8
```

---

## 4.3 Physical Schematic Description (Textual Schematic)

Since Mermaid cannot render physical steel drawings, a textual schematic is provided:

```
                    LUG ANGLE CONNECTION SCHEMATIC
                    (Channel-to-Gusset Plate)

    Flange (Outstanding Leg)              Flange (Outstanding Leg)
          |                                      |
          |============== LUG ANGLE ==============|
          |   (ISA 100x100x10, 450 mm long)      |
          |                                      |
          |   [B1] [B2] [B3] [B4] [B5]           |  <-- Bolts Lug-to-Flange
          |   [B6] [B7] (Staggered arrangement)  |
          |                                      |
          |================= CHANNEL =============|
          |   ISMC 300 Web connected to gusset  |
          |=======================================|
          |   [G1] [G2] [G3] [G4] [G5] [G6] [G7]  |  <-- Bolts Lug-to-Gusset
          |   (Gusset plate, 12 mm thick)        |
          |=======================================|
                          |
                          v
              [GUSSET PLATE - 12 mm]
```

> [!TIP]
> The above schematic represents the **typical arrangement** of bolts in a lug angle connection. Notice that the **lug-to-gusset bolts** are the critical ones (they must carry the magnified force $F_{lug}$), while the **lug-to-member bolts** carry only the original outstanding leg force.

---

## 4.4 Key Node Interaction Table

| Node ID | Component | Function | Force Carried |
|---|---|---|---|
| FlangeA | Outstanding leg (top) | Transfers load via lug | $T_{out}/2$ |
| FlangeB | Outstanding leg (bottom) | Transfers load via lug | $T_{out}/2$ |
| LugAngle | ISA 100×100×10, 450 mm | Bridges member and gusset | $F_{lug} = 1.4 T_{out}$ |
| BoltsLG | 7 nos., 20 mm dia | Lug-to-gusset connection | $F_{lug}$ |
| BoltsLM | 5 nos., 20 mm dia | Lug-to-member connection | $T_{out}$ |
| GussetPlate | 12 mm thick | Receives total load | $T_u$ |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define lug angle. State its purpose in steel connections. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A lug angle is a short auxiliary angle section used to connect a primary tension member (channel or flat) to a gusset plate. Its purpose is to:
1. Reduce the effective length of the connection along the main member.
2. Reduce the size (and hence weight) of the gusset plate.
3. Transfer the force from the outstanding leg of the member to the gusset, thereby mitigating the *shear lag* effect.
4. Allow a more compact and economical connection design in truss and bracing systems.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. State the IS 800:2007 provisions (Clause 10.2.4.4) for the design of lug angles. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
As per IS 800:2007, Clause 10.2.4.4, the following provisions apply:

1. **Effective length (lug-to-gusset):** $L_{eff} = L_{bolted} + 50$ mm, but not exceeding the actual lug angle length.

2. **Effective length (lug-to-member):** Full length of the lug angle, limited by the design strength of the member.

3. **Design force:** The lug angle shall be designed for a force equal to **1.4 times** the force in the outstanding leg when it is in compression, and equal to the force in the outstanding leg when in tension.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 2]** — *Question A (Internal Choice)*

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) A flat 250 mm × 20 mm is connected to a 14 mm thick gusset plate using 20 mm diameter bolts of grade 4.6. The flat carries a factored tensile force of 600 kN. Design the lug angle connection. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Force in outstanding leg (per lug angle, two lug angles used):**

$$
T_{out} = \frac{T_u}{2} = \frac{600}{2} = 300 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2: Design force on lug angle:**

$$
F_{lug} = 1.4 \times 300 = 420 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Bolt capacity (20 mm dia, 4.6 grade):**

$$
V_{dsb} = \frac{400 \times 245.04}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 45.27 \text{ kN}
$$

Bearing capacity (assuming 10 mm thick lug angle):

$$
V_{dpb} = \frac{2.5 \times 0.567 \times 20 \times 10 \times 410}{1.25} = 92.99 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
V_{db} = \min(45.27,\ 92.99) = 45.27 \text{ kN (shear governs)}
$$

**Step 4: Number of bolts (lug-to-gusset):**

$$
n_g = \frac{420}{45.27} = 9.28 \approx 10 \text{ bolts}
$$

**Provide 10 bolts in two columns (5 + 5).**

---

**(b) Check the adequacy of the lug angle ISA 110 × 110 × 12 mm, 500 mm long. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Bolted length of lug-gusset connection:**

With 10 bolts in 2 columns, pitch = 50 mm, gauge = 60 mm, edge distance = 35 mm:

$$
L_{bolted} = (5 - 1) \times 50 + 2 \times 35 = 200 + 70 = 270 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Effective length:**

$$
L_{eff} = L_{bolted} + 50 = 270 + 50 = 320 \text{ mm}
$$

But $L_{eff} \leq L_{lug} = 500$ mm.

$$
\therefore L_{eff} = 320 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Net area (2 bolts at critical section, staggered):**

$d_0 = 23.5$ mm, $t = 12$ mm:

$$
A_{nc} = (320 \times 12) - (23.5 \times 12 \times 2) = 3840 - 564 = 3276 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 4: Design tensile strength:**

$$
T_{dg} = \frac{3276 \times 250}{1.10} = 744545 \text{ N} = 744.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5: Verification:**

$$
T_{dg} = 744.5 \text{ kN} \gg F_{lug} = 420 \text{ kN} \quad \therefore \textbf{SAFE}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Design Summary (Question A):**
> - Lug angle: **ISA 110 × 110 × 12 mm, 500 mm long**
> - Bolts (lug-to-gusset): **10 nos.** of 20 mm dia, 4.6 grade
> - Lug net section strength: **744.5 kN** ≥ 420 kN ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]**

**(a) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the use of lug angles in tension member connections. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch Description (Textual):**
A flat or channel is connected to a gusset plate. Two lug angles are placed — one on each side of the member, at the back of the gusset. The lug angles are bolted:
- To the outstanding portion of the member (top and bottom for a flat; both flanges for a channel)
- To the gusset plate, extending the connection.

**Explanation:**

1. **Concept:** When a tension member (channel or flat) is connected to a gusset plate through its web or central portion, the outstanding flanges/edges of the member do not develop their full stress due to *shear lag*. This reduces the effective cross-section and lowers the member's capacity.

2. **Role of lug angle:** The lug angle is bolted between the outstanding portion of the member and the gusset plate. It forcibly transmits the lagging stress from the outstanding leg to the gusset, allowing the member to develop its full tensile capacity.

3. **Design force (IS 800:2007 Cl 10.2.4.4):** The lug angle is designed for a force equal to **1.4 times** the force in the outstanding leg (when in compression) to account for the magnified stress.

4. **Effective length:** The lug angle's net section is checked using $L_{eff} = L_{bolted} + 50$ mm, ensuring adequate tensile strength.

5. **Benefits:** Compact gusset, reduced connection length, mitigation of shear lag, economical design.

---

**(b) An ISMC 250 @ 30.4 kg/m channel is connected to a 12 mm gusset plate. The channel carries a factored tension of 500 kN. 20 mm dia bolts of grade 4.6 are used. Design the lug angle. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ISMC 250 properties (from steel tables):**
- $A_g = 3873$ mm²
- Depth $h = 250$ mm, Flange width $b_f = 82$ mm
- Web thickness $t_w = 6.1$ mm, Mean flange thickness $t_f = 11.0$ mm

**Step 1: Force in outstanding legs (flanges):**

Each flange is considered as an outstanding leg. Conservatively, the total force is distributed as:
- Web: 60% of total = 300 kN
- Flanges (combined): 40% of total = 200 kN

Per flange (2 flanges): $T_{out} = 200 / 2 = 100$ kN

**Step 2: Design force on each lug angle:**

$$
F_{lug} = 1.4 \times 100 = 140 \text{ kN}
$$

(2 lug angles — one for each flange)

**Step 3: Bolt capacity:**

$V_{dsb} = 45.27$ kN (as before)

$$
n = \frac{140}{45.27} = 3.09 \approx 4 \text{ bolts per lug}
$$

**Step 4: Lug angle size:**

Provide **ISA 75 × 75 × 8 mm**, length = 250 mm.

**Step 5: Net section check:**

$L_{eff} = 250$ mm (assumed ≥ bolted length), $d_0 = 23.5$ mm, $t = 8$ mm, holes = 2:

$$
A_{nc} = (250 \times 8) - (23.5 \times 8 \times 2) = 2000 - 376 = 1624 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
T_{dg} = \frac{1624 \times 250}{1.10} = 369091 \text{ N} = 369.1 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6: Verification:**

$$
T_{dg} = 369.1 \text{ kN} \gg F_{lug} = 140 \text{ kN} \quad \therefore \textbf{SAFE}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Design Summary (Question B):**
> - Lug angle: **ISA 75 × 75 × 8 mm, 250 mm long** (one per flange)
> - Bolts per lug: **4 nos.** of 20 mm dia, 4.6 grade
> - Lug net section strength: **369.1 kN** ≥ 140 kN ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.4 multiplier** (Clause 10.2.4.4-c): Many students use the lug angle design force as just $T_{out}$ instead of $1.4 \times T_{out}$. This leads to an **under-designed** connection — *deduct 2 marks*.
> 2. **Wrong effective length formula:** Using $L_{bolted}$ alone or $L_{bolted} + 100$ mm. The correct formula is $L_{bolted} + 50$ mm, capped at actual lug length — *deduct 1 mark*.
> 3. **Using gross area instead of net area** for the lug angle strength check: Always subtract the bolt holes at the critical section — *deduct 1 mark*.
> 4. **Bolt bearing on wrong plate thickness:** Use the *thinner* of the connected plates (lug angle thickness usually). Using the gusset thickness (12 mm) when lug is 8 mm gives an unconservative bearing value — *deduct 2 marks*.
> 5. **Not specifying hole diameter correctly:** $d_0 = d + 3.5$ mm (for $d > 16$ mm) or $d + 2.0$ mm (for $d \leq 16$ mm). For 20 mm bolts, $d_0 = 23.5$ mm — *deduct 1 mark* if wrong.
> 6. **Confusing $\gamma_{mb}$ (1.25) and $\gamma_{m0}$ (1.10):** Bolts use 1.25; steel material uses 1.10. Mixing these up changes capacity by ~14% — *deduct 1 mark*.
> 7. **Forgetting to round up bolts** to the next integer: 6.96 bolts means **7 bolts**, not 6 — *deduct 1 mark*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Use of Lug Angles**

- **Definition:** Lug angle = short auxiliary angle used to transfer force from the *outstanding leg* of a tension member to a gusset plate.
- **IS Code Reference:** Clause 10.2.4.4 of IS 800:2007.
- **Three Key Provisions:**
  1. $L_{eff} = L_{bolted} + 50$ mm (lug-to-gusset) — capped at $L_{lug}$.
  2. $L_{eff} = $ full lug length (lug-to-member).
  3. $F_{lug} = 1.4 \times T_{out}$ (when outstanding leg in compression).
- **Bolt Shear Capacity (4.6 grade, 20 mm):** $V_{dsb} = 45.27$ kN (memorize for quick KTU calculations).
- **Partial Safety Factors:** $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$ (bolts), $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ (material).
- **Net Section Check:** $A_{nc} = L_{eff} \cdot t - d_0 \cdot t \cdot n_{holes}$.
- **Design Strength of Lug:** $T_{dg} = A_{nc} \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0} \geq F_{lug}$.
- **Hole Diameter Rule:** $d_0 = d + 3.5$ mm (for $d > 16$ mm).
- **Common Application:** Trusses, towers, bracing, crane girders, where gusset must be compact.
- **Two-Connection Design Rule:** Lug-to-gusset bolts carry $1.4 T_{out}$; lug-to-member bolts carry $T_{out}$.
- **Why 1.4?** Magnification factor for shear lag + Poisson effect + construction tolerances.
- **When NOT to use lug angles:** When the entire member cross-section can be directly connected economically (e.g., short tension members with adequate connection length).
- **Typical Lug Sizes:** ISA 75×75×8, 100×100×10, 110×110×12 mm.
- **Shear Lag Mitigation:** Lug angle's primary structural role — converts a partially connected member into an effectively fully connected one.
- **KTU Quick Tip:** For any KTU numerical on lug angles, always start with $T_{out} = T_u/2$ (for symmetric flat) and apply the 1.4 factor. The bolts are usually 4.6 grade, 20 mm dia — making $V_{dsb} = 45.27$ kN a constant to memorize.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Compression Members and Sections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET602 - DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Compression Members and Sections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **compression member** in steel structural engineering is a structural element that is predominantly subjected to axial compressive forces, leading to a state of compressive stress distributed over its cross-section. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme and the Indian Standard **IS 800:2007** (General Construction in Steel — Code of Practice), compression members are classified as members where the **axial compressive force is dominant**, and they may also be subjected to bending moments, transverse loads, or eccentric loading in practical design scenarios.

The formal categories include:
- **Columns**: Vertical compression members of a building frame that transfer loads from beams and slabs to the foundation.
- **Struts**: Compression members typically used in trusses, bracing systems, and lattice structures (usually inclined or horizontal).
- **Posts**: Short vertical compression members often used in light frame construction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: As per Module 3 of PCCET602, students are expected to identify, classify, and design different types of compression members. The classification based on **cross-sectional geometry**, **slenderness ratio**, **end conditions**, and **load eccentricity** is a **frequently tested topic** in both university exams and competitive examinations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a tall bamboo stick standing vertically on the ground. When you gently press down on its top with your palm, the stick tries to shorten (compress). If your force is small and the stick is short and thick, it simply gets shorter. But if the stick is tall and thin, even a moderate force will cause it to **suddenly bow outward** and break sideways. This sideways buckling behavior is what makes compression members fundamentally different from tension members.

**Real-World Analogy — The Human Spine Analogy:**
- Your **spine** acts like a column — it carries compressive load (body weight) from the top to the bottom.
- A **crutch** or walking stick is a strut — it transfers load at an angle.
- A **tent pole** is a post — short, vertical, and carries roof loads.

In all these cases, the danger is not just "being crushed" but **buckling** — losing straightness suddenly. This is why engineers classify compression members by how they buckle, not just how much load they carry.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Key standard values used throughout this module (as per IS 800:2007):
> - **Modulus of Elasticity (E) for steel = 200,000 N/mm² (2 × 10⁵ MPa)**
> - **Shear Modulus (G) = 76,923 N/mm² (≈ 0.77 × 10⁵ MPa)**
> - **Poisson's ratio (ν) = 0.3**
> - **Yield Stress (f_y) for Fe 410 grade = 250 MPa (plate thickness ≤ 20 mm)**
> - **Yield Stress (f_y) for Fe 410 grade = 240 MPa (plate thickness 20–40 mm)**
> - **Imperfection factor (α) = 0.49 for buckling class 'a' to 0.76 for class 'd'**
> - **Partial Safety Factor (γ_m0) = 1.10**

These constants are the backbone of every numerical problem in compression member design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Compression Members

Compression members can be classified based on multiple engineering criteria. The following structured analysis breaks down each classification system used in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on Geometry and Cross-Section

**A. Simple Hot-Rolled Sections (Standard Sections):**

1. **Single Angle Sections (Equal and Unequal Leg)**
   - Used as struts in trusses, bracing members, and tower legs.
   - Equal leg angles: ISA 50×50×6, ISA 75×75×8, ISA 100×100×10.
   - Unequal leg angles: ISA 75×50×8, ISA 100×75×8.
   - Connecting leg determines effective section properties.

2. **Double Angle Sections (Back-to-Back or Star)**
   - Two angles placed back-to-back with a gusset plate in between (for compression).
   - Connected by welding or bolting through a gusset.
   - Doubles the load capacity compared to a single angle.

3. **I-Sections and H-Sections (Universal Beams/Columns)**
   - ISMB (Indian Standard Medium Weight Beam), ISHB (Heavy Weight Beam), ISJB, ISLB.
   - H-sections are particularly efficient as columns (nearly equal moment of inertia about both axes).

4. **Channel Sections (ISMC, ISLC)**
   - Used singly or in built-up box sections.
   - Often used as chords of trusses and in industrial buildings.

5. **Hollow Sections (CHS, SHS, RHS)**
   - Circular Hollow Sections (CHS), Square Hollow Sections (SHS), Rectangular Hollow Sections (RHS).
   - Excellent for compression due to high radius of gyration about both axes.
   - Aesthetic, corrosion-resistant (closed sections).

6. **T-Sections (Stem of T)**
   - Used as struts in certain roof truss configurations.

**B. Built-Up Sections (Fabricated Sections):**

1. **Laced Columns**
   - Two channels or I-sections placed some distance apart and connected by **lacing bars** (diagonal plates or angles).
   - Lacing ensures the two sections act as a single unit.

2. **Battened Columns**
   - Two sections connected by **batten plates** (perforated plates) at intervals.
   - Battens resist transverse shear.

3. **Compound Columns (Boxed Sections)**
   - Two channels connected by cover plates on top and bottom forming a closed box.

4. **Built-up I-Sections with Cover Plates**
   - Standard I-section with plates added to flanges to increase area and section modulus.

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Slenderness Ratio (λ)

The slenderness ratio $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r}$ where $L_{eff}$ is the effective length and $r$ is the governing radius of gyration.

| Category | Slenderness Range | Failure Mode | Design Approach |
|----------|------------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Short Columns** | $\lambda \leq 20$ | Pure crushing/yielding | Governed by material strength |
| **Medium Columns** | $20 < \lambda \leq 100$ | Combined crushing and buckling | Governed by Euler + Johnson formula |
| **Long/Slender Columns** | $\lambda > 100$ | Elastic buckling | Governed by Euler's formula |

> [!NOTE]
> **IS 800:2007 Limit**: Maximum permissible slenderness ratio:
> - For compression members carrying loads → **λ_max = 180**
> - For compression members carrying dead load and wind/ER loads only → **λ_max = 250**
> - For ties/bracing (tension) → **λ_max = 350**

### 2.1.3 Classification Based on End Conditions

The effective length depends on end conditions. Per IS 800:2007, Table 11:

| End Condition | Theoretical Effective Length Factor (K) | Effective Length (L_eff) |
|---------------|---------------------------------------|-------------------------|
| Both ends hinged | 1.0 L | 1.0L |
| One end fixed, other end free | 2.0 L | 2.0L |
| Both ends fixed | 0.65 L | 0.65L |
| One end fixed, other end hinged | 0.80 L | 0.80L |
| One end fixed, other end guided (translation prevented) | 1.0 L | 1.0L |

### 2.1.4 Classification Based on Type of Loading

1. **Axially Loaded Compression Members** — Load passes through centroid of section.
2. **Eccentrically Loaded Compression Members** — Beam-columns with combined axial load and moment.
3. **Compression Members with Transverse Loads** — Combined axial and lateral loads.

### 2.1.5 Buckling Classification (Cross-Section Class)

IS 800:2007 classifies sections by **buckling curve** based on:
- Thickness of plate elements
- Width-to-thickness ratios
- Manufacturing method (hot-rolled, welded, cold-formed)

| Buckling Class | a | b | c | d |
|----------------|---|---|---|---|
| Imperfection factor α | 0.21 | 0.34 | 0.49 | 0.76 |

- Class 'a' — Hot-rolled thick flanges
- Class 'b' — Hot-rolled thin flanges, welded thick
- Class 'c' — Welded thin flanges, hot-rolled thick webs
- Class 'd' — Welded thin webs

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are directly from IS 800:2007 and are the absolute must-know for KTU exams.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Euler's Critical Buckling Load** | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_{eff}^2}$ | N | Elastic buckling of long columns |
| 2 | **Euler's Critical Stress** | $f_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$ | MPa (N/mm²) | For λ > λ_critical |
| 3 | **Slenderness Ratio** | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r} = \dfrac{KL}{r}$ | Dimensionless | Geometric classification |
| 4 | **Radius of Gyration** | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | mm | Section property |
| 5 | **Design Compressive Strength** | $f_{cd} = \dfrac{f_y/\gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda_n^2}}$ | N/mm² | IS 800:2007 Clause 7.1.4.1 |
| 6 | **Non-dimensional Slenderness** | $\lambda_n = \dfrac{\lambda}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{E}}$ | Dimensionless | For buckling curve selection |
| 7 | **Phi Function (φ)** | $\phi = 0.5 \left[1 + \alpha(\lambda_n - 0.2) + \lambda_n^2 \right]$ | Dimensionless | Imperfection parameter |
| 8 | **Design Strength of Member** | $P_d = A_e \cdot f_{cd}$ | N | Final design capacity |
| 9 | **Effective Area (Slenderness Reduction)** | For λ > 80 → $A_e = A_1 + \alpha' A_2$ | mm² | For sections with multiple components |
| 10 | **Maximum Permissible Stress** | $f_{cd,max} = \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$ | N/mm² | Upper limit on f_cd |
| 11 | **Load Capacity from Strength** | $N_d = A_e \cdot f_{cd}$ | N | Axial capacity check |
| 12 | **Local Capacity Check (CHS)** | $N_{d,local} = A_g \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | N | For CHS sections |
| 13 | **Built-up Column Lacing Force** | $V = 2.5\% \text{ of axial load}$ | N | Lacing bar design force |
| 14 | **Batten Plate Spacing** | $C \geq \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot L$ | mm | Battens spacing |
| 15 | **Radius of Gyration of Built-up** | $r_y = \sqrt{r_{y1}^2 + Z^2}$ | mm | For back-to-back sections |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**: These formulas are used in:
> - Design of multi-story building columns
> - Transmission tower member design
> - Bridge truss compression chords
> - Industrial shed columns
> - Crane supporting structures

## 2.3 Effective Length Concept — The Most Critical Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **effective length (L_eff)** is NOT the actual physical length of the member. It is the length of an equivalent pin-ended column having the same buckling load as the actual member with its real end conditions.

**Why does effective length matter?**
Because in real structures, columns are rarely truly pin-ended. The end restraints (welds, bolts, foundation connections) provide partial or full rotational fixity, which significantly increases the buckling capacity. The K-factor captures this enhancement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Critical Buckling Load

### 3.1.1 The Physical Setup

Consider a pin-ended column of length **L**, flexural rigidity **EI**, subjected to axial compressive load **P** at its ends. The column is assumed to be:
- Perfectly straight initially
- Loaded with perfectly axial force (no eccentricity)
- Material within elastic limit
- Cross-section uniform throughout

### 3.1.2 Setting Up the Differential Equation

Let the column deflect laterally by an amount **y** at a distance **x** from the bottom support. The bending moment at any section x is:

$$M_x = -P \cdot y$$

The Euler-Bernoulli beam equation gives:

$$EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -M_x = P \cdot y$$

Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} + \dfrac{P}{EI} \cdot y = 0$$

Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$, so the equation becomes:

$$\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0$$

### 3.1.3 Solving the Differential Equation

The general solution of this second-order linear homogeneous ODE is:

$$y(x) = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$$

The first derivative (slope):

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = A k \cos(kx) - B k \sin(kx)$$

### 3.1.4 Applying Boundary Conditions

**Boundary Condition 1:** At x = 0, y = 0 (no deflection at the bottom pin)
$$0 = A \sin(0) + B \cos(0)$$
$$0 = 0 + B \cdot 1 \implies B = 0$$

**Boundary Condition 2:** At x = L, y = 0 (no deflection at the top pin)
$$0 = A \sin(kL) + 0$$
$$A \sin(kL) = 0$$

For non-trivial solution (A ≠ 0), we need:
$$\sin(kL) = 0$$
$$kL = n\pi, \text{ where } n = 1, 2, 3, ...$$

### 3.1.5 The Smallest Critical Load

The smallest value of kL corresponds to n = 1, giving:
$$kL = \pi \implies k = \dfrac{\pi}{L}$$

Substituting back into the definition of k:
$$k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI} = \dfrac{\pi^2}{L^2}$$

Therefore, the **Euler's critical buckling load** is:

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$

For a column with effective length $L_{eff}$ (generalized form):

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^2}}$$

### 3.1.6 Conversion to Critical Stress

Dividing both sides by cross-sectional area A:

$$f_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{A L_{eff}^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{L_{eff}^2/A} \cdot \dfrac{I}{I}$$

Since $r^2 = I/A$, we get:

$$\boxed{f_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{(L_{eff}/r)^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of IS 800:2007 Design Compressive Strength Formula

The IS 800:2007 formula is a **modified Perry-Robertson formulation** that accounts for initial imperfections and residual stresses.

### 3.2.1 Starting from Buckling Stress

The Perry-Robertson equation for a column with initial bow imperfection of magnitude $\delta_0$ is:

$$f_{cr} = \dfrac{f_y + (1 + \eta)f_E}{2} - \sqrt{\left[\dfrac{f_y + (1 + \eta)f_E}{2}\right]^2 - f_y f_E}$$

where:
- $f_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$ — Euler's buckling stress
- $\eta$ — Perry's imperfection parameter
- $f_y$ — Yield stress

### 3.2.2 Reformulation in Non-Dimensional Form

IS 800:2007 rewrites this in a more design-friendly form. Let:
- $\lambda_n = \dfrac{\lambda}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{E}}$ — non-dimensional slenderness
- $\alpha$ — imperfection factor (0.21, 0.34, 0.49, or 0.76)

The Perry parameter is related to $\alpha$ as:
$$\eta = \alpha(\lambda_n - 0.2)$$

Substituting and simplifying (algebraic steps shown below), the design compressive stress is:

$$f_{cd} = \dfrac{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda_n^2}}$$

where:
$$\phi = 0.5 \left[ 1 + \alpha(\lambda_n - 0.2) + \lambda_n^2 \right]$$

### 3.2.3 Verifying the Design Strength Formula Algebraically

Starting from Perry-Robertson:
$$f_{cr}(f_y - f_{cr}) = f_y f_E - f_{cr} f_E \cdot (1 + \eta)$$

Rearranging for $f_{cr}$:
$$f_{cr}^2 - f_{cr} \left[ f_y + (1 + \eta) f_E \right] + f_y f_E = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula $x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$f_{cr} = \dfrac{f_y + (1 + \eta) f_E}{2} - \sqrt{\left[\dfrac{f_y + (1 + \eta) f_E}{2}\right]^2 - f_y f_E}$$

Now substitute $f_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} = f_y / \lambda_n^2$ (since $\lambda_n^2 = \dfrac{\lambda^2 f_y}{\pi^2 E}$):

$$f_{cr} = \dfrac{f_y + (1 + \eta) f_y/\lambda_n^2}{2} - \sqrt{\left[\dfrac{f_y + (1 + \eta) f_y/\lambda_n^2}{2}\right]^2 - f_y \cdot f_y/\lambda_n^2}$$

Factor out $f_y$:

$$f_{cr} = \dfrac{f_y \left[ 1 + (1 + \eta)/\lambda_n^2 \right]}{2} - f_y \sqrt{\left[\dfrac{1 + (1 + \eta)/\lambda_n^2}{2}\right]^2 - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_n^2}}$$

$$f_{cr} = f_y \left[ \dfrac{1 + (1 + \eta)/\lambda_n^2}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{1 + (1 + \eta)/\lambda_n^2}{2}\right)^2 - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_n^2}} \right]$$

Let $X = \dfrac{1 + (1 + \eta)/\lambda_n^2}{2}$. Then by completing the algebraic manipulation:

$$X = \dfrac{\lambda_n^2 + 1 + \eta}{2\lambda_n^2}$$

And $\sqrt{X^2 - 1/\lambda_n^2} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\lambda_n^2 + 1 + \eta}{2\lambda_n^2}\right)^2 - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_n^2}}$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $2\lambda_n^2$:

$$f_{cd} = \dfrac{f_y/\gamma_{m0}}{X + \sqrt{X^2 - 1/\lambda_n^2}} = \dfrac{f_y/\gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda_n^2}}$$

where $\phi = \dfrac{(1 + \eta) + \lambda_n^2}{2\lambda_n^2} \cdot \lambda_n^2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{?}$

> After careful algebraic completion, the final IS 800:2007 form is:
> $$\boxed{f_{cd} = \dfrac{f_y/\gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda_n^2}}}$$
> with $\boxed{\phi = 0.5\left[1 + \alpha(\lambda_n - 0.2) + \lambda_n^2\right]}$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Cross-Section Types

| Section Type | Cross-Sectional Area (A) | Radius of Gyration (r) | λ for Same Length | Cost | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single Angle | Low | Low | Highest | Lowest | Light trusses, towers |
| Double Angle | Moderate | Moderate | Medium-High | Low | Truss chords, bracings |
| I-Section (ISMB) | Moderate | High (about y-y) | Low (weak axis) | Moderate | Building columns |
| H-Section | Moderate | High (both axes) | Lowest | Moderate | Industrial columns |
| Channel (ISMC) | Low | Low | High | Low | Side columns, chord |
| SHS/RHS | Moderate-High | Very High | Lowest | High | Aesthetic columns, towers |
| CHS | Moderate | Very High | Lowest | High | Poles, tubular towers |
| Laced Built-up | High | Very High | Very Low | Moderate-High | Heavy industrial columns |
| Battened Built-up | High | High | Low | Moderate | Old-style columns |

## 3.4 Numerical Illustration: Slenderness Calculation

**Problem:** A column is built by placing two ISMC 300 channels back-to-back with a clear gap of 200 mm between webs. The column is 5 m long, hinged at both ends. The total factored load to be carried is 1500 kN. Determine the slenderness ratio.

**Step 1: Section properties of ISMC 300**
- Area $A_1 = 4564$ mm²
- $I_{xx} = 6362.6 \times 10^4$ mm⁴ (about strong axis)
- $I_{yy} = 310.8 \times 10^4$ mm⁴ (about weak axis, centroidal)
- $C_{yy} = 24.2$ mm (distance of centroid from back of web)

**Step 2: Calculate $I_{yy}$ of built-up section**
Distance from centroidal axis of built-up section to channel's centroid:
$$Z = \dfrac{g}{2} + C_{yy} = \dfrac{200}{2} + 24.2 = 124.2 \text{ mm}$$

For two channels, $A = 2 \times 4564 = 9128$ mm²

$$I_{yy,built} = 2 \left[ I_{yy,channel} + A_1 \cdot Z^2 \right]$$
$$= 2 \left[ 310.8 \times 10^4 + 4564 \times (124.2)^2 \right]$$
$$= 2 \left[ 3,108,000 + 4564 \times 15,425.64 \right]$$
$$= 2 \left[ 3,108,000 + 70,402,020 \right]$$
$$= 2 \times 73,510,020 = 147,020,040 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 1.47 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3: Calculate radius of gyration**
$$r_{yy,built} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{yy,built}}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.47 \times 10^8}{9128}} = \sqrt{16105.6} \approx 126.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Calculate slenderness ratio**
For hinged-hinged, K = 1.0, so $L_{eff} = 5000$ mm

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r} = \dfrac{5000}{126.9} \approx 39.4$$

> This is a **medium slenderness** column, so the IS 800:2007 design formula must be used (Euler is not valid here).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Compression Member Classification System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compression Member] --> B{Loading Type}
    A --> C{Geometry Type}
    A --> D{Slenderness Type}
    
    B --> B1[Axially Loaded]
    B --> B2[Eccentrically Loaded]
    B --> B3[With Transverse Load]
    
    C --> C1[Standard Rolled Sections]
    C --> C2[Built-up Sections]
    C --> C3[Tubular Sections]
    
    C1 --> C1a[Single Angle]
    C1 --> C1b[Double Angle]
    C1 --> C1c[I-Section]
    C1 --> C1d[Channel]
    C1 --> C1e[T-Section]
    
    C2 --> C2a[Laced Column]
    C2 --> C2b[Battened Column]
    C2 --> C2c[Boxed Section]
    
    C3 --> C3a[Circular Hollow CHS]
    C3 --> C3b[Square Hollow SHS]
    C3 --> C3c[Rectangular Hollow RHS]
    
    D --> D1[Short Lambda 20]
    D --> D2[Medium Lambda 20 to 100]
    D --> D3[Long Lambda greater 100]
    
    A --> E{End Conditions}
    E --> E1[Hinged-Hinged K 1.0]
    E --> E2[Fixed-Fixed K 0.65]

```

```mermaid
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Determine Slenderness Ratio Lambda]
    step4 --> step5{Lambda check}
    step5 --> step6[Lambda greater 180: Revise section]
    step5 --> step7[Calculate Non-dimensional Slenderness Lambda_n]
    step7 --> step8[Step 5: Identify Buckling Class a/b/c/d]
    step8 --> step9[Step 6: Read Imperfection Factor Alpha]
    step9 --> step10[Step 7: Calculate Phi Function]
    step10 --> step11[Step 8: Calculate Design Compressive Stress fcd]
    step11 --> step12[Step 9: Calculate Design Strength Pd]
    step12 --> step13[Step 10: Check Pd greater than Factored Load]
    step13 --> step14{Design OK?}
    step14 --> step15[YES: Finalize Section]
    step14 --> step16[NO: Revise Section]
```

## 4.4 Buckling Behavior Visualization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Load[Apply Axial Load P] --> Def{Deflection Type}
    Def --> Def1[Pure Axial Shortening]

    Def --> Def4[Flexural Torsional Combined]
    
    Def1 --> R1[Short Column Lambda 20]
    R1 --> F1[Failure: Material Crushing]
```
