# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define a compression member. List any four standard rolled steel sections used as compression members in practice.**

**Model Answer:**

A compression member is a structural element that primarily carries axial compressive loads, leading to compressive stresses distributed over its cross-section. The primary concern in design is its tendency to **buckle** (lose stability) before reaching its material crushing strength, especially for slender members.

**Four standard rolled steel sections used as compression members:**

1. **Single Angle Sections (ISA)** — Used as struts in roof trusses and bracing systems. Common sizes: ISA 50×50×6, ISA 75×75×8, ISA 100×100×10.

2. **I-Sections (ISMB, ISHB)** — Used as columns in building frames. The H-shape provides nearly equal moments of inertia about both principal axes, making them efficient as columns.

3. **Channel Sections (ISMC, ISLC)** — Used as chord members in trusses, in built-up columns (laced/battened), and as side rails.

4. **Hollow Sections (CHS, SHS, RHS)** — Modern sections used for columns, poles, and aesthetic applications. They offer high radius of gyration about both axes and are aesthetically pleasing.

> [!NOTE]
> Other valid answers: T-sections, double angles, built-up laced/battened sections.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the effective length of a compression member? How does it differ from the actual length?**

**Model Answer:**

The **effective length (L_eff)** of a compression member is the length of an equivalent pin-ended column that has the same buckling load as the actual member with its real end conditions. It is calculated as:

$$L_{eff} = K \cdot L$$

where K is the **effective length factor** (depends on end conditions) and L is the actual unsupported length.

**Difference from actual length:**

| Aspect | Actual Length (L) | Effective Length (L_eff) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Physical length of the column | Length of equivalent pin-ended column |
| Depends on | Geometric layout | End conditions, restraint |
| Value | Always = physical length | Can be more or less than L |
| Used for | Drawing, fabrication | **Buckling calculations** |
| Relationship | $L_{eff} = K \cdot L$ | K varies from 0.65 to 2.0 |

For example, a column of actual length 4 m, fixed at both ends, has $L_{eff} = 0.65 \times 4000 = 2600$ mm, which means it buckles like a pin-ended column of only 2.6 m length. This is because end fixity significantly enhances stability.

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Classify compression members based on (i) cross-section geometry, and (ii) slenderness ratio. Explain each category with neat sketches and suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A built-up column consists of two ISMC 350 channels placed back-to-back with clear spacing of 250 mm between the webs. The column is 6 m long with both ends fixed. Determine the radius of gyration about the weak axis. **(7 marks)**

**Given:** For ISMC 350: $A = 5360$ mm², $I_{xx} = 10400 \times 10^4$ mm⁴, $I_{yy} = 432.6 \times 10^4$ mm⁴, $C_{yy} = 26.2$ mm

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Classification based on Cross-Section Geometry:**

1. **Simple Hot-Rolled Sections:** Sections that are hot-rolled as a single piece in the steel mill. Examples: single angles, double angles, I-sections, channel sections, T-sections, hollow sections. These are economical for moderate loads.

2. **Built-Up Sections:** Sections fabricated by joining two or more standard sections using lacing, battening, or cover plates. Examples: laced columns, battened columns, boxed sections. These are used for heavy loads and to achieve high radii of gyration.

3. **Composite Sections:** Modern sections like concrete-filled tubes, where concrete infill enhances the compressive capacity.

**Classification based on Slenderness Ratio:**

1. **Short Columns (λ ≤ 20):** Failure occurs by pure crushing or yielding of material. Design is governed by material strength. The Euler formula is NOT applicable.

2. **Medium Columns (20 < λ ≤ 100):** Failure occurs by inelastic buckling. IS 800:2007 design formula is used. The behavior is partially plastic and partially elastic.

3. **Long Columns (λ > 100):** Failure occurs by elastic buckling. Euler's formula is applicable with reasonable accuracy. The stress at buckling is below proportional limit.

**Maximum slenderness limits per IS 800:2007:**
- Compression members with dead + imposed loads: λ_max = 180
- Compression members with dead load + wind/ER: λ_max = 250

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Calculate Z (distance from built-up centroid to channel centroid)**

$$Z = \dfrac{g}{2} + C_{yy} = \dfrac{250}{2} + 26.2 = 125 + 26.2 = 151.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Total area of built-up section**

$$A = 2 \times 5360 = 10{,}720 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate $I_{yy}$ of built-up section (about weak axis)**

$$I_{yy} = 2 \left[ I_{yy,channel} + A_1 \cdot Z^2 \right]$$

$$= 2 \left[ 432.6 \times 10^4 + 5360 \times (151.2)^2 \right]$$

Calculate $(151.2)^2 = 22,861.44$ mm²

$$= 2 \left[ 4{,}326{,}000 + 5360 \times 22{,}861.44 \right]$$

$$= 2 \left[ 4{,}326{,}000 + 122{,}536{,}518.4 \right]$$

$$= 2 \times 126{,}862{,}518.4$$

$$= 253{,}725{,}036.8 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 2.537 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 4: Calculate radius of gyration about weak axis**

$$r_{yy} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{yy}}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.537 \times 10^8}{10{,}720}}$$

$$= \sqrt{23{,}667.6} \approx 153.84 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Slenderness ratio (for verification)**

For fixed-fixed ends, K = 0.65, so $L_{eff} = 0.65 \times 6000 = 3900$ mm

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{yy}} = \dfrac{3900}{153.84} \approx 25.35$$

> This is a **medium column**, so the design will be governed by the IS 800:2007 formula.

**Final Answer:** $r_{yy} = 153.84$ mm, $\lambda = 25.35$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of effective length with reference to the end conditions of a column. Provide a table of effective length factors for different end conditions as per IS 800:2007. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive Euler's critical buckling load formula for a pin-ended column. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Concept of Effective Length:**

The effective length of a compression member is the length between successive points of contraflexure of the buckled shape, or equivalently, the length of an equivalent pin-ended column that would buckle under the same load. The actual column's end conditions (restrained or free to rotate) can be mathematically represented as an effective length of a pin-ended column.

**Mathematical representation:** $L_{eff} = K \cdot L$

where K is the **effective length factor** (sometimes called the K-factor or column effective length constant).

**Effective Length Factors per IS 800:2007 (Table 11):**

| Boundary Conditions | Diagram Description | K Factor | Effective Length |
|---|---|---|---|
| Both ends hinged | Rotates freely, no translation | **1.0 L** | 1.0L |
| Both ends fixed | No rotation, no translation | **0.65 L** | 0.65L |
| One end fixed, other end hinged | No rotation at fixed end, free rotation at hinged | **0.80 L** | 0.80L |
| One end fixed, other end free | Free end can translate freely | **2.0 L** | 2.0L |
| One end fixed, other end guided (translation prevented) | Sliding prevented, free rotation | **1.0 L** | 1.0L |

**Practical Significance:**
- The fixed-fixed condition provides the highest buckling resistance (K = 0.65).
- The fixed-free (cantilever) condition is the weakest (K = 2.0).
- Most practical columns fall between K = 0.8 and K = 1.0.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Euler's Critical Buckling Load — Complete Derivation:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The column is initially perfectly straight with no initial curvature.
2. The load is perfectly axial (no eccentricity).
3. The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law (linearly elastic).
4. Self-weight of the column is neglected.
5. The cross-section is uniform throughout the length.
6. Flexural rigidity EI is constant.
7. Shortening due to direct compression is negligible.
8. Buckling occurs in a single plane.

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Consider a pin-ended column AB of length L subjected to axial compressive load P. Let the column deflect laterally with deflection y at distance x from bottom end A.

**Step 2:** Bending moment at section x:
$$M = -P \cdot y$$
(Negative sign indicates the moment causes curvature opposite to assumed deflection direction)

**Step 3:** The differential equation of the deflection curve (Euler-Bernoulli equation):
$$EI \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M = -Py$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging:
$$\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} + \dfrac{P}{EI} y = 0$$

**Step 5:** Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$, so:
$$\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0$$

**Step 6:** The general solution is:
$$y = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$$

**Step 7:** Slope equation:
$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = Ak\cos(kx) - Bk\sin(kx)$$

**Step 8:** Applying **Boundary Condition 1** — At x = 0, y = 0 (no deflection at pin end A):
$$0 = A\sin(0) + B\cos(0) \implies B = 0$$

**Step 9:** Equation simplifies to:
$$y = A\sin(kx)$$

**Step 10:** Applying **Boundary Condition 2** — At x = L, y = 0 (no deflection at pin end B):
$$0 = A\sin(kL)$$

**Step 11:** For non-trivial solution (A ≠ 0):
$$\sin(kL) = 0 \implies kL = n\pi \text{ where } n = 1, 2, 3, ...$$

**Step 12:** For the smallest critical load (n = 1):
$$kL = \pi \implies k = \dfrac{\pi}{L}$$

**Step 13:** Substituting back:
$$k^2 = \dfrac{\pi^2}{L^2} = \dfrac{P}{EI}$$

**Step 14:** Therefore, **Euler's critical load**:
$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$

**Generalized form with effective length**:
$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in M = -Py**: Many students write M = Py, which gives the wrong sign convention. While the final formula is the same, the differential equation setup gets confusing.
> 
> 2. **Boundary condition errors**: Always check that BOTH boundary conditions are applied correctly. Forgetting to set B = 0 before applying the second condition leads to wrong results.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting assumptions**: The derivation is valid only under the stated assumptions. Euler's formula is NOT applicable for short or medium columns. Examiners frequently deduct 2 marks if assumptions are not stated.
> 
> 4. **Using actual length instead of effective length**: In a 14-mark problem, the formula must be in terms of $L_{eff}$, not L, unless explicitly stated as pin-ended.
> 
> 5. **Not mentioning the elastic limit condition**: The formula is valid only when buckling stress $f_{cr} \leq$ proportional limit. Always state this condition.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Compression member** = structural element carrying predominantly axial compressive load. Its failure is usually by **buckling**, not crushing.

- **Euler's critical load** (P_cr = π²EI/L_eff²) is valid **ONLY for long, slender columns** where buckling occurs in the **elastic range**.

- **Effective length (L_eff)** depends on end conditions: K = 1.0 (hinged-hinged), 0.65 (fixed-fixed), 0.80 (fixed-hinged), 2.0 (fixed-free).

- **Slenderness ratio (λ) = L_eff / r** is the most important geometric parameter. λ_max limits: 180 (with imposed load), 250 (with wind only).

- **IS 800:2007 design formula** for ALL classes of columns:
  $$f_{cd} = \dfrac{f_y/\gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda_n^2}}$$
  with $\phi = 0.5[1 + \alpha(\lambda_n - 0.2) + \lambda_n^2]$ and $\lambda_n = \dfrac{\lambda}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{E}}$.

- **Imperfection factor (α)**: 0.21 (class a), 0.34 (class b), 0.49 (class c), 0.76 (class d).

- **Standard sections**: Single angle (struts), double angle (truss chords), I-section (columns), H-section (heavy columns), CHS/SHS/RHS (modern columns).

- **Built-up sections** — Laced columns use diagonal lacing bars (transverse force = 2.5% of axial load); battened columns use batten plates (spacing ≥ 2/3 L).

- **Partial safety factor** γ_m0 = **1.10** (always remember this for KTU 2024 Scheme).

- **f_y for Fe 410**: 250 MPa (t ≤ 20 mm), 240 MPa (20 < t ≤ 40 mm).

- **E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²** — universal steel modulus.

- **Built-up column radius of gyration** (back-to-back): $r_y = \sqrt{r_{y1}^2 + Z^2}$ where $Z = g/2 + C_{yy}$.

- **Upper limit**: $f_{cd} \leq f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ — design strength can NEVER exceed material yield strength.

- **For sections with λ > 80**, the **effective area (A_e)** is reduced based on the St. Venant's principle of shear lag.

- **Knee braces and gusset plates** in built-up columns: detailed design includes checking lacing bars for both axial and shear forces.

- **Lacing bars must be inclined at 40°–70°** to the axis of the member (IS 800:2007 Cl. 7.7.1).

- **Batten plates spacing**: $C \geq \dfrac{2}{3}L$ and must be provided at ends.

> **End of Topic Notes — Module 3, PCCET602, KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Behaviour and Types of Failures - Effective Length - Slenderness Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET602 - DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Behaviour and Types of Failures - Effective Length - Slenderness Ratio ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Behaviour of Steel Columns Under Axial Compression A **steel column** is a vertical structural member whose primary function is to transfer axial compressive loads from beams and slabs down to the foundations. Unlike a short stub or a tension member, the behaviour of a slender column is governed predominantly by its **geometric proportions** and **end support conditions** rather than by the material's crushing strength. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU/IS 800:2007):** > A compression member is a structural element subjected to **predominantly axial compressive force**. Its design is governed by the clauses of **IS 800:2007 (General Construction in Steel — Code of Practice)** and is fundamentally different from a tension member because **buckling**, not yielding, is the governing failure criterion. ### 1.2 The Physical Behaviour — Why Steel Columns "Bend" Before They "Crush" When a perfectly straight, slender steel column is loaded axially, the load path is straightforward. However, the moment the load reaches a critical threshold, the member suddenly **bows out sideways** in a half-wave (or multiple half-waves) shape. This sudden lateral deflection is called **buckling**, and it occurs at a stress much lower than the yield stress of the material. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Metre Ruler Experiment** > Hold a flat plastic ruler vertically on a table with your thumb and forefinger (pinching the top). Press down gently along its axis. The ruler carries some load, then suddenly snaps sideways. You will observe: > - The ruler did **not** break or yield — it **bent** out of plane. > - Reducing the length by half dramatically increases the load it can carry (this is the *effective length* idea). > - Holding the bottom rigidly and the top freely (one end fixed, one end free) makes it buckle much earlier than when both ends are held firmly. > This is *exactly* how a steel column behaves. ### 1.3 Types of Failures in Steel Columns Steel compression members may fail in any of the following modes, depending on their **slenderness**, **cross-section**, and **end conditions**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Catalogue of Failure Modes in Steel Columns:** | # | Failure Mode | Description | Governing Parameter | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Squashing / Crushing** | Yielding or crushing of material in short, stocky columns where buckling is prevented. | Cross-sectional area $A_g$ and yield stress $f_y$. | | 2 | **Flexural Buckling (Euler buckling)** | Sudden lateral deflection about the weaker principal axis in a half-sine wave. | Effective length $KL$, radius of gyration $r$, Euler load $P_{cr}$. | | 3 | **Torsional Buckling** | Rotation of the cross-section about its longitudinal axis; occurs in doubly-symmetric thin sections (e.g., cruciform, thin-walled open sections). | St. Venant torsion constant $I_t$, warping constant $I_w$. | | 4 | **Flexural–Torsional Buckling** | Combined lateral deflection **and** twist; typical of **singly-symmetric** sections (e.g., channel, tee, equal-angle). | Coupled flexural and torsional stiffnesses. | | 5 | **Local Buckling** | Buckling of individual plate elements of the section (web/flange) before overall member buckling. Governed by **b/t** or **d/t** ratios and section classification. | Width-to-thickness ratios; IS 800:2007 Table 2. | | 6 | **Combined Compression + Bending Failure** | When the member carries axial load and bending moments simultaneously (beam-column). | Interaction equation of IS 800:2007 Cl. 9.3. | ### 1.4 What is "Effective Length"? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Effective Length (KL)** is the length of an *equivalent pin-ended column* that has the same buckling load as the actual column with its real end conditions. Mathematically, if the actual unsupported length between end restraints is $L$, the effective length is: $$KL = \text{Effective Length}$$ where $K$ is the **Effective Length Factor** that *amplifies* the actual length based on end restraint conditions. If both ends are pinned, $K = 1.0$ and $KL = L$ (no amplification). If one end is fixed and the other free, $K = 2.0$ and $KL = 2L$ (the column behaves like a *double-length* pin-ended member). ### 1.5 What is "Slenderness Ratio"? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda$)** is the ratio of the effective length of a compression member to the appropriate **radius of gyration** of its cross-section: $$\lambda = \dfrac{KL}{r}$$ where $r = \sqrt{I/A}$ is the radius of gyration about the relevant buckling axis. A **high slenderness ratio** means a slender, flexible member prone to early buckling. A **low slenderness ratio** means a stocky, strong member where material yielding may govern. > [!NOTE] > **Why This Topic Matters in KTU:** > The slenderness ratio is the **single most important non-dimensional parameter** that decides *which* failure mode (crushing, flexural buckling, local buckling) will dominate a given compression member. Without mastering $KL$ and $\lambda$, no column design problem can be attempted. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Euler's Theory of Flexural Buckling — The Foundation Leonhard Euler (1757) developed the classical theory for a pin-ended, initially straight, prismatic, perfectly elastic column under axial load. The governing differential equation is: $$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + P \cdot y = 0$$ Solving this with the boundary conditions $y = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $x = L$ yields the **Euler Critical (Buckling) Load**: $$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$ The corresponding **Euler critical stress** is: $$f_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{(L/r)^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$$ ### 2.2 The Effective Length Factor (K) — End Condition Logic The actual column rarely has both ends pinned. The end conditions affect the *shape* of the buckled mode, which in turn changes the critical load. Euler's equation is generalised by substituting $L \rightarrow KL$: $$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(KL)^2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why K exists (The "How"):** When end conditions become stiffer (more restraint against rotation and translation), the buckled shape's curvature reduces → the column effectively behaves as if it were shorter → critical load rises → $K$ falls below 1.0. Conversely, weak end conditions allow easy sway and rotation → buckled shape curvature rises → critical load falls → $K$ rises above 1.0. ### 2.3 KTU/IS 800:2007 — Theoretical vs. Recommended Values The **theoretical** values (assuming perfectly rigid or perfectly flexible supports) are textbook ideals. The **recommended design values** in IS 800:2002/2007 account for the *realistic* condition that supports are neither perfectly rigid nor perfectly flexible, and that columns in a frame are restrained by beams of finite stiffness. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Table: Effective Length Factor K for Compression Members (IS 800:2007 Cl. 7.2.2 & Theoretical)** | End Condition (Buckling about axis perpendicular to plane of figure) | Theoretical K | Recommended Design K | |---|---|---| | Both ends effectively held in position and restrained against rotation (Fixed–Fixed) | 0.5 | **0.65** | | Both ends effectively held in position but not restrained against rotation (Pinned–Pinned) | 1.0 | **1.0** | | One end held in position and restrained against rotation; other end held in position but free to rotate (Fixed–Pinned) | 0.7 | **0.8** | | One end held in position and restrained; other end not held in position (Fixed–Free, cantilever) | 2.0 | **2.0** | | One end held in position and restrained; other end partially restrained (sway frame, theoretical approx.) | 1.0 | **1.2** (sway) | | One end held in position but free to rotate; other end not held in position (Pinned–Free) | 2.0 | — (treated as cantilever) | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Where these concepts apply:** > - **Building frames:** Interior columns of braced frames use $K \approx 1.0$; columns in unbraced (sway) frames use $K > 1.0$. > - **Industrial trestles, towers, and chimneys:** Use cantilever ($K = 2.0$) or partially restrained values. > - **Bridge piers and crane columns:** Use fixed-fixed or fixed-pinned behaviour. > - **Cross-bracing systems:** Determine slenderness of compression diagonals to compute their buckling capacity. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **📋 Master Formula Table for Module 3 — Column Basics** | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Euler Critical Load | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(KL)^2}$ | N; $E$ in MPa, $I$ in mm⁴, $L$ in mm | | 2 | Euler Critical Stress | $f_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$ | MPa | | 3 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{KL}{r}$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | Radius of Gyration | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | mm | | 5 | Effective Length | $KL$ | mm | | 6 | Minimum Radius of Gyration (for design) | $r_{min} = \min(r_{xx}, r_{yy})$ | Used for $\lambda_{max}$ | | 7 | Maximum Slenderness Ratio (IS 800:2007 Cl. 7.2.6) | $\lambda_{max} \le 180$ (for compression members) | Limit on $\lambda$ | | 8 | Limit for Tension Members | $\lambda_{max} \le 400$ (for ties only) | — | | 9 | Squash Load | $P_d = A_g \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | N; design yield load | | 10 | Design Compressive Strength (IS 800 Cl. 7.1) | $P_d = A_e \cdot f_{cd}$ | Where $f_{cd}$ from column curve | | 11 | Buckling Class a/b/c/d | From IS 800:2007 Table 7.1 | Depends on cross-section type | | 12 | Imperfection Factor $\alpha$ | From IS 800:2007 Table 7.2 | a = 0.21; b = 0.34; c = 0.49; d = 0.76 | | 13 | Non-dimensional Slenderness | $\lambda_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{f_{cr}}} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{E}}$ | Used for Perry-Robertson curve | | 14 | Buckling Stress (Perry-Robertson) | $f_{bd} = \dfrac{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda_0^2}}$ | Where $\phi = 0.5\left[1 + \alpha(\lambda_0 - 0.2) + \lambda_0^2\right]$ | ### 2.6 Buckling Classes (IS 800:2007 — Critical for Column Curve Selection) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Buckling Class** maps the cross-section geometry to an imperfection factor $\alpha$, which is then used to compute the design buckling stress $f_{bd}$ from Perry-Robertson formula. | Buckling Class | $\alpha$ | Typical Sections | |---|---|---| | **a** | 0.21 | Hot-rolled I-sections with $h/b > 1.2$ (flanges machine flame-cut) | | **b** | 0.34 | Hot-rolled I-sections with $h/b \le 1.2$, welded I-sections (flame-cut) | | **c** | 0.49 | Welded I-sections (rolled plates), hollow circular/tubular sections | | **d** | 0.76 | Rolled or welded box sections, angle/tee sections | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Students very commonly misread the buckling class tables. Always confirm $h/b$ ratio of the **given I-section** first before selecting class **a** or **b**. A mix-up causes an error of ~5–15% in $f_{bd}$. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Euler's Critical Load for a Pin-Ended Column **Given:** A perfectly straight, prismatic, initially stress-free column of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, pinned at both ends, loaded axially by a compressive force $P$. **Assumptions:** - Column is perfectly straight (no initial imperfections). - Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic (Hooke's Law valid). - Axial load $P$ acts exactly along the centroidal axis. - Self-weight is neglected. - Buckling deflection $y$ is small (linearised theory). - Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the deflected neutral axis. **Step 1 — Equilibrium of a deflected segment.** Consider the column deflected laterally by a small amount $y(x)$. At any section, the bending moment due to the axial load $P$ acting through an eccentricity $y$ is: $$M(x) = -P \cdot y(x)$$ The negative sign is conventional (sagging vs. hogging convention). **Step 2 — Euler–Bernoulli Beam Equation.** Using the standard beam-column differential equation (curvature $\approx$ second derivative of deflection for small deflections): $$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -M(x) = P \cdot y$$ Rearranging: $$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + P \cdot y = 0$$ Or equivalently: $$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$$ **Step 3 — General Solution.** The general solution of the second-order linear ODE is: $$y(x) = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$$ **Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions (Pinned–Pinned).** For a column pinned at both ends: $y = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $x = L$. - At $x = 0$: $y(0) = B = 0$ - At $x = L$: $y(L) = A \sin(kL) = 0$ The trivial solution is $A = 0$ (no buckling). For non-trivial buckling, we need: $$\sin(kL) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$ The **lowest** critical load corresponds to $n = 1$ (first mode, half-sine wave): $$kL = \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \dfrac{\pi}{L}$$ **Step 5 — Back-substitute to find $P_{cr}$.** Since $k^2 = P/EI$: $$\left(\dfrac{\pi}{L}\right)^2 = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{EI}$$ $$\therefore \boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$ This is the famous **Euler Buckling Load**. **Step 6 — Critical Stress.** Dividing both sides by the cross-sectional area $A$: $$f_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{A L^2}$$ Since $I = A r^2$ (where $r$ is the radius of gyration), we get: $$f_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E \cdot A r^2}{A L^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{(L/r)^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$$ $$\boxed{f_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}}$$ This is the form most often used in KTU derivations. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Generalised Effective Length Formula **Why?** The derivation above assumed a pin-ended column. For other end conditions, the buckled shape changes, and consequently the critical load changes. We seek a single formula valid for *all* end conditions. **Step 1 — Concept of Equivalent Pin-Ended Column.** The effective length method works on the principle of *equivalence of buckled shapes*. A column with non-ideal end conditions buckles in a shape that is geometrically similar to the half-sine wave of a pin-ended column, but with a different *effective* wavelength. For example, a **cantilever column** (fixed at base, free at top) buckles in a **quarter-sine wave**. A quarter-sine wave has twice the wavelength of a half-sine wave's half. Therefore, the cantilever behaves like a pin-ended column of length $2L$, giving: $$P_{cr, \text{cantilever}} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(2L)^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{4L^2}$$ This is one-fourth the strength of a pinned-pinned column of the same length — a dramatic reduction. **Step 2 — General Form.** By the same logic, for *any* end condition: $$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(KL)^2}}$$ where $K$ is the effective length factor that *converts* the actual length $L$ into the equivalent pin-ended length $KL$. **Step 3 — Physical Meaning of K.** The factor $K$ is the **ratio of the effective length to the actual length**. Mathematically: $$K = \dfrac{\text{Equivalent Pin-Ended Length}}{\text{Actual Length}}$$ For each end condition, $K$ is found by solving the ODE with the corresponding boundary conditions (rotational restraints, translational restraints). ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Slenderness Ratio and its Significance **Step 1 — Definition Recap.** $$\lambda = \dfrac{KL}{r} = \dfrac{KL}{\sqrt{I/A}}$$ **Step 2 — Non-Dimensionalisation of Buckling Load.** Substitute into Euler: $$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E A}{\lambda^2}$$ $$\dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = f_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$$ **Step 3 — Practical Range of Slenderness.** - **Very low** $\lambda$ (≤ 10–20): $f_{cr}$ exceeds $f_y$. Member yields before it buckles. Failure is by **crushing/squashing**. - **Intermediate** $\lambda$ (20–80 for hot-rolled sections): Inelastic (non-Euler) buckling. **IS 800:2007 column curves** (Perry-Robertson) govern. - **High** $\lambda$ (> 80, up to 180): Elastic Euler buckling governs. **Step 4 — IS 800:2007 Non-Dimensional Slenderness.** $$\lambda_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{f_{cr}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y \lambda^2}{\pi^2 E}} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{E}}$$ For $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa and $f_y = 250$ MPa: $$\sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{E}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{250}{2 \times 10^5}} = \sqrt{1.25 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.03536$$ $$\lambda_0 = \dfrac{\lambda}{\pi} \times 0.03536 = \dfrac{\lambda}{88.84}$$ So the boundary between elastic and inelastic buckling roughly corresponds to $\lambda_0 \approx 1.0$, i.e., $\lambda \approx 88.84$. **Step 5 — Perry-Robertson Buckling Stress Formula.** IS 800:2007 uses this semi-empirical formula: $$f_{bd} = \dfrac{f_y/\gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda_0^2}}$$ with $$\phi = 0.5\left[1 + \alpha(\lambda_0 - 0.2) + \lambda_0^2\right]$$ where $\alpha$ is the imperfection factor from the buckling class. The design strength is then $P_d = A_e \cdot f_{bd}$. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When Does Each Failure Mode Govern? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Governing Failure Mode Selector — Decision Table for KTU Problems** | Slenderness Range | Buckling Class | Governing Failure | Code Reference | |---|---|---|---| | $\lambda < 0.2 \pi \sqrt{E/f_y}$ (very low) | Any | **Squashing / Yielding** ($P_d = A_g f_y / \gamma_{m0}$) | IS 800 Cl. 7.1.1 | | $0.2 \pi \sqrt{E/f_y} < \lambda < 180$ | a/b/c/d | **Flexural Buckling** ($P_d = A_e f_{bd}$) | IS 800 Cl. 7.1.2 | | Singly-symmetric section (channel/tee/angle) | — | **Flexural–Torsional Buckling** (use $f_{cd}$ from Cl. 7.1.2.1) | IS 800 Cl. 7.1.2.1 | | Plate elements with b/t > limiting | — | **Local Buckling** (slender section, $A_e < A_g$) | IS 800 Cl. 3.7 | | $\lambda > 180$ | — | **NOT permitted** — redesign required | IS 800 Cl. 7.2.6 | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Buckled Shapes for Different End Conditions ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Axial Load P on Column] --> B{End Conditions} B --> C1[Pinned-Pinned] B --> C2[Fixed-Fixed] B --> C3[Fixed-Pinned] B --> C4[Fixed-Free Cantilever] C1 --> D1[Half Sine Wave
