# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Elastic Critical Moment $M_{cr}$

The derivation of $M_{cr}$ proceeds from classical **beam-column theory** combined with **non-uniform torsion theory** (Vlasov's theory of thin-walled open sections).

### 3.1.1 Governing Differential Equations

For a **doubly symmetric I-section** subjected to uniform major-axis bending, the lateral-torsional buckling mode is governed by two coupled differential equations. Let:
- $u(x)$ = lateral displacement of the centroid (along the y-axis)
- $\phi(x)$ = twist angle of the cross-section (about the longitudinal z-axis)
- $M_x$ = applied major-axis bending moment
- $M_y$ = lateral bending moment = $M_x \phi$ (from the displaced compression flange)

**Equation 1 — Lateral Bending (about minor axis):**
$$E I_y \frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} + M_x \phi = 0$$

**Equation 2 — Torsion (with warping restraint):**
$$E I_w \frac{d^4 \phi}{dx^4} - G I_t \frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} + M_x \frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} = 0$$

### 3.1.2 Boundary Conditions for Simply Supported Beam

For a **simply supported beam** with fork supports (lateral displacement and twist prevented at ends, warping permitted):

$$u(0) = u(L) = 0, \quad \phi(0) = \phi(L) = 0, \quad \phi''(0) = \phi''(L) = 0$$

### 3.1.3 Solution by Superposition

Assume sinusoidal buckling modes:
$$u(x) = C_1 \sin\frac{\pi x}{L}, \quad \phi(x) = C_2 \sin\frac{\pi x}{L}$$

Substituting into the governing equations, the second derivatives give:
$$\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} = -\left(\frac{\pi}{L}\right)^2 u, \quad \frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = -\left(\frac{\pi}{L}\right)^2 \phi$$

These expressions, when substituted back and after eliminating $u$, yield the **characteristic equation for $M_{cr}$**:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{cr} \cdot \left(\frac{\pi}{L}\right)^2 &= \sqrt{ \left[ \frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{L^2} \cdot \frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L^2} \right] + \left[ \frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{L^2} \cdot G I_t \right] } \\
M_{cr} &= \frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{L^2} \cdot \sqrt{ \frac{I_w}{I_y} + \frac{L^2 G I_t}{\pi^2 E I_y} }
\end{aligned}$$

This can be rewritten in the standard form:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{cr} &= \frac{\pi}{L_{LT}} \sqrt{E I_y \, G I_t} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L_{LT}^2 \, G I_t}} \\
\text{where } L_{LT} &= \text{effective length for LTB}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation of $M_{cr}$:**
> - The term $\sqrt{E I_y \, G I_t}/L_{LT}$ is the **St. Venant (pure) torsion** contribution.
> - The term under the second square root adds the **warping torsion** contribution.
> - For **short beams**, warping dominates ($I_w$ term large).
> - For **long beams**, St. Venant torsion dominates ($I_t$ term large).

## 3.2 Derivation: The Perry-Robertson Buckling Formula for Beams

The buckling reduction factor $\chi_{LT}$ is derived analogously to the **Perry-Robertson column formula** used in IS 800:2007 for flexural buckling.

### 3.2.1 Conceptual Basis

The Perry-Robertson approach assumes an **initial sinusoidal imperfection** (camber/eccentricity) in the beam. The cross-section yields progressively from the extreme compression fibre as the moment is increased. The moment at which the **maximum compressive stress** equals the yield stress $f_y$ is the **failure moment**.

### 3.2.2 Mathematical Formulation

Define:
- $\lambda_{LT}$ = non-dimensional slenderness ratio
- $\alpha_{LT}$ = imperfection factor (depends on buckling class)
- $\eta_0$ = initial camber amplitude (related to $\alpha_{LT}$)

The non-dimensional slenderness is related to the imperfection via:
$$\lambda_{LT}^2 = \frac{1}{1 - M_{cr}/M_p} \cdot \text{geometrical factor}$$

For beam buckling, this leads to the **quadratic equation**:
$$\chi_{LT}^2 - \chi_{LT}\left[ 1 + \alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2) \right] + \lambda_{LT}^2 = 0$$

The discriminant is:
$$\Delta = \left[ 1 + \alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2) \right]^2 - 4 \lambda_{LT}^2$$

### 3.2.3 Closed-Form Solution

The smaller root (the **physically valid** one) gives:
$$\chi_{LT} = \frac{1 + \alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2) - \sqrt{[1 + \alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2)]^2 - 4\lambda_{LT}^2}}{2}$$

Regrouping the constants as $\phi_{LT}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_{LT} &= \frac{1 + \alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2)}{2} = 0.5\left[1 + \alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2)\right] \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly the form used in **IS 800:2007, Clause 8.2.1.1**, mirroring the column buckling formula with the same Perry-Robertson heritage. The student should recognize the parallel structure: **$\lambda_{LT}$ here is analogous to $\lambda$ in column buckling.**

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Typical 14-mark style):**

A simply supported steel beam of **ISMB 500** spans **6 m** and supports a uniformly distributed load. The compression flange is **NOT laterally restrained** along the span. Check the adequacy for flexure. Given:
- $f_y = 250$ MPa, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $G = 0.7692 \times 10^5$ MPa
- Factored UDL = 60 kN/m
- $\beta_b = 1.0$ (uniform moment equivalent)

**Section Properties of ISMB 500** (from SP:6 / steel tables):
- $h = 500$ mm, $b_f = 180$ mm, $t_f = 17.2$ mm, $t_w = 10.2$ mm
- $Z_p = 2208.55 \times 10^3$ mm³, $Z_e = 1858.4 \times 10^3$ mm³
- $I_y = 6873.6 \times 10^4$ mm⁴, $r_y = 35.2$ mm
- $I_t = 141.5 \times 10^4$ mm⁴, $I_w = 4124 \times 10^9$ mm⁶
- Section Class: Plastic

### Step 1: Factored Design Moment

$$\begin{aligned}
M_u &= \frac{w_u L^2}{8} = \frac{60 \times 6^2}{8} \\
M_u &= \frac{60 \times 36}{8} = 270 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Section Classification Check

Flange check (rolled section, outstand):
$$\lambda_f = \frac{b_f}{2 t_f} = \frac{180}{2 \times 17.2} = 5.23$$

Limit for Class 1 (rolled): $9.4\varepsilon = 9.4$ (for $f_y = 250$).
Since $5.23 < 9.4$ → **Class 1 (Plastic) flange** ✓

Web check:
$$\lambda_w = \frac{h}{t_w} = \frac{500 - 2 \times 17.2}{10.2} = \frac{465.6}{10.2} = 45.65$$

Limit for Class 1 (web): $84\varepsilon = 84$.
Since $45.65 < 84$ → **Class 1 (Plastic) web** ✓

**Section is Plastic.**

### Step 3: Plastic Moment Capacity

$$\begin{aligned}
M_p &= Z_p \cdot f_y = 2208.55 \times 10^3 \times 250 \\
M_p &= 552.14 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 552.14 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Effective Length for LTB

For a simply supported beam (fork supports, free warping) with UDL on the entire span:
$$L_{LT} = 1.0 \times L = 1.0 \times 6000 = 6000 \text{ mm}$$

> Note: Per **IS 800:2007, Table 14**, the effective length for LTB depends on end conditions. For simple supports with no warping restraint: $K = 1.0$.

### Step 5: Elastic Critical Moment $M_{cr}$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{cr} &= \frac{\pi}{L_{LT}} \sqrt{E I_y \cdot G I_t} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L_{LT}^2 G I_t}} \\
\end{aligned}$$

Compute each term:
$$E I_y = 2 \times 10^5 \times 6873.6 \times 10^4 = 1.3747 \times 10^{13} \text{ N·mm²}$$

$$G I_t = 0.7692 \times 10^5 \times 141.5 \times 10^4 = 1.0884 \times 10^{11} \text{ N·mm²}$$

$$\frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L_{LT}^2} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 4124 \times 10^9}{(6000)^2} = \frac{8.139 \times 10^{18}}{3.6 \times 10^7} = 2.261 \times 10^{11}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{E I_y \cdot G I_t} &= \sqrt{1.3747 \times 10^{13} \times 1.0884 \times 10^{11}} \\
&= \sqrt{1.496 \times 10^{24}} = 1.223 \times 10^{12}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{1 + \frac{2.261 \times 10^{11}}{1.0884 \times 10^{11}}} &= \sqrt{1 + 2.077} = \sqrt{3.077} = 1.754
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{cr} &= \frac{\pi}{6000} \times 1.223 \times 10^{12} \times 1.754 \\
M_{cr} &= 5.236 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.223 \times 10^{12} \times 1.754 \\
M_{cr} &= 1.123 \times 10^{9} \text{ N·mm} = 1123 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Non-Dimensional Slenderness

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{LT} &= \sqrt{\frac{\beta_b Z_p f_y}{M_{cr}}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.0 \times 2208.55 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.123 \times 10^9}} \\
\lambda_{LT} &= \sqrt{\frac{5.521 \times 10^8}{1.123 \times 10^9}} = \sqrt{0.4917} = 0.7012
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7: Buckling Class & Imperfection Factor

For **rolled section** with $h/b_f = 500/180 = 2.78 > 2.0$ → **Buckling Class b**.
Imperfection factor $\alpha_{LT} = 0.34$.

### Step 8: Buckling Parameter $\phi_{LT}$

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5 \left[ 1 + \alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2) + \lambda_{LT}^2 \right] \\
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.34(0.7012 - 0.2) + (0.7012)^2 \right] \\
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.34 \times 0.5012 + 0.4917 \right] \\
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.1704 + 0.4917 \right] \\
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5 \times 1.6621 = 0.8311
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 9: Reduction Factor $\chi_{LT}$

$$\begin{aligned}
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{0.8311 + \sqrt{0.8311^2 - 0.7012^2}} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{0.8311 + \sqrt{0.6907 - 0.4917}} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{0.8311 + \sqrt{0.1990}} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{0.8311 + 0.4461} = \frac{1}{1.2772} = 0.783
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 10: Design Bending Strength $M_d$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_d &= \frac{\chi_{LT} \cdot \beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{0.783 \times 1.0 \times 2208.55 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} \\
M_d &= \frac{0.783 \times 5.521 \times 10^8}{1.10} = \frac{4.323 \times 10^8}{1.10} \\
M_d &= 3.930 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 393.0 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 11: Upper Limit Check

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{d,\text{upper}} &= \frac{1.5 Z_e f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{1.5 \times 1858.4 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} \\
M_{d,\text{upper}} &= \frac{6.969 \times 10^8}{1.10} = 6.335 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 633.5 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $M_d = 393.0 < 633.5$ ✓ Upper limit satisfied.

### Step 12: Adequacy Check

Applied factored moment: $M_u = 270$ kN·m
Design strength: $M_d = 393.0$ kN·m

$$M_u = 270 \text{ kN·m} < M_d = 393.0 \text{ kN·m} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion: The ISMB 500 beam is SAFE against Lateral-Torsional Buckling.**

### 3.4 Comparison Table — Supported vs Unsupported Case

| Parameter | Laterally Supported | Laterally Unsupported (This Problem) |
|---|---|---|
| Governing failure | Cross-section yielding | LTB |
| Design strength $M_d$ | $Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0} = 501.9$ kN·m | $\chi_{LT} Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0} = 393.0$ kN·m |
| Strength reduction | None | **21.7%** reduction due to LTB |
| Check required | $M_u \leq M_d$ only | $M_u \leq M_d$ AND $M_d \leq 1.5 Z_e f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Beam Behaviour Schematic — Lateral-Torsional Buckling

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **progressive failure behaviour** of a beam as slenderness increases, mapping out the regime transitions and the corresponding design formula applied at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beam Under Major-Axis Bending] --> B{Section Classification}
    B -- Class 1/2 (Plastic/Compact) --> C[Plastic Moment Mp Available]
    B -- Class 3 (Semi-Compact) --> D[Elastic Yield Moment My Available]
    B -- Class 4 (Slender) --> E[Effective Section Required]
    A --> F{Lateral Support Condition}
    F -- Compression Flange Restrained --> G[Laterally Supported Beam]
    F -- Compression Flange Unrestrained --> H[Laterally Unsupported Beam]
    H --> I{Non-dimensional Slenderness}
    I -- lambda LT less than 0.4 --> J[Full Plastic Moment Mp]
    I -- lambda LT between 0.4 and 1.0 --> K[Inelastic LTB Regime]
    I -- lambda LT greater than 1.0 --> L[Elastic LTB Regime]
    J --> M[Design Strength Md equals Zp fy by gamma m0]
    K --> N[Design Strength Md equals chi LT Zp fy by gamma m0]
    L --> N
    G --> O[Check Mu less than or equal to Md]
    H --> P[Check Mu less than or equal to Md AND Md less than 1.5 Ze fy by gamma m0]
```

## 4.2 Buckling Curve Architecture — IS 800 Approach

The following diagram maps the **four buckling classes** defined in IS 800:2007 to the **Perry-Robertson reduction factor curves** for LTB.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Section h over bf] --> B1{h over bf check}
    B1 -- h over bf less than or equal 2 --> C1[Rolled Class a or b]
    B1 -- h over bf greater than 2 --> D1[Rolled Class b]
    A1 --> E1[Welded h over bf check]
    E1 -- h over bf less than or equal 2 --> F1[Welded Class b]
    E1 -- h over bf greater than 2 --> G1[Welded Class c]
    C1 --> H1[alpha LT equals 0.21]
    D1 --> I1[alpha LT equals 0.34]
    F1 --> I1
    G1 --> J1[alpha LT equals 0.49]
    H1 --> K1[Compute phi LT]
    I1 --> K1
    J1 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[Compute chi LT]
    L1 --> M1[Compute Design Moment Md]
    M1 --> N1[Check Upper Limit 1.5 Ze fy by gamma m0]
```

## 4.3 LTB Physical Mechanism — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vertical Load Applied to Beam] --> B[Major Axis Bending Stress]
    B --> C[Top Flange in Compression]
    C --> D{Lateral Restraint Available}
    D -- Yes --> E[Compression Flange Remains Straight]
    D -- No --> F[Compression Flange Tends to Bow Sideways]
    E --> G[Beam Fails by Yielding]
    F --> H[Lateral Displacement of Flange]
    H --> I[Cross Section Twists]
    I --> J[Lateral Torsional Buckling]
    J --> K[Moment Capacity Reduced]
    G --> L[Full Plastic Moment Mp Achieved]
    K --> M[Reduced Moment chi LT times Mp]
```

## 4.4 Effective Length Coefficients for LTB

| Support Condition at Beam Ends | Moment Distribution | Effective Length $K$ |
|---|---|---|
| Simply supported (fork ends) | Uniform moment | **1.00** |
| Simply supported (fork ends) | Moment at one end = 0 | 0.80 |
| One end fixed, one end free (cantilever) | Uniform moment | **2.00** |
| Both ends fixed | Uniform moment | 0.65 |
| One end fixed, one end simply supported | Uniform moment | 0.80 |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

**Differentiate between laterally supported and laterally unsupported beams. Under what circumstances does IS 800:2007 consider a beam to be laterally supported?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Laterally Supported Beam | Laterally Unsupported Beam |
|---|---|---|
| Compression flange | Restraint against lateral movement | No lateral restraint |
| Failure mode | Cross-section yielding | Lateral-torsional buckling |
| Design moment | $M_d = \beta_b Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | $M_d = \chi_{LT} \beta_b Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ |

**Condition for lateral support (per IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.1.2):**
A beam is laterally supported when $L_{LT} / r_y \leq 250 / \sqrt{f_y}$ for simply supported/continuous beams, and $L_{LT} / r_y \leq 300 / \sqrt{f_y}$ for cantilever beams. The compression flange may also be considered effectively restrained if the slab or decking is connected through shear connectors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distribution of Marks (3 marks):**
> - Definition of laterally supported — 1 mark
> - Definition of laterally unsupported — 1 mark
> - Code condition (slenderness limit) — 1 mark

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]

**What is lateral-torsional buckling? State the expression for elastic critical moment $M_{cr}$ of a doubly symmetric I-section under uniform bending.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Lateral-torsional buckling (LTB):** LTB is a mode of instability in beams where the compression flange, being laterally unrestrained, undergoes simultaneous **lateral displacement** and **twisting** of the cross-section about the longitudinal axis of the beam. The critical moment at which this buckling initiates is the **elastic critical moment** $M_{cr}$.

**Expression for $M_{cr}$ (IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.2.1.1):**
$$M_{cr} = \frac{\pi}{L_{LT}} \sqrt{E I_y \cdot G I_t} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L_{LT}^2 G I_t}}$$

where $E$ = Young's modulus, $G$ = shear modulus, $I_y$ = minor axis second moment of area, $I_t$ = torsion constant, $I_w$ = warping constant, and $L_{LT}$ = effective length for LTB.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distribution of Marks (3 marks):**
> - Definition of LTB — 1.5 marks
> - Expression for $M_{cr}$ with all terms — 1.5 marks

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a)** A laterally unsupported simply supported steel beam of **ISMB 450** spans **8 m** and carries a uniformly distributed load. The compression flange is not restrained. Determine the **design bending strength** of the beam considering lateral-torsional buckling. Assume $f_y = 250$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **section classification** in steel beams as per IS 800:2007. How does the section classification influence the design moment capacity? **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Section Properties of ISMB 450 (from steel tables):**
- $h = 450$ mm, $b_f = 150$ mm, $t_f = 17.4$ mm, $t_w = 9.4$ mm
- $Z_p = 1535.3 \times 10^3$ mm³, $Z_e = 1350.7 \times 10^3$ mm³
- $I_y = 4399.5 \times 10^4$ mm⁴, $r_y = 31.5$ mm
- $I_t = 76.7 \times 10^4$ mm⁴, $I_w = 2320 \times 10^9$ mm⁶
- Section Class: Plastic (verify $\lambda_f = 150/(2\times17.4) = 4.31 < 9.4\varepsilon$, $\lambda_w = (450-2\times17.4)/9.4 = 44.21 < 84\varepsilon$)

**Step 1: Effective Length for LTB**

For simply supported beam, fork supports, uniform moment equivalent:
$$L_{LT} = 1.0 \times 8000 = 8000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Elastic Critical Moment $M_{cr}$**

Compute auxiliary terms:
$$E I_y = 2 \times 10^5 \times 4399.5 \times 10^4 = 8.799 \times 10^{12}$$

$$G I_t = 0.7692 \times 10^5 \times 76.7 \times 10^4 = 5.90 \times 10^{10}$$

$$\frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L_{LT}^2} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 2320 \times 10^9}{8000^2} = \frac{4.580 \times 10^{18}}{6.4 \times 10^7} = 7.156 \times 10^{10}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{E I_y \cdot G I_t} &= \sqrt{8.799 \times 10^{12} \times 5.90 \times 10^{10}} \\
&= \sqrt{5.191 \times 10^{23}} = 7.205 \times 10^{11}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sqrt{1 + \frac{7.156 \times 10^{10}}{5.90 \times 10^{10}}} = \sqrt{1 + 1.213} = \sqrt{2.213} = 1.488$$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{cr} &= \frac{\pi}{8000} \times 7.205 \times 10^{11} \times 1.488 \\
M_{cr} &= 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \times 7.205 \times 10^{11} \times 1.488 \\
M_{cr} &= 4.213 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 421.3 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Non-Dimensional Slenderness $\lambda_{LT}$**

With $\beta_b = 1.0$ (uniform moment):

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{LT} &= \sqrt{\frac{\beta_b Z_p f_y}{M_{cr}}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.0 \times 1535.3 \times 10^3 \times 250}{4.213 \times 10^8}} \\
\lambda_{LT} &= \sqrt{\frac{3.838 \times 10^8}{4.213 \times 10^8}} = \sqrt{0.9110} = 0.9545
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Buckling Class & Imperfection Factor**

$h/b_f = 450/150 = 3.0 > 2.0$ → Rolled section, **Class b**, $\alpha_{LT} = 0.34$.

**Step 5: Buckling Parameter $\phi_{LT}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.34(0.9545 - 0.2) + 0.9545^2 \right] \\
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.2563 + 0.9111 \right] = 0.5 \times 2.1674 = 1.0837
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Reduction Factor $\chi_{LT}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{1.0837 + \sqrt{1.0837^2 - 0.9545^2}} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{1.0837 + \sqrt{1.1744 - 0.9111}} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{1.0837 + \sqrt{0.2633}} = \frac{1}{1.0837 + 0.5131} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{1.5968} = 0.6263
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Design Bending Strength $M_d$**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_d &= \frac{\chi_{LT} \beta_b Z_p f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{0.6263 \times 1.0 \times 1535.3 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} \\
M_d &= \frac{2.404 \times 10^8}{1.10} = 2.185 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 218.5 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8: Upper Limit Check**

$$M_{d,\text{upper}} = \frac{1.5 \times 1350.7 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} = 4.605 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 460.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_d = 218.5 < 460.5$ kN·m ✓

> **Design Bending Strength $M_d = 218.5$ kN·m**

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Section Classification Concept:**

IS 800:2007 classifies rolled and welded steel cross-sections into **four classes** based on the **width-to-thickness ratios (b/t)** of the compression elements, which govern the susceptibility to **local buckling**.

**The Four Classes:**

1. **Class 1 (Plastic):** Sections that can develop a full plastic hinge and sustain large rotation capacities (required for plastic analysis). The cross-section yields fully without local buckling.

2. **Class 2 (Compact):** Sections that can develop the full plastic moment $M_p$ but with **limited** rotation capacity due to onset of local buckling.

3. **Class 3 (Semi-compact):** Sections in which the **extreme fibre in compression** reaches the yield stress $f_y$ before local buckling, but the section cannot reach $M_p$. The design moment is the **elastic moment** $M_y = Z_e f_y$.

4. **Class 4 (Slender):** Sections where local buckling occurs **before** the yield stress is reached anywhere in the cross-section. **Effective width** concept must be used.

**Influence on Design Moment Capacity:**

| Section Class | Design Moment $M_d$ | Rotation Capacity | Use in Design |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class 1 (Plastic) | $\beta_b Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Full | Plastic design (continuous beams) |
| Class 2 (Compact) | $\beta_b Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Limited | Elastic design with $M_p$ |
| Class 3 (Semi-compact) | $Z_e f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | None | Elastic design only |
| Class 4 (Slender) | Effective $Z_e f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | None | Effective section analysis |

**Why this matters:**
- The classification determines whether you can use **$Z_p$** (plastic modulus) or only **$Z_e$** (elastic modulus) in design.
- Plastic design savings (typically 10–15% higher capacity) are only available for Class 1 sections.
- Slender webs (Class 4) require **shear buckling** checks and **web stiffeners** — significantly more complex.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distribution of Marks (7 marks):**
> - Explanation of four classes — 3 marks
> - Influence on $M_d$ formula — 2 marks
> - Practical implications (plastic vs elastic design) — 2 marks

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** Explain the **Perry-Robertson approach** used in IS 800:2007 for the determination of the design bending strength of laterally unsupported beams. Derive the expression for $\chi_{LT}$ in terms of $\phi_{LT}$ and $\lambda_{LT}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported beam of **ISMB 400** with a span of **5 m** is laterally unsupported. It carries a **factored concentrated load of 180 kN** at mid-span. Check the adequacy of the section against lateral-torsional buckling. Given $f_y = 250$ MPa, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $G = 0.7692 \times 10^5$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**The Perry-Robertson Approach — Conceptual Foundation:**

The Perry-Robertson method, originally developed for **column buckling**, models the imperfect member as having an **initial sinusoidal camber** (initial imperfection) of amplitude $\eta_0$. Under increasing load, the cross-section experiences **progressive yielding** from the extreme compression fibre. The **failure moment** is the moment at which the **maximum compressive stress** in the cross-section equals the yield stress $f_y$.

For **lateral-torsional buckling**, the same Perry-Robertson philosophy is applied to a beam in major-axis bending with an initial lateral imperfection (twist and lateral bow of the compression flange).

**Governing Equation Derivation:**

Let the initial imperfection cause a relative eccentricity $\eta_0$ at mid-span. Under the applied moment $M = \chi_{LT} M_p$, the additional moment due to lateral deflection is $M \eta / (1 - M/M_{cr})$, leading to the Perry equation:

$$\chi_{LT} - \chi_{LT}\alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2) + \lambda_{LT}^2 = 0$$

> Note: After algebraic manipulation, the result is the quadratic in $\chi_{LT}$ as derived in Section 3.2.

Define the auxiliary parameter:
$$\phi_{LT} = \frac{1 + \alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2)}{2} = 0.5\left[1 + \alpha_{LT}(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2)\right]$$

The quadratic becomes:
$$\chi_{LT}^2 - 2\phi_{LT}\chi_{LT} + \lambda_{LT}^2 = 0$$

Solving by the quadratic formula (taking the **smaller, physically valid root**):

$$\begin{aligned}
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{2\phi_{LT} - \sqrt{4\phi_{LT}^2 - 4\lambda_{LT}^2}}{2} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \phi_{LT} - \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{(\phi_{LT} - \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2})(\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2})}{\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{\phi_{LT}^2 - (\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2)}{\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{\lambda_{LT}^2}{\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}}
\end{aligned}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2})$ and using the identity $\phi_{LT}^2 - (\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2) = \lambda_{LT}^2$:

$$\boxed{\chi_{LT} = \frac{1}{\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}} \leq 1.0}$$

This is the **IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.2.1.1** expression. The constraint $\chi_{LT} \leq 1.0$ ensures the reduction factor never exceeds unity (i.e., the design strength can never exceed the plastic moment).

> [!NOTE]
> **Distribution of Marks (7 marks):**
> - Perry-Robertson concept — 2 marks
> - Governing equation setup — 2 marks
> - Derivation of $\chi_{LT}$ — 2 marks
> - Final boxed expression — 1 mark

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Section Properties of ISMB 400:**
- $h = 400$ mm, $b_f = 140$ mm, $t_f = 16.0$ mm, $t_w = 8.9$ mm
- $Z_p = 1176.2 \times 10^3$ mm³, $Z_e = 1025.3 \times 10^3$ mm³
- $I_y = 3178.4 \times 10^4$ mm⁴, $r_y = 28.2$ mm
- $I_t = 60.07 \times 10^4$ mm⁴, $I_w = 1410 \times 10^9$ mm⁶
- Section class: Plastic (check omitted for brevity)

**Step 1: Factored Design Moment**

For a simply supported beam with central concentrated load:
$$M_u = \frac{P_u L}{4} = \frac{180 \times 5}{4} = 225 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2: Effective Length & $\beta_b$**

For simply supported, central point load: $L_{LT} = 1.0 \times 5000 = 5000$ mm.
Moment distribution factor for central point load: $\beta_b = 1.0$ (conservative; tabulated value is 1.084).

> **Note:** Using $\beta_b = 1.0$ is the conservative approach commonly used in KTU exams unless otherwise specified.

**Step 3: Elastic Critical Moment $M_{cr}$**

$$E I_y = 2 \times 10^5 \times 3178.4 \times 10^4 = 6.357 \times 10^{12}$$

$$G I_t = 0.7692 \times 10^5 \times 60.07 \times 10^4 = 4.621 \times 10^{10}$$

$$\frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L_{LT}^2} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 1410 \times 10^9}{5000^2} = \frac{2.783 \times 10^{18}}{2.5 \times 10^7} = 1.113 \times 10^{11}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{E I_y \cdot G I_t} &= \sqrt{6.357 \times 10^{12} \times 4.621 \times 10^{10}} = \sqrt{2.938 \times 10^{23}} = 5.420 \times 10^{11}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sqrt{1 + \frac{1.113 \times 10^{11}}{4.621 \times 10^{10}}} = \sqrt{1 + 2.409} = \sqrt{3.409} = 1.846$$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{cr} &= \frac{\pi}{5000} \times 5.420 \times 10^{11} \times 1.846 \\
M_{cr} &= 6.283 \times 10^{-4} \times 5.420 \times 10^{11} \times 1.846 \\
M_{cr} &= 6.286 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 628.6 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Non-Dimensional Slenderness $\lambda_{LT}$**

$$\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\frac{1.0 \times 1176.2 \times 10^3 \times 250}{6.286 \times 10^8}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.941 \times 10^8}{6.286 \times 10^8}} = \sqrt{0.4678} = 0.6840$$

**Step 5: Buckling Class & Imperfection**

$h/b_f = 400/140 = 2.857 > 2.0$ → Rolled **Class b**, $\alpha_{LT} = 0.34$.

**Step 6: Buckling Parameter $\phi_{LT}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5\left[1 + 0.34(0.6840 - 0.2) + 0.6840^2\right] \\
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5\left[1 + 0.1646 + 0.4679\right] = 0.5 \times 1.6325 = 0.8163
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Reduction Factor $\chi_{LT}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{0.8163 + \sqrt{0.8163^2 - 0.6840^2}} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{0.8163 + \sqrt{0.6664 - 0.4679}} = \frac{1}{0.8163 + 0.4455} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{1.2618} = 0.7926
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8: Design Bending Strength $M_d$**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_d &= \frac{0.7926 \times 1.0 \times 1176.2 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} \\
M_d &= \frac{2.331 \times 10^8}{1.10} = 2.119 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 211.9 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 9: Upper Limit Check**

$$M_{d,\text{upper}} = \frac{1.5 \times 1025.3 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} = 3.495 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 349.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_d = 211.9 < 349.5$ kN·m ✓

**Step 10: Adequacy Check**

$$M_u = 225 \text{ kN·m} > M_d = 211.9 \text{ kN·m} \quad \times \text{ UNSAFE}$$

> **Conclusion: The ISMB 400 beam is NOT adequate against LTB under the given loading.** Either a heavier section (ISMB 450) must be selected, or lateral bracing must be provided to reduce $L_{LT}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distribution of Marks (7 marks):**
> - Factored moment and section properties — 1.5 marks
> - Computation of $M_{cr}$ — 2 marks
> - Computation of $\lambda_{LT}$, $\phi_{LT}$, $\chi_{LT}$ — 2 marks
> - Final $M_d$ calculation and adequacy check — 1.5 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Total: ~6 marks lost per typical 14-mark answer)**
> 
> 1. **Wrong effective length** ($L_{LT}$): Using the actual span $L$ instead of $K \times L$. Always refer to **IS 800:2007, Table 14** for the correct $K$ value. **(1–2 marks lost)**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the upper limit check** $M_d \leq 1.5 Z_e f_y / \gamma_{m0}$: This is a **mandatory second check** for LTB. Skipping it leads to direct deduction. **(1 mark lost)**
> 
> 3. **Confusing the Perry formula for LTB with that for columns**: The LTB formula uses $\alpha_{LT}$ and $\lambda_{LT}$, NOT the column $\alpha$ and $\lambda$. The structure is similar but the symbols and imperfection values are different. **(1 mark lost)**
> 
> 4. **Using $\gamma_{m0} = 1.0$ instead of $1.10$**: A very common slip-up. Always use $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ for yielding checks. **(0.5–1 mark lost)**
> 
> 5. **Ignoring section classification**: The student must first classify the section (Class 1/2/3) before applying the appropriate formula. A Class 3 section cannot use $Z_p$ — only $Z_e$. **(1 mark lost)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This and Pin to Your Study Wall!**

### 5.3.1 Conceptual Foundation
- [x] Flexural strength = maximum moment a beam can resist before failure.
- [x] Laterally supported beam: failure by **yielding** (governed by section classification).
- [x] Laterally unsupported beam: failure by **lateral-torsional buckling (LTB)**.
- [x] LTB involves coupled **lateral deflection** and **twisting** of the cross-section.

### 5.3.2 Section Classification (b/t Ratios)
- [x] **Class 1 (Plastic):** Full plastic moment + rotation capacity. Flange $\lambda_f \leq 9.4\varepsilon$ (rolled).
- [x] **Class 2 (Compact):** Full plastic moment, no rotation. Flange $\lambda_f \leq 10.5\varepsilon$ (rolled).
- [x] **Class 3 (Semi-compact):** Only elastic yield moment $Z_e f_y$ attainable. Flange $\lambda_f \leq 15.7\varepsilon$ (rolled).
- [x] **Class 4 (Slender):** Local buckling precedes yield. Effective section needed.
- [x] $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250/f_y}$ — parameter for $f_y$-dependent limits.

### 5.3.3 Lateral Support Criterion
- [x] Simply supported / continuous beam: $L_{LT}/r_y \leq 250/\sqrt{f_y}$.
- [x] Cantilever beam: $L_{LT}/r_y \leq 300/\sqrt{f_y}$.

### 5.3.4 Design Moment Strength — Two Formulas
- [x] **Class 1 / 2:** $M_d = \beta_b Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ (full plastic).
- [x] **Class 3:** $M_d = Z_e f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ (elastic only).
- [x] **LTB case (unsupported):** $M_d = \chi_{LT} \beta_b Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$, but $M_d \leq 1.5 Z_e f_y / \gamma_{m0}$.

### 5.3.5 LTB Calculation Sequence (Mnemonic: "C-C-M-C-P-U")
1. **C**ompute section properties ($Z_p$, $Z_e$, $I_y$, $I_t$, $I_w$).
2. **C**lassify the section.
3. **M**easure $M_{cr}$ from the elastic critical moment formula.
4. **C**ompute $\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\beta_b Z_p f_y / M_{cr}}$.
5. **P**ick $\alpha_{LT}$ from buckling class table.
6. **U**se $\chi_{LT}$ formula and check upper limit.

### 5.3.6 Key Constants (Memorize!)
- [x] $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa = **200 GPa**
- [x] $G = 0.7692 \times 10^5$ MPa = **76.92 GPa**
- [x] $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ (yielding)
- [x] $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ (ultimate stress)
- [x] $\gamma_{mb} = 1.10$ (bearing)
- [x] $f_y$ = **250 MPa** (Fe 410) or **350 MPa** (Fe 540)

### 5.3.7 Buckling Class & Imperfection Factor
- [x] Rolled, $h/b_f \leq 2$ → **Class a**, $\alpha_{LT} = 0.21$
- [x] Rolled, $h/b_f > 2$ → **Class b**, $\alpha_{LT} = 0.34$
- [x] Welded, $h/b_f \leq 2$ → **Class b**, $\alpha_{LT} = 0.34$
- [x] Welded, $h/b_f > 2$ → **Class c**, $\alpha_{LT} = 0.49$

### 5.3.8 The Two Mandatory Checks for LTB Beams
1. **Strength check:** $M_u \leq M_d$
2. **Upper limit check:** $M_d \leq 1.5 Z_e f_y / \gamma_{m0}$

### 5.3.9 Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Forgetting the partial safety factor $\gamma_{m0}$.
- ❌ Using span length $L$ directly instead of effective length $L_{LT}$.
- ❌ Skipping the upper limit $1.5 Z_e f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ check.
- ❌ Mixing up elastic modulus $E$ and shear modulus $G$.
- ❌ Applying plastic moment $M_p$ formula to a Class 3 section.
- ❌ Ignoring the high-shear reduction $M_{dv}$ when $V > 0.6 V_d$.

### 5.3.10 One-Line Summary for Viva
> *"A laterally supported beam fails by yielding (governed by section class); a laterally unsupported beam fails by LTB (governed by $\chi_{LT}$ and the Perry-Robertson buckling curve). The design moment is always the lesser of the plastic strength and 1.5 times the elastic yield strength."*

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Flexural Strength and Lateral Stability of Beams**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET602 - DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Laterally Supported and Laterally Unsupported Beams. Design of Roof Trusses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Laterally Supported Beams (LSB)

A **Laterally Supported Beam** is a flexural member in which the compression flange is prevented from moving laterally (sideways) for its entire length, either by means of **lateral bracing**, **shear connectors to a slab**, **purlins connected through cleats**, or by being **embedded in a concrete slab**. Because lateral displacement and twisting of the cross-section are restrained, the failure mode is governed purely by **flexural yielding** (plastic moment capacity) and not by lateral-torsional instability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** As per **IS 800:2007 (Limit State Method)**, for laterally supported beams, design bending strength is governed by the **plastic moment capacity** $M_p = Z_p \cdot f_y$ or the **yield moment capacity** $M_y = Z_e \cdot f_y$, depending on the **section classification** (plastic/compact/semi-compact).

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine holding a wooden ruler flat on a table with both hands and pressing down on the middle. It bends and fails by sagging — that is **flexural failure (LSB)**. Now imagine flipping the ruler on its narrow edge without holding the sides and pressing down. It does not just bend — it twists and falls sideways! That sideways twist is **Lateral-Torsional Buckling (LTB)**. Lateral support is like having an invisible hand pressing the top edge of the ruler, preventing that sideways fall.

## 1.2 Laterally Unsupported Beams (LUSB)

A **Laterally Unsupported Beam** is a flexural member where the compression flange is **free to displace laterally and rotate about the longitudinal axis** between supports (or between points of lateral restraint). Such beams fail not by yielding alone but by a combination of **lateral deflection, twist, and bending** — termed **Lateral-Torsional Buckling (LTB)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Definition:** Per **IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.2.2**, the unsupported length $L_{LT}$ is the lateral unsupported length of the compression flange between points of effective lateral restraint. A beam with $L_{LT} \le L_u$ (limiting length) is treated as laterally supported.

### Intuitive Analogy
Returning to the ruler example: when the ruler stands on its narrow edge without side support and you load it, it tries to **bow out sideways and twist** simultaneously. This is LTB — failure at a stress much lower than the yield stress. Adding side brackets (purlins or bracing) is like tying a string along the top edge to keep it straight.

## 1.3 Roof Trusses

A **Roof Truss** is a triangulated plane frame system, used to support roof loads over large spans economically. Members are primarily **pin-connected** (in design assumption) and carry **axial forces** — either tension (chords and ties) or compression (rafters, struts, and webs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Scope:** Design of **Angle-Section** members of a roof truss (tension and compression), selection of **purlins** and **roof sheeting**, **gusset plate** design, and application of **load combinations** as per IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.6 (Industrial Buildings).

### Intuitive Analogy
A roof truss is like a strong bridge made of triangles for your roof. Triangles are the only shape that cannot be squashed without changing the length of its sides — so triangles make trusses very stiff. The **top members (rafters)** are pushed down and bend like a slope, the **bottom member (tie)** holds the walls from spreading, and the **inner members (web)** distribute forces between them — much like the bones in your forearm do for your wrist.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (As Per IS 800:2007)

| Parameter | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Partial Safety Factor for steel $\gamma_{m0}$** | **1.10** | Yielding & buckling checks |
| **Partial Safety Factor $\gamma_{m1}$** | **1.25** | Ultimate stress checks |
| **Partial Safety Factor $\gamma_{m2}$** | **1.25** | Bolt/weld capacity at ultimate |
| **Partial Safety Factor for load $\gamma_f$ (DL)** | **1.5** | Dead Load (unfavorable) |
| **Partial Safety Factor for load $\gamma_f$ (LL/WL)** | **1.5** | Live/Wind Load (unfavorable) |
| **Young's Modulus $E$** | **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** | Steel stiffness |
| **Shear Modulus $G$** | **0.769 × 10⁵ N/mm²** | Steel torsion |
| **Yield Stress $f_y$ (Fe 410)** | **250 N/mm²** | Most common grade |
| **Yield Stress $f_y$ (Fe 540)** | **410 N/mm²** | High-strength grade |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Section Classification (IS 800:2007, Cl. 3.7)

Each element of a cross-section is classified based on width-to-thickness ratio $b/t$ as:
- **Class 1 (Plastic)** — can form plastic hinge with adequate rotation
- **Class 2 (Compact)** — can develop plastic moment but limited rotation
- **Class 3 (Semi-compact)** — can develop yield moment $M_y$ but not plastic
- **Class 4 (Slender)** — local buckling precedes yield

> [!NOTE]
> For Indian Standard Hot-Rolled I-sections (ISHB, ISMB, ISJB), most are Class 1 (plastic) by default when used as beams — this is the most design-efficient case in KTU problems.

## 2.2 Design of Laterally Supported Beams

### Step 1: Check Section Classification
For I-sections with standard proportions, the section is plastic (Class 1) if $b/t_f \le 9.4 \varepsilon$ and $d/t_w \le 83.9 \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250/f_y}$.

### Step 2: Bending Strength (Low Shear Case)
When factored design shear $V \le 0.6 V_d$, the design bending moment capacity is:

$$M_d = \beta_b \cdot \frac{Z_p \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

where $\beta_b = 1.0$ for **plastic and compact** sections, and for **semi-compact**:

$$M_d = \frac{Z_e \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

### Step 3: Shear Strength
For I-sections, plastic shear capacity is:

$$V_d = \frac{f_{yw} \cdot h_w \cdot t_w}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}}$$

where $f_{yw}$ is yield stress of web, $h_w$ is web depth, $t_w$ is web thickness.

### Step 4: High Shear Case
When $V > 0.6 V_d$, reduce bending capacity per IS 800:2007, Cl. 9.2.5:

$$M_{dv} = M_d - \beta \cdot (V - 0.6 V_d) \le M_d$$

where $\beta = \frac{(M_d - M_{fd})/V_d}{1 - 0.6}$ and $M_{fd} = Z_e \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ (low shear yield moment).

## 2.3 Design of Laterally Unsupported Beams (LTB)

### The Critical Concept: Lateral-Torsional Buckling

When the compression flange is unsupported, the beam fails by **bending + lateral deflection + twist**. The critical stress $f_{cr,b}$ is reduced below yield. The design uses the **Lateral-Torsional Buckling Strength Method** (IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.2.2.1, Appendix F).

### Governing Equations

**Step 1:** Compute the **elastic critical moment** $M_{cr}$ for lateral-torsional buckling:

$$M_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I_y \cdot h_f}{2 L_{LT}^2} \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{20} \left( \frac{L_{LT}/r_y}{h_f/r_y} \right)^2 \right]^{0.5}$$

or in full form:

$$M_{cr} = \sqrt{ \left( \frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{L_{LT}^2} \right) \left( \frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L_{LT}^2} + G I_t \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{L_{LT}^2} \right) }$$

Wait, the cleaner IS 800:2007 (Annex F) form is:

$$M_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{(L_{LT}/r_y)^2} \sqrt{ \frac{C_w}{I_y} \left( 1 + \frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{L_{LT}^2} \cdot \frac{1}{C_w} \cdot \frac{G I_t L_{LT}^2}{\pi^2} \right) }$$

Simplified to:

$$M_{cr} = \frac{\pi \sqrt{E I_y G I_t}}{L_{LT}} \sqrt{1 + \frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L_{LT}^2 G I_t}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute **non-dimensional slenderness**:

$$\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{ \frac{\beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_y}{M_{cr}} }$$

**Step 3:** Compute **buckling strength reduction factor $\chi_{LT}$** using the Perry-Robertson buckling curve (Table 14 of IS 800):

$$\chi_{LT} = \frac{1}{\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}} \le 1.0$$

$$\phi_{LT} = 0.5 \left[ 1 + \alpha_{LT} (\lambda_{LT} - 0.2) + \lambda_{LT}^2 \right]$$

For rolled sections: $\alpha_{LT} = 0.21$ (rolled I-sections, Table 14, IS 800:2007).

**Step 4:** Design bending strength:

$$M_d = \frac{\chi_{LT} \cdot f_y \cdot \beta_b \cdot Z_p}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simplified KTU Design Approach:** For KTU exam efficiency, when the beam is **doubly symmetric I-section** with standard proportions, students can use the **effective stress method** $f_{bd} = \chi_{LT} f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ approach, computing $\lambda_{LT}$ from a direct formula involving $L_{LT}/r_y$.

## 2.4 Roof Trusses — Design Philosophy

### (a) Loads on Trusses (IS 875 Series)
- **Dead Load (DL):** Self-weight of truss + purlins + sheeting + bracing
- **Live Load (LL):** **0.75 kN/m²** for inaccessible roof (IS 875 Part 2)
- **Wind Load (WL):** As per IS 875 Part 3 (coefficient method on sloped roof)

### (b) Load Combinations (IS 800:2007, Table 4)
| Combination | Equation |
|---|---|
| 1 | $1.5 (DL + LL)$ |
| 2 | $1.2 (DL + LL \pm WL)$ |
| 3 | $1.5 (DL \pm WL)$ |
| 4 | $0.9 DL \pm 1.5 WL$ |

The **critical (worst) combination** governs each member design.

### (c) Member Design — Tension Members
Design strength of tension member:

$$T_{dg} = \frac{A_g \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

And rupture strength (when connected by bolts/welds):

$$T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \cdot A_n \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$$

**Governing strength:** $T_d = \min(T_{dg}, T_{dn})$.

> [!NOTE]
> For angles connected through one leg, use **shear lag factor** $\beta$ for net area — the connected leg carries full force but the unconnected leg carries less due to non-uniform stress. Effective net area: $A_e = \alpha \cdot A_n$ (Table 11, IS 800:2007).

### (d) Member Design — Compression Members
Design strength:

$$P_d = \frac{A_g \cdot f_{cd}}{\gamma_{m0}}$$

where $f_{cd} = \frac{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda^2}} = \chi \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$

$$\phi = 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.49 (\lambda - 0.2) + \lambda^2 \right]$$

Slenderness ratio $\lambda = \sqrt{f_y / f_{cc}} = (KL/r) \cdot \sqrt{f_y / (\pi^2 E)}$.

For angles, IS 800:2007, Cl. 7.5.1.2 specifies **effective radius of gyration** based on axis of buckling:
- For **unequal angles**, weak axis $v-v$ governs in plane of gusset, $x-x$ out of plane
- Effective length $KL = 0.85L$ to $1.0L$ depending on end conditions

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Plastic moment | $M_p = Z_p \cdot f_y$ | $Z_p$ = plastic section modulus |
| Yield moment | $M_y = Z_e \cdot f_y$ | $Z_e$ = elastic section modulus |
| Design moment (LSB, low shear) | $M_d = \beta_b Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | $\beta_b = 1.0$ for plastic |
| Design moment (LSB, high shear) | $M_{dv} = M_d - \beta(V - 0.6 V_d)$ | When $V > 0.6 V_d$ |
| Shear capacity (I-section) | $V_d = f_{yw} h_w t_w / (\sqrt{3} \gamma_{m0})$ | Plastic shear |
| Elastic LTB moment | $M_{cr} = \pi \sqrt{E I_y G I_t} / L_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \pi^2 E I_w / (L_{LT}^2 G I_t)}$ | Annex F, IS 800 |
| Non-dim LTB slenderness | $\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\beta_b Z_p f_y / M_{cr}}$ | — |
| Buckling curve $\phi_{LT}$ | $\phi_{LT} = 0.5[1 + 0.21(\lambda_{LT} - 0.2) + \lambda_{LT}^2]$ | Rolled I-section |
| Reduction factor | $\chi_{LT} = 1/(\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}) \le 1.0$ | — |
| LSB design moment | $M_d = \chi_{LT} f_y \beta_b Z_p / \gamma_{m0}$ | — |
| Tension strength (yield) | $T_{dg} = A_g f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Gross section |
| Tension strength (rupture) | $T_{dn} = 0.9 A_n f_u / \gamma_{m1}$ | Net section |
| Compression strength | $P_d = \chi f_y A_g / \gamma_{m0}$ | $\chi$ from buckling curve |
| Imperfection factor (angle) | $\alpha = 0.49$ | Table 7, IS 800 |
| Slenderness limit (tension) | $\lambda \le 400$ | Cl. 7.1.2 |
| Slenderness limit (compression) | $\lambda \le 180$ | Cl. 7.1.2 |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Laterally Supported Beams** are typical in **floor systems of buildings** where concrete slabs act as continuous lateral bracing, in **bridge girders** with deck slab attachment, and in **crane girders with longitudinal stiffeners**.
- **Laterally Unsupported Beams** are common in **roof purlins spanning between rafters**, **gantry girders** (top flange may be unsupported between stiffeners), **long-span floor beams** before deck placement, and **walkway platforms**.
- **Roof Trusses** are ubiquitous in **industrial sheds, warehouses, airport hangers, railway platforms, exhibition halls, and stadiums** for spans 10 m to 60+ m. Angles are chosen because they are cheap, light, and connect easily via gusset plates.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 DERIVATION: Elastic Critical Moment for Lateral-Torsional Buckling

### Setup
Consider a **doubly symmetric I-section beam** of length $L_{LT}$ with **simply supported** boundary conditions at both ends:
- Lateral deflection $u = 0$ at both ends
- Twist rotation $\phi = 0$ at both ends
- Warping free at both ends

The total potential energy $\Pi$ of the buckled configuration is:

$$\Pi = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^{L_{LT}} \left[ E I_y \left(\frac{d^2 u}{dz^2}\right)^2 + E I_w \left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dz^2}\right)^2 + G I_t \left(\frac{d\phi}{dz}\right)^2 \right] dz - M \int_0^{L_{LT}} \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{d\phi}{dz}\right)^2 \phi \, dz$$

> **Note:** $E I_y (u'')^2$ = lateral bending strain energy, $E I_w (\phi'')^2$ = warping strain energy, $G I_t (\phi')^2$ = St. Venant torsion strain energy, and $M \phi^2 / 2$ is the destabilizing work done by the applied moment.

### Equilibrium Equations (Euler–Bernoulli)
By the principle of stationary potential energy, we obtain two coupled differential equations:

$$E I_y \frac{d^4 u}{dz^4} + M \frac{d^2 \phi}{dz^2} = 0 \quad \text{(lateral equilibrium)}$$

$$E I_w \frac{d^4 \phi}{dz^4} - G I_t \frac{d^2 \phi}{dz^2} + M \frac{d^2 u}{dz^2} = 0 \quad \text{(torsional equilibrium)}$$

### Solution via Sinusoidal Mode Shape
Assume both lateral deflection and twist vary as:

$$u(z) = U_0 \sin\left(\frac{\pi z}{L_{LT}}\right), \qquad \phi(z) = \Phi_0 \sin\left(\frac{\pi z}{L_{LT}}\right)$$

This satisfies all four boundary conditions ($u = u'' = 0$ and $\phi = \phi'' = 0$ at $z=0, L_{LT}$ for warping-free ends).

### Substituting
Let $k = \pi / L_{LT}$. Then:

$$\frac{d^2 u}{dz^2} = -k^2 U_0 \sin(kz), \qquad \frac{d^2 \phi}{dz^2} = -k^2 \Phi_0 \sin(kz)$$

Substituting into the lateral equation:

$$E I_y \cdot k^4 U_0 - M k^2 \Phi_0 = 0 \implies U_0 = \frac{M k^2 \Phi_0}{E I_y k^4} = \frac{M \Phi_0}{E I_y k^2} = \frac{M L_{LT}^2 \Phi_0}{\pi^2 E I_y}$$

Substituting into the torsional equation:

$$E I_w k^4 \Phi_0 + G I_t k^2 \Phi_0 - M k^2 U_0 = 0$$

Replacing $U_0$:

$$(E I_w k^4 + G I_t k^2) \Phi_0 - M k^2 \cdot \frac{M L_{LT}^2 \Phi_0}{\pi^2 E I_y} = 0$$

Dividing by $\Phi_0$ and noting $k^2 = \pi^2 / L_{LT}^2$:

$$E I_w \frac{\pi^4}{L_{LT}^4} + G I_t \frac{\pi^2}{L_{LT}^2} - \frac{M^2 L_{LT}^2}{\pi^2 E I_y} = 0$$

### Solving for M
Rearranging:

$$\frac{M^2 L_{LT}^2}{\pi^2 E I_y} = \frac{\pi^2}{L_{LT}^2} \left( \frac{E I_w \pi^2}{L_{LT}^2} + G I_t \right)$$

$$M^2 = \frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{L_{LT}^2} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{L_{LT}^2} \cdot \left( E I_w \frac{\pi^2}{L_{LT}^2} + G I_t \right)$$

$$M^2 = \frac{\pi^4 E^2 I_y I_w}{L_{LT}^4} + \frac{\pi^2 E I_y G I_t}{L_{LT}^2}$$

Factoring out $\frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{L_{LT}^2}$:

$$M^2 = \frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{L_{LT}^2} \cdot \left( \frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L_{LT}^2} + G I_t \right)$$

### Final Closed-Form Elastic Critical Moment

$$\boxed{M_{cr} = \frac{\pi}{L_{LT}} \sqrt{E I_y G I_t} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L_{LT}^2 G I_t}}}$$

This is the **exact elastic critical moment** governing LTB for doubly symmetric I-sections under uniform moment, as codified in **IS 800:2007, Annex F**.

## 3.2 DERIVATION: Reduction Factor $\chi_{LT}$ (Perry-Robertson)

### Why is this needed?
The elastic critical moment $M_{cr}$ is the **theoretical bifurcation load** assuming perfect geometry. Real beams have **initial imperfections** (slight out-of-straightness, residual stresses). Perry-Robertson modifies the perfect bifurcation theory to capture realistic buckling.

### Mathematical Basis
For a beam-column with initial sinusoidal imperfection of amplitude $\delta_0$, the total deflection under moment $M$ is:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{E I_y} = -\frac{d^2 v}{dz^2} \implies v = v_0 \sin\left(\frac{\pi z}{L}\right)$$

The mid-span deflection $v_m$ satisfies:

$$v_m = \frac{\delta_0}{1 - M / M_{cr}}$$

The maximum stress in the extreme fiber (summing direct stress + bending due to lateral deflection) reaches yield $f_y$ at:

$$M = M_y \left[ 1 - \frac{\lambda^2}{\lambda^2 + (M_y / M_{cr})^{-1}} \right]$$

After algebraic manipulation (substituting $\lambda^2 = M_y / M_{cr}$ for the non-dimensional slenderness), the result is:

$$(M_{cr}/M_y) = \phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda^2}$$

where $\phi = 0.5(1 + \eta + \lambda^2)$ and $\eta$ is the **imperfection parameter**.

### Imperfection Parameter
The Perry-Robertson formula yields:

$$\phi_{LT} = 0.5 \left[ 1 + \alpha_{LT} (\lambda_{LT} - 0.2) + \lambda_{LT}^2 \right]$$

For **rolled I-sections (hot-rolled)**, $\alpha_{LT} = 0.21$ (per **IS 800:2007, Table 14**).
For **welded I-sections**, $\alpha_{LT} = 0.49$.

### Final Inversion

$$\chi_{LT} = \frac{M_d / M_p}{1} = \frac{1}{\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}}$$

This is what we use in design: $M_d = \chi_{LT} \beta_b Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$.

## 3.3 NUMERICAL WORKED EXAMPLE: LSB CHECK

**Given:** ISMB 300 section, Fe 410, $f_y = 250$ N/mm², factored moment $M_u = 120$ kN·m, factored shear $V_u = 80$ kN.

**Step 1: Section properties of ISMB 300**
- $h = 300$ mm, $b_f = 140$ mm, $t_f = 12.4$ mm, $t_w = 7.5$ mm
- $Z_p = 651.74 \times 10^3$ mm³ (from steel tables)
- $Z_e = 573.6 \times 10^3$ mm³
- $h_w = 300 - 2 \times 12.4 = 275.2$ mm

**Step 2: Section Classification**
For Fe 410, $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250/250} = 1.0$.
- Flange: $b/t_f = 70/12.4 = 5.65 < 9.4 \varepsilon = 9.4$ → **Plastic**
- Web: $d/t_w = 275.2/7.5 = 36.7 < 83.9 \varepsilon = 83.9$ → **Plastic**
- Section is **Class 1 (Plastic)**.

**Step 3: Bending Capacity**
$\beta_b = 1.0$ for plastic section.

$$M_d = \frac{1.0 \times 651.74 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} = 148.12 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 148.12 \text{ kN·m}$$

Check: $M_u = 120$ kN·m $< M_d = 148.12$ kN·m → **SAFE in bending**.

**Step 4: Shear Capacity**

$$V_d = \frac{250 \times 275.2 \times 7.5}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.10} = 271.0 \times 10^3 \text{ N} = 271.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: High Shear Check**
$V_u / V_d = 80 / 271 = 0.295 < 0.6$ → **No reduction in bending capacity needed**.

## 3.4 NUMERICAL WORKED EXAMPLE: LUSB CHECK

**Given:** Same ISMB 300, $M_u = 120$ kN·m, $L_{LT} = 4.0$ m (laterally unsupported).

**Step 1: Section properties**
- $I_y = 4536 \times 10^4$ mm⁴, $r_y = 26.5$ mm
- $I_t = 73.7 \times 10^4$ mm⁴, $I_w = 0.6035 \times 10^{10}$ mm⁶
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $G = 0.769 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- $Z_p = 651.74 \times 10^3$ mm³

**Step 2: Compute $M_{cr}$**
$L_{LT} = 4000$ mm. First compute inner bracket:

$$\frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L_{LT}^2 G I_t} = \frac{9.87 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 0.6035 \times 10^{10}}{4000^2 \times 0.769 \times 10^5 \times 73.7 \times 10^4}$$

Numerator: $9.87 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 0.6035 \times 10^{10} = 11.91 \times 10^{15}$
Denominator: $16 \times 10^6 \times 0.769 \times 10^5 \times 73.7 \times 10^4 = 9.07 \times 10^{16}$

Ratio: $11.91 \times 10^{15} / 9.07 \times 10^{16} = 0.1313$

So $\sqrt{1 + 0.1313} = \sqrt{1.1313} = 1.0636$.

Next:

$$\frac{\pi \sqrt{E I_y G I_t}}{L_{LT}} = \frac{3.1416 \sqrt{2 \times 10^5 \times 4536 \times 10^4 \times 0.769 \times 10^5 \times 73.7 \times 10^4}}{4000}$$

Inside sqrt: $2 \times 10^5 \times 4536 \times 10^4 \times 0.769 \times 10^5 \times 73.7 \times 10^4 = 5.14 \times 10^{20}$

$\sqrt{5.14 \times 10^{20}} = 2.27 \times 10^{10}$

Multiply by $\pi / L_{LT}$: $\frac{3.1416 \times 2.27 \times 10^{10}}{4000} = 1.78 \times 10^7$ N·mm = 17.8 kN·m

So $M_{cr} = 17.8 \times 1.0636 = 18.93$ kN·m.

**Step 3: Compute $\lambda_{LT}$**
$\beta_b = 1.0$ (plastic section).

$$\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\frac{1.0 \times 651.74 \times 10^3 \times 250}{18.93 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{8.605} = 2.93$$

**Step 4: Compute $\phi_{LT}$ and $\chi_{LT}$**
$\alpha_{LT} = 0.21$ (rolled section).

$$\phi_{LT} = 0.5[1 + 0.21(2.93 - 0.2) + 2.93^2] = 0.5[1 + 0.574 + 8.585] = 5.080$$

$$\chi_{LT} = \frac{1}{5.080 + \sqrt{5.080^2 - 2.93^2}} = \frac{1}{5.080 + \sqrt{25.81 - 8.585}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{5.080 + \sqrt{17.22}} = \frac{1}{5.080 + 4.150} = \frac{1}{9.230} = 0.1083$$

**Step 5: Design Moment Capacity**

$$M_d = \frac{0.1083 \times 1.0 \times 651.74 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} = 16.04 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Conclusion:** $M_u = 120$ kN·m $\gg M_d = 16.04$ kN·m → **UNSAFE! Beam fails by LTB**.

> [!WARNING]
> This example illustrates the **dramatic reduction in capacity** when lateral support is removed. The plastic moment capacity (148 kN·m) drops by 89% (to 16 kN·m) when the beam is unsupported over 4 m. **This is exactly why purlins and bracing are critical in steel buildings.**

## 3.5 ANALYTICAL MATRIX: When to Use What?

| Member Type | Governing Check | Code Clause | Key Input |
|---|---|---|---|
| Beam (LSB), plastic | $M_d = Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Cl. 8.2.1.2 | Plastic section modulus |
| Beam (LSB), high shear | $M_{dv} = M_d - \beta(V - 0.6V_d)$ | Cl. 9.2.5 | Both M and V |
| Beam (LUSB) | $M_d = \chi_{LT} f_y \beta_b Z_p / \gamma_{m0}$ | Cl. 8.2.2.1 | $L_{LT}$, $I_y$, $I_t$, $I_w$ |
| Tension member (yield) | $T_{dg} = A_g f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Cl. 6.1 | Gross area |
| Tension member (rupture) | $T_{dn} = 0.9 A_n f_u / \gamma_{m1}$ | Cl. 6.3.1 | Net area, $f_u$ |
| Compression member | $P_d = \chi f_y A_g / \gamma_{m0}$ | Cl. 7.1 | $KL/r$, $f_y$ |
| Gusset plate | Whitmore section yielding + buckling | Cl. 7.4 | Effective width |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Beam Design Decision Tree (LSB vs LUSB)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steel Beam Subjected to Bending] --> B{Compression Flange\nRestrained Laterally?}
    B -- Yes, full length --> C[Laterally Supported Beam]
    B -- No, partial / none --> D[Laterally Unsupported Beam]
    
    C --> E{Section Class?}
    E -- Plastic / Compact --> F[Md = Zp x fy / gamma_m0]
    E -- Semi-compact --> G[Md = Ze x fy / gamma_m0]
    E -- Slender --> H[Reduce Md for local buckling]
    
    F --> I{Shear V > 0.6 Vd?}
    I -- No --> J[OK: Use full Md]
    I -- Yes --> K[Reduce Md using high-shear formula]
    
    D --> L[Compute Elastic Critical Mcr]
    L --> M[Compute lambda_LT]
    M --> N[Compute chi_LT via Perry-Robertson]
    N --> O[Md = chi_LT x fy x Zp / gamma_m0]
    O --> P{Shear Check}
    P -- OK --> Q[SAFE Beam Design]
    P -- Not OK --> R[Increase Section / Add Web Stiffeners]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Roof Sheeting] --> B[Purlins at 1.2 - 1.5 m c/c]
    B --> C[Rafters - Top Chord\nCarries Compression + Bending]
    C --> D[Web Members\nDiagonals and Verticals]
    D --> E[Bottom Chord - Tie\nCarries Pure Tension]
    E --> F[End Support]
    
    subgraph LOADS
        G[Dead Load - Truss + Sheeting + Purlins] --> C
        H[Live Load - 0.75 kN/sqm] --> C
        I[Wind Load - per IS 875 Part 3] --> C
    end
    
    subgraph DESIGN_STEPS
        J[Step 1: Compute Panel Loads] --> K[Step 2: Truss Analysis - Method of Joints]
        K --> L[Step 3: Identify Critical Load Combination per Member]
        L --> M[Step 4: Design Tension / Compression Members]
        M --> N[Step 5: Design Gusset Plate and Welds]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Truss Member Classification (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Roof Truss Member] --> B{Tension or Compression?}
    
    B -- Tension --> C[Compute Force in Member Tmax]
    C --> D{Available Strength Check}
    D --> D1[Yielding on Gross: Tdg = Ag x fy / gamma_m0]
    D --> D2[Rupture on Net: Tdn = 0.9 x An x fu / gamma_m1]
    D --> D3[Block Shear Tdb per Cl 6.4]
    D1 --> E[Governing Td = min Tdg, Tdn, Tdb]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F{For Angle Section}
    F --> F1[Apply shear lag factor alpha]
    F1 --> F2[Compute effective net area Ae = alpha x Anc]
    F2 --> G[Select Section - Equal Angle ISA]
    
    B -- Compression --> H[Compute Force in Member Pmax]
    H --> I[Compute slenderness lambda = KL / r]
    I --> J[Identify relevant axis of buckling]
    J --> J1[For angle: min of rvv and rxx effective]
    J1 --> K[Compute fcd from buckling curve]
    K --> L[Compute Pd = Ag x fcd]
    L --> M[Check lambda less than 180]
    M --> N[Select Angle Section - ISA]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Cross-Section Behavior Under Loading

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LSB_Case
        A1[Beam with Full Lateral Support] --> A2[Compression Flange Held]
        A2 --> A3[Beam Yields in Pure Bending]
        A3 --> A4[Plastic Hinge Forms]
        A4 --> A5[Design Md = Zp x fy / gamma_m0]
    end
    
    subgraph LUSB_Case
        B1[Beam without Lateral Support] --> B2[Compression Flame Free]
        B2 --> B3[Beam Bows Out + Twists]

    end
    
    subgraph TRUSS_Case
        C1[Truss Member Pin-Ended] --> C2[Pure Axial Force]

    end
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Slenderness and Buckling Curve Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Slenderness lambda] --> B[lambda less than 0.2]
    A --> C[0.2 to 1.0]
    A --> D[greater than 1.0]
    
    B --> B1[chi equals 1.0\nFull Plastic Capacity]
    C --> C1[Transition Region\nPerry-Robertson Curve]
```

## 4.6 Schematic: Force Distribution in a Pratt-Type Roof Truss

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TRUSS_GEOMETRY
        L1[Left Support] --- B1[Bottom Node 1]
        B1 --- B2[Bottom Node 2]
        B2 --- B3[Bottom Node 3]
        B3 --- B4[Bottom Node 4]
        B4 --- B5[Bottom Node 5]
        B5 --- R1[Right Support]
        
        L1 --- T1[Top Node 1]
        T1 --- T2[Top Node 2]
        T2 --- T3[Top Node 3 Apex]
        T3 --- T4[Top Node 4]
        T4 --- T5[Top Node 5]
        T5 --- R1
        
        T1 --- B2
        T2 --- B3
        T4 --- B4
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the truss schematic:**
> - **Top chord (T1-T2-T3-T4-T5)** = Rafter, carries **compression** with bending.
> - **Bottom chord (B1-B2-B3-B4-B5)** = Tie, carries pure **tension**.
> - **End verticals (L1-T1, R1-T5)** = End posts, carry **compression**.
> - **Diagonals (T1-B2, T2-B3, B3-T4, B4-T5)** = Web members; in **Pratt truss**, diagonals slope toward center → **tension diagonals**, **compression verticals** (B2-T2, B3-T3, B4-T4).
> - **Apex node (T3)** = Critical node with maximum forces.
