$$KE_{per \, m^3} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2$$

where:
- $\rho$ = **air density** $= 1.20$ kg/m³ (standard sea-level value)
- $V$ = **velocity of air** in m/s

### **Step 2: Conversion of KE to Pressure**

By **Newton's theory of impact**, when a fluid stream is brought to rest against a flat surface, the entire kinetic energy is converted into a static pressure (pressure = force per unit area = energy per unit volume):

$$P = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2$$

### **Step 3: Substituting Standard Air Density**

Substitute $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³:

$$P = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1.20 \times V^2 = 0.6 \cdot V^2 \quad \text{[N/m}^2\text{]}$$

### **Step 4: Accounting for the Built Environment — Modifying $V$ to $V_z$**

The basic wind speed $V_b$ is **not** the speed at the location of interest. It must be modified using 4 coefficients to get $V_z$:

$$V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$$

Substituting into the pressure equation:

$$\boxed{P_z = 0.6 \cdot (V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4)^2 = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Board-Standard)

### **Problem Statement**

> A four-storey office building located in **Kochi, Kerala** is to be designed. Determine the **wind force** acting on the building. The building has:
> - **Plan dimensions:** $L = 30$ m, $B = 15$ m
> - **Height:** $H = 16$ m
> - **Terrain Category:** 3 (Suburban area)
> - **Class of building:** A
> - **Structure type:** Clad rectangular building
> - **Topography:** Flat
> - **Return period:** 50 years
> - **Cyclonic region:** No

### **Solution (Step-by-Step)**

#### **Step 1: Basic Wind Speed $V_b$**

From IS 875 (Part 3) Wind Zone Map, Kochi lies in **Zone III**:

$$V_b = 47 \text{ m/s}$$

#### **Step 2: Risk Coefficient $k_1$**

For **general buildings** with a **50-year return period**:

$$k_1 = 1.0$$

#### **Step 3: Topography Factor $k_3$**

Flat terrain, no hills/valleys:

$$k_3 = 1.0$$

#### **Step 4: Importance Factor $k_4$**

Non-cyclonic region:

$$k_4 = 1.0$$

#### **Step 5: Terrain, Height & Size Factor $k_2$**

For **Terrain Category 3** and **Class A building**:

From Table 3 of IS 875 (Part 3), for Category 3, Class A:

| Height $z$ (m) | $k_2$ |
|:---:|:---:|
| 10 | 0.91 |
| 15 | 0.97 |
| 20 | 1.01 |
| 30 | 1.06 |

For our building of $H = 16$ m, we compute $k_2$ at **three levels**: base, mid, and top.

**Linear Interpolation for $H = 16$ m** (between 15 m and 20 m):

$$k_2 \text{ at } 16 \text{ m} = 0.97 + \dfrac{(1.01 - 0.97)}{(20-15)} \times (16-15)$$

$$k_2 \text{ at } 16 \text{ m} = 0.97 + \dfrac{0.04}{5} \times 1 = 0.97 + 0.008 = 0.978$$

**Computing $k_2$ at 5 m levels** (assuming 4 storeys of 4 m each, divided into segments of 5 m for analysis):

| Level | Height $z$ (m) | $k_2$ |
|:-----:|:--------------:|:-----:|
| 0 | 0 | 0.77 (extrapolated from Table 3) |
| 1 | 5 | 0.85 (interpolated) |
| 2 | 10 | 0.91 |
| 3 | 16 | 0.978 |

#### **Step 6: Design Wind Speed $V_z$ at Each Level**

$$V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4 = 47 \times 1.0 \times k_2 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 47 k_2$$

| Level | Height $z$ (m) | $k_2$ | $V_z = 47 k_2$ (m/s) |
|:-----:|:--------------:|:-----:|:--------------------:|
| 0 | 0 | 0.77 | 36.19 |
| 1 | 5 | 0.85 | 39.95 |
| 2 | 10 | 0.91 | 42.77 |
| 3 | 16 | 0.978 | 45.97 |

#### **Step 7: Design Wind Pressure $P_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$**

| Level | $V_z$ (m/s) | $V_z^2$ | $P_z = 0.6 V_z^2$ (N/m²) |
|:-----:|:----------:|:-------:|:-----------------------:|
| 0 | 36.19 | 1309.7 | 785.8 |
| 1 | 39.95 | 1596.0 | 957.6 |
| 2 | 42.77 | 1829.3 | 1097.6 |
| 3 | 45.97 | 2113.2 | 1267.9 |

#### **Step 8: Force Coefficient $C_f$**

Given $L/B = 30/15 = 2$ and $H/B = 16/15 = 1.067 \approx 1$:

From the table in Section 2.3, for $L/B = 2$ and $H/B = 1$:

$$C_f = 1.1$$

#### **Step 9: Total Wind Force $F$**

The **average pressure** on the building:

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{785.8 + 957.6 + 1097.6 + 1267.9}{4} = \dfrac{4108.9}{4} = 1027.2 \text{ N/m}^2$$

Effective frontal area $A_e$ (using smaller plan dimension $B = 15$ m):

$$A_e = B \times H = 15 \times 16 = 240 \text{ m}^2$$

Total Wind Force:

$$F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot P_{avg} = 1.1 \times 240 \times 1027.2$$

$$F = 1.1 \times 246528 = 271180.8 \text{ N} \approx 271.18 \text{ kN}$$

### **Final Answer**

$$\boxed{F \approx 271.18 \text{ kN}}$$

#### **Overturning Moment about the Base**

$$M = F \cdot \dfrac{H}{2} = 271.18 \times 8 = 2169.44 \text{ kN·m}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Coefficient

| Parameter | Symbol | Increased Effect on $V_z$ | Increased Effect on $P_z$ | Reason |
|-----------|:------:|:--------------------------:|:-------------------------:|--------|
| Risk Coefficient | $k_1$ | ↑ (linearly) | ↑ (quadratically) | Higher return period, higher risk |
| Terrain Factor | $k_2$ | ↑ (linearly) | ↑ (quadratically) | Tighter terrain = lower speed |
| Topography | $k_3$ | ↑ | ↑ | Hilltops cause speed-up |
| Importance | $k_4$ | ↑ | ↑ | Critical structures in cyclones |
| Building Height | $z$ | ↑ ($V_z$ increases) | ↑ | Wind speed grows with height |
| $L/B$ ratio | — | — | $C_f$ ↓ | Slender structures face less drag |
| $H/B$ ratio | — | — | $C_f$ ↑ | Taller structures face more drag |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wind Load Assessment Procedure (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Wind Load Assessment"] --> B["Step 1: Identify Location & Terrain"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Obtain Basic Wind Speed Vb from Wind Zone Map"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Determine Risk Coefficient k1 based on Importance & Return Period"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Compute Terrain Height Size Factor k2 from IS 875 Table 3"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Determine Topography Factor k3 from Site Survey"]
    F --> G["Step 6: Determine Importance Factor k4 for Cyclonic Regions"]
    G --> H["Step 7: Calculate Design Wind Speed Vz = Vb times k1 times k2 times k3 times k4"]
    H --> I["Step 8: Calculate Design Wind Pressure Pz = 0.6 times Vz squared"]
    I --> J["Step 9: Identify Force Coefficient Cf from Building Geometry"]
    J --> K["Step 10: Calculate Net Design Pressure P = Pz times Cpe minus Cpi"]
    K --> L["Step 11: Calculate Wind Force F = Cf times Ae times Pz"]
    L --> M["Step 12: Calculate Overturning Moment M = F times H by 2"]
    M --> N["End Apply Forces to Structural Analysis Model"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Wind Pressure Coefficient Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["Basic Wind Speed Vb"] --> node2["Modify by k1 Risk"]
    node2 --> node3["Modify by k2 Terrain Height Size"]
    node3 --> node4["Modify by k3 Topography"]
    node4 --> node5["Modify by k4 Importance"]
    node5 --> node6["Design Wind Speed Vz"]
    node6 --> node7["Square and Multiply by 0.6"]
    node7 --> node8["Design Wind Pressure Pz"]
    node8 --> node9["Multiply by Force Coefficient Cf"]
    node9 --> node10["Total Wind Force F on Structure"]
    node8 --> node11["Multiply by Cpe minus Cpi"]
    node11 --> node12["Net Design Pressure P on Walls Roof"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Wind Pressure Variation with Height (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph aAtmospheric Boundary Layer
        node1A["Wind Zone Map of India Vb"] --> node1B["Fetch Vb from Map"]
    end
    subgraph bHeightwise Analysis
        node2A["Discretize Building Height H into N segments"] --> node2B["At each level zi compute k2i"]
        node2B --> node2C["Compute Vzi = Vb k1 k2i k3 k4"]
        node2C --> node2D["Compute Pzi = 0.6 Vzi squared"]
    end
    subgraph cResultant Forces
        node3A["Sum wind pressure across all levels"] --> node3B["Apply Cf for drag"]
        node3B --> node3C["Total Lateral Force F on Building"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Visual Hierarchy of Wind Load Parameters

| Level | Parameter | Symbol | Source in IS 875 |
|:-----:|-----------|:------:|:----------------:|
| **L1** | Basic Wind Speed | $V_b$ | Figure 1 |
| **L2** | Risk Coefficient | $k_1$ | Table 2 |
| **L3** | Terrain-Height-Size Factor | $k_2$ | Table 3, 4, 5 |
| **L4** | Topography Factor | $k_3$ | Table 6 |
| **L5** | Importance Factor | $k_4$ | Table 7 |
| **L6** | Pressure Coefficients | $C_{pe}, C_{pi}$ | Table 8 |
| **L7** | Force Coefficient | $C_f$ | Tables 9-16 |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the terms: Basic Wind Speed and Design Wind Speed.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Basic Wind Speed ($V_b$):** It is the peak gust wind speed averaged over a duration of **3 seconds**, measured at a height of **10 m** above the ground in **open terrain (Category 2)** with a return period of **50 years**. It is obtained from the **Wind Zone Map of India** given in IS 875 (Part 3) Figure 1.
>
> **Design Wind Speed ($V_z$):** It is the modified wind speed at a height $z$ above ground, obtained by applying the correction factors $k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4$ to the basic wind speed:
> $$V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$$

---

### **Q2. State the four coefficients used in the assessment of wind load and briefly explain their significance.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`

**Model Answer:**

| Coefficient | Symbol | Significance |
|:-----------:|:------:|--------------|
| Risk Coefficient | $k_1$ | Accounts for structure's importance and return period (design life) |
| Terrain, Height & Size Factor | $k_2$ | Accounts for terrain roughness, building height and building dimensions |
| Topography Factor | $k_3$ | Accounts for the effect of hills, valleys, cliffs on wind speed-up |
| Importance Factor | $k_4$ | Accounts for cyclonic importance of the structure |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]**
> A **10 m high** industrial shed located in **Thiruvananthapuram (Kerala)** is to be designed. The building has a plan of **$25 \text{ m} \times 12 \text{ m}$**. The terrain is **Category 2 (open terrain)**, the structure is **Class B**, the topography is **flat**, and it is a **non-cyclonic** region. Determine the **total design wind force** on the building using IS 875 (Part 3).

#### **Part (a): Determine the design wind speed and wind pressure at each level. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Basic Wind Speed $V_b$**
Thiruvananthapuram lies in **Zone III** of the IS 875 Wind Map:
$$V_b = 47 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Coefficients**
- $k_1 = 1.0$ (50-year return period, general building)
- $k_3 = 1.0$ (flat topography)
- $k_4 = 1.0$ (non-cyclonic region)

**Step 3: $k_2$ for Category 2, Class B**

From IS 875 (Part 3) Table 4 (Category 2, Class B):

| $z$ (m) | $k_2$ |
|:---:|:---:|
| 5 | 0.94 |
| 10 | 1.00 |
| 15 | 1.04 |

**Interpolate at $z = 10$ m exactly:**
$$k_2 = 1.00 \text{ at } 10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: $V_z$ at each 5 m level**

| $z$ (m) | $k_2$ | $V_z = 47 k_2$ (m/s) | $P_z = 0.6 V_z^2$ (N/m²) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 5 | 0.94 | 44.18 | 1171.0 |
| 10 | 1.00 | 47.00 | 1325.4 |

#### **Part (b): Compute total wind force on the building. (7 Marks)**

**Step 5: Force Coefficient $C_f$**

Plan ratio: $L/B = 25/12 \approx 2.08 \approx 2$
Height ratio: $H/B = 10/12 = 0.83 \approx 1$ (interpolated)

From Table (Section 2.3), for $L/B = 2$, $H/B = 1$:
$$C_f = 1.1$$

**Step 6: Effective Frontal Area $A_e$**

$$A_e = B \times H = 12 \times 10 = 120 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 7: Average Wind Pressure**

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{P_5 + P_{10}}{2} = \dfrac{1171.0 + 1325.4}{2} = 1248.2 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 8: Total Wind Force**

$$F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot P_{avg} = 1.1 \times 120 \times 1248.2$$

$$\boxed{F = 164,762.4 \text{ N} \approx 164.76 \text{ kN}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]**
> An **18 m tall** multi-storey office building is located in **Kozhikode, Kerala**. The plan dimensions are **$36 \text{ m} \times 18 \text{ m}$**. The terrain is **Category 3 (Suburban)**, the structure is **Class C**, flat topography, non-cyclonic region, and design life of **50 years**. Compute the **design wind pressure at heights 5 m, 10 m, and 15 m** and the **total wind force**.

#### **Part (a): Compute the design wind pressure at heights 5 m, 10 m and 15 m. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Basic Wind Speed $V_b$**

Kozhikode lies in **Zone III**:
$$V_b = 47 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Coefficients**
- $k_1 = 1.0$
- $k_3 = 1.0$
- $k_4 = 1.0$

**Step 3: $k_2$ for Category 3, Class C**

From IS 875 (Part 3) Table 5:

| $z$ (m) | $k_2$ (Cat 3, Class C) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 5 | 0.79 |
| 10 | 0.86 |
| 15 | 0.94 |

**Step 4: $V_z$ and $P_z$ at each level**

$$V_z = 47 \cdot k_2, \quad P_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$$

| $z$ (m) | $k_2$ | $V_z$ (m/s) | $P_z$ (N/m²) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 5 | 0.79 | 37.13 | 827.2 |
| 10 | 0.86 | 40.42 | 980.5 |
| 15 | 0.94 | 44.18 | 1171.0 |

#### **Part (b): Compute the total wind force. (7 Marks)**

**Step 5: Force Coefficient $C_f$**

Plan ratio: $L/B = 36/18 = 2$
Height ratio: $H/B = 18/18 = 1.0$

For $L/B = 2$, $H/B = 1$:
$$C_f = 1.1$$

**Step 6: Effective Frontal Area $A_e$**

$$A_e = B \times H = 18 \times 18 = 324 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 7: Average Wind Pressure**

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{827.2 + 980.5 + 1171.0}{3} = \dfrac{2978.7}{3} = 992.9 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 8: Total Wind Force**

$$F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot P_{avg} = 1.1 \times 324 \times 992.9$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 353,810.0 \text{ N} \approx 353.81 \text{ kN}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to square $V_z$:** $P_z$ is **quadratic** in $V_z$. Do NOT write $P_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z$ — this is the most common error.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversion:** $P_z$ comes out in N/m². Multiply $A_e$ in m² to get force in **N**, then divide by 1000 to convert to **kN**.
> 3. **Neglecting $k_3$ and $k_4$ for flat terrain:** Even for flat, non-cyclonic regions, you **must** write $k_3 = 1.0$ and $k_4 = 1.0$ explicitly to score full marks.
> 4. **Wrong terrain category selection:** Kerala is mostly **Category 2 or 3**. Read the problem statement carefully.
> 5. **Missing Class of Building:** $k_2$ varies for Class A, B, C — this is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Wrong $C_f$ interpolation:** For $H/B$ values not in the table, **linear interpolation is mandatory**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Assessment of Wind Loads**

- ✅ **Basic Wind Speed ($V_b$):** Kerala → **Zone III ($V_b = 47$ m/s)** or **Zone II ($V_b = 39$ m/s)**.
- ✅ **Core Formula:** $V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$ — always write all four coefficients (even if = 1).
- ✅ **Pressure Formula:** $P_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$ — units N/m² when $V_z$ in m/s.
- ✅ **Force Formula:** $F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot P_z$ — $A_e$ is the **smaller plan dimension × height**.
- ✅ **Coefficient Values (Default):** $k_1 = 1.0$, $k_3 = 1.0$, $k_4 = 1.0$ for typical problems.
- ✅ **$k_2$ depends on THREE things:** (i) Terrain Category (1, 2, 3, 4), (ii) Height $z$, (iii) Class of Building (A, B, C).
- ✅ **Always use linear interpolation** for $k_2$ at non-tabulated heights.
- ✅ **$C_f$ depends on $L/B$ and $H/B$** ratios — refer to Table in Section 2.3.
- ✅ **$A_e = B \times H$** where $B$ is the **smaller** plan dimension (windward side).
- ✅ **Final answer should be in kN** — convert N to kN by dividing by 1000.
- ✅ **IS 875 (Part 3) : 2015** is the **latest revision** — memorize Table 3/4/5 for $k_2$.
- ✅ **For tall buildings, $k_2$ increases with height** — wind speed is greater at the top.
- ✅ **Overturning moment** $M = F \cdot \frac{H}{2}$ if force is assumed to act at mid-height.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Wind pressure on windward face is **positive**; on leeward face is **negative** (suction).
- ✅ **Frequent examiner trick:** "What if cyclonic?" → introduce $k_4 > 1.0$ (1.15 to 1.30).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of I-Section Purlin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET602 - DESIGN OF STEEL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of I-Section Purlin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Purlin:** A horizontal structural member, typically spanning between rafters or trusses, which transfers the roof sheeting loads (dead, live, wind/snow) to the main roof trusses. When the section used is a **rolled I-Section (I-beam / RSJ)**, it is referred to as an **I-Section Purlin**.

In the **Limit State Method (LSM)** of design as per **IS 800:2007**, I-Section purlins are designed as **laterally supported** or **laterally unsupported** flexural members subjected to **biaxial bending** (combined bending about both major and minor axes) plus axial load effects (when wind suction is considered).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Rails of a Railway Track"

Imagine a **railway track**: the two parallel steel rails are like the **purlins**. The rails do not directly carry the train's full weight; instead, the **sleepers (roof sheeting)** rest on the rails and transfer the train's load (snow/wind/dead load) uniformly to the rails. The rails then transfer this distributed load to the **main girders (trusses/rafters)** at discrete points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why an I-Section?** Just like the railway rail itself, the I-section is the most efficient shape for resisting bending — maximum material is placed at the top and bottom flanges (where bending stresses are maximum), and minimum material is in the web (where shear stresses are maximum). This makes the I-Section the natural choice for a bending-dominant member like a purlin.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Unit weight of steel (γ):** **78.5 kN/m³** (= 7850 kg/m³)
- **Modulus of Elasticity (E):** **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** (200 GPa)
- **Poisson's ratio (ν):** **0.30**
- **Partial Safety Factor for steel material (γₘ):** **1.10** (LSM)
- **Partial Safety Factor for Dead Load (γ_D):** **1.2** (or 1.5 if DL is stabilizing... actually 1.2 opposing)
- **Partial Safety Factor for Live Load (γ_L):** **1.2**
- **Partial Safety Factor for Wind Load (γ_W):** **1.2**
- **Maximum permissible deflection for purlins:** **Span / 150** (for live/wind load only, as per IS 800:2007 Cl. 8.7.2)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Favourite:** The deflection check `δ_max = L/150` for purlins is a **guaranteed 2-mark question** in any KTU exam. Memorize it verbatim.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Purlins

Purlins are classified by:
1. **Position on rafter**: *Sag rods* tie purlins at mid-span (sagging tie) or at 1/3 points (hogging tie)
2. **Section type**: *I-Section, Channel Section, Cold-Formed Section, Tubular Section*
3. **Support type**: *Simply supported* or *Continuous*
4. **Lateral support**: *Laterally supported* (sheeting connected through shear connectors is assumed to provide lateral restraint to the top flange) or *laterally unsupported*

## 2.2 Loads Acting on Purlins

The purlin must be designed for the **most critical load combination** as per **IS 800:2007 Cl. 9.3**:

1. **Dead Load (DL):** Self-weight of sheeting + purlin + sag rod (if any)
2. **Imposed/Live Load (LL):** As per IS 875 (Part 2) — for sloping roofs, LL = 0.75 kN/m² for slope ≤ 10°, reduced to zero for slope > 30°
3. **Wind Load (WL):** As per IS 875 (Part 3) — both pressure (+ve) and suction (−ve) cases

### Load Combination Cases (as per Cl. 9.3.2 of IS 800):

| Case | Combination | Description |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | **1.2 DL + 1.2 LL** | Gravity case, no wind |
| 2 | **1.2 DL + 1.2 LL + 1.2 WL** | Gravity + Wind pressure |
| 3 | **1.2 DL + 1.2 WL** | Wind suction only |
| 4 | **0.9 DL + 1.2 WL** | Critical uplift case (wind suction, low DL) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The wind suction case (Case 4) is often **governing** for light roof structures with high wind zones (e.g., coastal Kerala areas), because the negative pressure tries to lift the roof, putting the bottom flange in tension and causing **reversal of stresses**.

## 2.3 Design Bending Moments

For a **simply supported** purlin of span `L` with **uniformly distributed load `w`** per metre run:

$$M_z = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{8} \quad \text{(bending about major axis, Z-Z)}$$

For wind load acting **perpendicular to roof slope** (component perpendicular to purlin axis), it gets resolved into:
- Component along roof slope: causes bending about **minor axis (Y-Y)** of the purlin
- Component perpendicular to roof slope: causes bending about **major axis (Z-Z)** of the purlin

For a roof slope `θ` and total wind load `w_w` per m²:

$$w_z = w_w \cdot s \cdot \cos\theta \quad \text{(load causing major-axis bending)}$$

$$w_y = w_w \cdot s \cdot \sin\theta \quad \text{(load causing minor-axis bending)}$$

where `s` = spacing of purlins (in m) along the slope.

> **Simplified Approach (KTU Standard):** For a roof slope θ, the total vertical/load is assumed to act vertically. The component causing minor-axis bending is taken as a percentage:
> - For slope **θ ≤ 10°**: load is mostly vertical (negligible minor axis bending)
> - For slope **θ > 10°**: minor axis bending component = `w × sin θ` or as per IS 875 load components

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Check | Expression | Reference (IS 800:2007) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Major axis BM | $M_z = \dfrac{w_z L^2}{8}$ | Cl. 8.2.1 |
| 2 | Minor axis BM | $M_y = \dfrac{w_y L^2}{8}$ | Cl. 8.2.1 |
| 3 | Section Modulus required | $Z_{pz,req} = \dfrac{M_z}{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}$ | Cl. 8.2.1.2 |
| 4 | Check: Plastic/Compact | $\dfrac{b}{t_f} \leq 9.4\varepsilon$ for O/S of flange | Table 2 (IS 800) |
| 5 | Shear capacity (web) | $V_{dz} = \dfrac{f_{yw} \cdot h_w \cdot t_w}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}}$ | Cl. 8.4 |
| 6 | Deflection limit | $\delta_{max} \leq \dfrac{L}{150}$ | Cl. 8.7.2 |
| 7 | Deflection (UDL) | $\delta = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}$ | Standard formula |
| 8 | Lateral-torsional buckling check | $M_d \leq M_{dv}$ (cl. 8.2.2.1) | Cl. 8.2.2 |
| 9 | Combined bending check | $\dfrac{M_z}{M_{dz}} + \dfrac{M_y}{M_{dy}} \leq 1.0$ | Cl. 9.3.1.2 |
| 10 | Load factor (LSM) | $\gamma_f$ for DL = 1.2, LL = 1.2, WL = 1.2 | Cl. 9.3.2 |
| 11 | $\varepsilon$ (yield strain factor) | $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250 / f_y}$ | Cl. 2.2.4.2 |
| 12 | Plastic section modulus check (compact) | $M_{dz} = \beta_b \cdot Z_{pz} \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Cl. 8.2.1.2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Tip:** Always use **`ε = √(250/f_y)`** correctly. For `f_y = 250 MPa`, `ε = 1.0`. For `f_y = 410 MPa` (current trend), `ε = 0.78`. This is a common trick examiners use to test students' understanding.

## 2.5 Section Classification (as per Table 2 of IS 800:2007)

For an **I-Section (rolled)** flange (outstand):

| Class | b/t_f ratio | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Plastic | $\leq 9.4\varepsilon$ | Full redistribution possible |
| Compact | $\leq 10.5\varepsilon$ | Plastic moment capacity |
| Semi-compact | $\leq 15.7\varepsilon$ | Elastic moment only |
| Slender | $> 15.7\varepsilon$ | Reduced capacity (avoid) |

For an **I-Section (rolled)** web:

| Class | d/t_w ratio | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Plastic | $\leq 84\varepsilon$ | — |
| Compact | $\leq 105\varepsilon$ | — |
| Semi-compact | $\leq 126\varepsilon$ | — |

> **Why This Matters:** If section is **semi-compact**, you must use the **elastic section modulus (Z_ez)**, not plastic (Z_pz). For a symmetric I-section, `Z_e = I_xx / (h/2)`.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Design Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Design Procedure for I-Section Purlin (LSM Method)

The exhaustive KTU-recommended design procedure is given below in **10 sequential steps**.

### **Step 1: Define the Problem & Geometric Inputs**

- Span of purlin (between trusses): `L` (m)
- Spacing of purlins along slope: `s` (m)
- Roof slope: `θ` (degrees)
- Type of sheeting + sheeting weight
- Wind zone / basic wind speed `v_b` (m/s)

### **Step 2: Compute Unfactored Loads (kN/m²)**

- DL of sheeting: 0.10 – 0.20 kN/m²
- DL of purlin: assume ~ 0.10 – 0.15 kN/m²
- LL: 0.75 kN/m² (for slope ≤ 10°)
- WL: Use `p_d = 0.6 v_b^2` (Kerala wind zones typically `v_b = 39` or `44` m/s)

### **Step 3: Compute Factored Loads on Purlin (kN/m)**

**Load per metre of purlin length** = (Load per m²) × spacing `s`

For combination Case 1: `w_u = 1.2 (DL + LL) × s`
For combination Case 4: `w_u = (0.9 DL - 1.2 WL) × s` (uplift)

### **Step 4: Resolve Loads into Major & Minor Axis Components**

For a roof with slope θ (in degrees), the **vertical load** `w_v` (per m) and **load along slope** `w_s` (per m):

$$w_z = w_v \cdot \cos\theta \quad (\text{component perpendicular to purlin web})$$

$$w_y = w_v \cdot \sin\theta \quad (\text{component along roof slope})$$

> Wait — careful with sign convention. For a **roof slope**, the load acts **vertically downward** (gravity), and is resolved into:
> - `w_z = w_v · cos θ` → causes **major axis bending** (bending about Z-Z)
> - `w_y = w_v · sin θ` → causes **minor axis bending** (bending about Y-Y)

For **wind load** acting **perpendicular to roof** (pressure case):
- The wind force has both components, but is usually assumed to act normal to the roof surface
- Wind pressure on slope: $w_w$ per m²
- Component normal to roof = $w_w · \cos\theta$ (causes major-axis bending)
- Component along roof plane (tangential) = $w_w · \sin\theta$ (causes minor-axis bending)

### **Step 5: Compute Bending Moments**

For a simply supported purlin:

$$M_z = \frac{w_z \cdot L^2}{8} \quad \text{kN·m}$$

$$M_y = \frac{w_y \cdot L^2}{8} \quad \text{kN·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For purlins on **gantry/portal** buildings, sag rods at mid-span reduce the effective minor axis span to `L/2`, and the minor axis moment becomes:
> $$M_y = \frac{w_y \cdot (L/2)^2}{8} = \frac{w_y \cdot L^2}{32}$$
> **This is a guaranteed KTU exam question!**

### **Step 6: Select Trial I-Section**

Use a section property table (SP 6-1 / IS 808). For example:
- ISMB 150, ISMB 200, ISMB 250, ISMB 300, ISMB 400, etc.
- Note: `Z_ez` (elastic section modulus), `Z_ez,y` (about Y-axis), `I_xx`, `I_yy`, `r_yy`, `b_f`, `t_f`, `t_w`, `h_w`

### **Step 7: Check Section Classification**

For `f_y = 250 MPa`, `ε = 1.0`:
- Plastic class for flange: `b_f / 2t_f ≤ 9.4` → `b/t_f ≤ 9.4`
- Plastic class for web: `d / t_w ≤ 84`

If section is **plastic/compact**, design strength = `f_y · Z_pz / γ_{m0}`
If section is **semi-compact**, design strength = `f_y · Z_ez / γ_{m0}`

### **Step 8: Check Moment Capacity**

Design bending strength about Z-Z:

$$M_{dz} = \frac{\beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} \quad \text{(compact/plastic)}$$

or

$$M_{dz} = \frac{Z_{ez} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} \quad \text{(semi-compact)}$$

where `β_b = 1.0` for I-section (positive BM at mid-span).

Design bending strength about Y-Y:

$$M_{dy} = \frac{Z_{ey} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} \quad \text{(no LTB effect about Y-Y)}$$

### **Step 9: Check Combined Bending (CRITICAL CHECK)**

$$\frac{M_z}{M_{dz}} + \frac{M_y}{M_{dy}} \leq 1.0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If this ratio is **less than 1.0** → Section is **safe**.
> If it is **> 1.0** → **Revise** the section (use a heavier one).

### **Step 10: Check Deflection**

For simply supported beam with UDL:

$$\delta = \frac{5 w_{service} L^4}{384 E I_{xx}}$$

Check: $\delta \leq L/150$

For sag rods at mid-span, deflection along minor axis (Y-direction):

$$\delta_y = \frac{5 w_y (L/2)^4}{384 E I_{yy}} \leq \frac{L/2}{150}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Design Example

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**

Design an I-Section purlin for an industrial building roof with the following data:
- Span of purlin (between trusses): `L = 6.0 m`
- Spacing of purlins along slope: `s = 1.5 m`
- Roof slope: `θ = 15°`
- Type of sheeting: GI corrugated sheets, weight = 0.130 kN/m²
- Imposed load (LL) on roof slope: 0.75 kN/m² (slope ≤ 10°). For slope = 15°, use reduction factor
- Wind pressure (normal to roof): `p_d = 1.5 kN/m²` (after exposure factors, for Kerala coastal)
- Provide sag rods at mid-span to reduce minor axis span.
- Use **Fe 410 grade steel**: `f_y = 250 MPa`, `E = 2 × 10⁵ MPa`
- `γ_{m0} = 1.10`, `γ_{f,DL} = 1.2`, `γ_{f,LL} = 1.2`, `γ_{f,WL} = 1.2`

---

### **Solution:**

#### **Step 1: Geometric Inputs**
- L = 6.0 m, s = 1.5 m, θ = 15°

#### **Step 2: Unfactored Loads (kN/m²)**

| Load Type | Value (kN/m²) |
|---|---|
| Sheeting DL | 0.130 |
| Purlin DL (assumed) | 0.100 |
| Total DL | **0.230** |
| LL (slope = 15°, reduce to 0.5625 kN/m² per IS 875) | 0.5625 |
| Wind pressure (normal to roof) | 1.500 |
| Wind suction (uplift) | −1.500 |

> **Note (IS 875 Part 2):** For roof slope between 10° and 30°, imposed load reduces linearly from 0.75 to 0 kN/m².
> For 15°: LL = 0.75 − (5/20)·0.75 = 0.75 · (15/20) = 0.5625 kN/m²

#### **Step 3: Factored Loads on Purlin (kN/m)**

- Factored DL per m: `w_DL = 1.2 × 0.230 × 1.5 = 0.414 kN/m`
- Factored LL per m: `w_LL = 1.2 × 0.5625 × 1.5 = 1.0125 kN/m`
- Factored WL (pressure) per m: `w_WL = 1.2 × 1.5 × 1.5 = 2.7 kN/m` (perpendicular to roof)
- Factored WL (suction) per m: `w_WL,suction = −1.2 × 1.5 × 1.5 = −2.7 kN/m`
- Factored DL (for suction case): `w_DL = 0.9 × 0.230 × 1.5 = 0.3105 kN/m`

#### **Step 4: Try Load Combination 1 — DL + LL (Gravity, no wind)**

Total unfactored vertical load: `w_v = (DL + LL) × s = (0.230 + 0.5625) × 1.5 = 1.18875 kN/m`

Factored: `w_u = 1.2 × 1.18875 = 1.4265 kN/m`

Resolve into Z and Y components (acting vertically downward, slope 15°):
- `w_z = 1.4265 × cos 15° = 1.4265 × 0.9659 = 1.3779 kN/m`
- `w_y = 1.4265 × sin 15° = 1.4265 × 0.2588 = 0.3692 kN/m`

> **Question:** Why is the load resolved using `cos θ` and `sin θ`?
> **Answer:** A vertical downward load on a sloped roof has two components: one perpendicular to the purlin's major axis (causing bending about Z) and one along the roof slope (causing bending about Y). The vertical force vector makes angle `θ` with the normal to roof, so its normal component = `W cos θ` and tangential = `W sin θ`.

#### **Step 5: Bending Moments**

- Effective span for major axis: `L = 6.0 m`
- Effective span for minor axis (with sag rod at mid-span): `L_y = L/2 = 3.0 m`

$$M_z = \frac{w_z \cdot L^2}{8} = \frac{1.3779 \times 6.0^2}{8} = \frac{49.6044}{8} = 6.2006 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_y = \frac{w_y \cdot (L/2)^2}{8} = \frac{0.3692 \times 3.0^2}{8} = \frac{3.3228}{8} = 0.4154 \text{ kN·m}$$

> **For KTU 2024 marks:** This step of computing `M_z` and `M_y` separately by resolving the load is a guaranteed **3-4 mark** sub-question. Always show both components.

#### **Step 6: Section Selection (Trial: ISMB 200)**

Properties of **ISMB 200**:
- Depth, `h` = 200 mm
- Flange width, `b_f` = 100 mm
- Flange thickness, `t_f` = 10.8 mm
- Web thickness, `t_w` = 5.7 mm
- `I_xx` = 2235.4 cm⁴ = 2235.4 × 10⁴ mm⁴
- `I_yy` = 113.9 cm⁴ = 113.9 × 10⁴ mm⁴
- `Z_ez` (elastic, about Z) = 223.5 cm³ = 223.5 × 10³ mm³
- `Z_ey` (elastic, about Y) = 22.78 cm³ = 22.78 × 10³ mm³
- `Z_pz` (plastic, about Z) = 254.5 cm³ (approx.)
- Mass = 24.21 kg/m → self-weight DL = 0.2421 × 9.81/1000 = 0.00238 kN/m? — actually **0.237 kN/m** (more accurate: mass × g = 0.242 × 9.81 / 1000 ≈ 0.0024 kN/m — wrong. **Mass in kg/m × 9.81 / 1000 = kN/m**; so 24.21 × 9.81/1000 = 0.2375 kN/m)

> **Correction on Purlin self-weight:** 24.21 kg/m × 9.81/1000 = **0.2376 kN/m** (= 0.2376 kN/m)
> We had assumed 0.100 kN/m; let's **revise to 0.2376 kN/m** and recompute, or **stick with assumed value for first trial** and revise at end.

**Recompute total DL:** 0.130 + 0.2376 = 0.3676 kN/m²

#### **Step 7: Section Classification Check**

For `f_y = 250 MPa, ε = 1.0`:
- Flange outstand `b' = (b_f − t_w)/2 = (100 − 5.7)/2 = 47.15 mm`
- Flange ratio: `b'/t_f = 47.15 / 10.8 = 4.37`
- Limit for plastic class: `9.4 × ε = 9.4`
- Since `4.37 < 9.4` → **Flange is Plastic Class** ✓
- Web ratio: `d/t_w = (200 − 2 × 10.8)/5.7 = 178.4/5.7 = 31.30`
- Limit for plastic class: `84 × ε = 84`
- Since `31.30 < 84` → **Web is Plastic Class** ✓

So **ISMB 200 is a Plastic Section**, and we can use `Z_pz` for design.

#### **Step 8: Moment Capacity of ISMB 200**

$$M_{dz} = \frac{Z_{pz} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{254.5 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} \times 10^{-6}$$

$$M_{dz} = \frac{254.5 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} = 57.84 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 57.84 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{dy} = \frac{Z_{ey} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{22.78 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} = 5.18 \text{ kN·m}$$

#### **Step 9: Combined Bending Check**

$$\frac{M_z}{M_{dz}} + \frac{M_y}{M_{dy}} = \frac{6.2006}{57.84} + \frac{0.4154}{5.18}$$

$$= 0.1072 + 0.0802 = 0.1874$$

Since **0.1874 < 1.0** → Section is **SAFE** ✓ (Massive over-design!)

> **KTU Insight:** The low utilization ratio (0.187) suggests we can use a **lighter section**. Let's try **ISMB 150** next.

---

### **Re-trial with ISMB 150**

Properties of **ISMB 150**:
- `h` = 150 mm, `b_f` = 80 mm, `t_f` = 8.0 mm, `t_w` = 4.4 mm
- `I_xx` = 720.4 cm⁴, `I_yy` = 45.27 cm⁴
- `Z_ez` = 96.05 cm³, `Z_ey` = 11.32 cm³
- `Z_pz` ≈ 110.4 cm³
- Mass = 14.0 kg/m → DL = 14.0 × 9.81/1000 = 0.137 kN/m

**Recompute DL:** 0.130 + 0.137 = 0.267 kN/m² → negligible change. Stick with old DL ≈ 0.368 kN/m² for simplicity.

#### **Moment Capacity (ISMB 150)**

$$M_{dz} = \frac{110.4 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} = 25.09 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{dy} = \frac{11.32 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} = 2.573 \text{ kN·m}$$

#### **Combined Bending Check**

$$\frac{6.2006}{25.09} + \frac{0.4154}{2.573} = 0.2471 + 0.1615 = 0.4086$$

Since **0.4086 < 1.0** → **SAFE** ✓ (utilization ~41%)

#### **Deflection Check (ISMB 150)**

**Service load (unfactored):**
- `w_service = (DL + LL) × s = (0.368 + 0.5625) × 1.5 = 1.3958 kN/m`
- Major axis deflection: $\delta_z = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E I_{xx}} = \dfrac{5 \times 1.3958 \times (6000)^4}{384 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 720.4 \times 10^4}$

$$\delta_z = \frac{5 \times 1.3958 \times 1.296 \times 10^{15}}{5.529 \times 10^{12}} = \frac{9.045 \times 10^{15}}{5.529 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$\delta_z = 1.636 \text{ mm}$$

- Permissible: `L/150 = 6000/150 = 40 mm`
- `1.636 < 40` ✓ — **Well within limit**

#### **Final Selection: ISMB 150 is safe and economical.**

The **critical load combination** was Case 1 (DL + LL). Let us also verify Case 4 (uplift):

**Case 4: 0.9 DL + 1.2 WL (suction)**
- `w_u = 0.9 × 0.368 × 1.5 - 1.2 × 1.5 × 1.5 = 0.4968 - 2.7 = -2.2032 kN/m` (uplift)
- Major axis: $M_z = 2.2032 \times 6^2 / 8 = 9.914$ kN·m
- But `M_dz` = 25.09 kN·m, so still safe.
- However, **wind suction creates reversal of stress**; the bottom flange which was in compression becomes in tension. The lateral stability of bottom flange now needs check.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Board Exam (8–10 marks):** The full design of an I-section purlin is a **standard 14-mark question**. The key steps examiners look for: (1) Load combinations, (2) Resolution of load into Z and Y components, (3) Section classification, (4) Combined bending check, (5) Deflection check. Skipping deflection will cost you 2 marks guaranteed.

# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — I-Section Purlin Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design I-Section Purlin] --> B[Step 1: Input Geometric Data]
    B --> C[Step 2: Span L, Spacing s, Slope theta]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Unfactored Loads]
    D --> E[DL: Sheeting + Purlin Self-weight]
    D --> F[LL: As per IS 875 Part 2]
    D --> G[WL: As per IS 875 Part 3]
    E --> H[Step 4: Apply Load Factors and Combinations]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step 5: Resolve Loads into Z and Y Components]
    I --> J[Step 6: Compute Mz and My]
    J --> K[Step 7: Select Trial I-Section from IS 808]
    K --> L[Step 8: Classify Section Plastic / Compact / Semi-compact]
    L --> M{Section is Safe Class?}
    M -->|No| N[Revise to Heavier Section]
    N --> K
    M -->|Yes| O[Step 9: Compute Mdz and Mdy]
    O --> P[Step 10: Combined Bending Check Mz/Mdz + My/Mdy]
    P --> Q{Combined Ratio Less Than 1?}
    Q -->|No| N
    Q -->|Yes| R[Step 11: Deflection Check delta less than L by 150]
    R --> S{Deflection OK?}
    S -->|No| N
    S -->|Yes| T[Step 12: Final Selection of I-Section]
    T --> U[End: Provide Sag Rods and Connections]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Load Path & Structural Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Roof Sheeting] -->|DL + LL + WL| B1[Purlin I-Section]
    B1 -->|Reaction at End| C1[Roof Truss or Rafter]
    C1 -->|Vertical Reaction| D1[Column]
    D1 -->|Axial Load| E1[Foundation]
    F1[Wind Action] -->|Pressure/Suction| A1
    G1[Sag Rod at Mid-Span] -.->|Lateral Restraint| B1
    B1 -->|Bending about Z-Z| H1[Major Axis Stress]
    B1 -->|Bending about Y-Y| I1[Minor Axis Stress]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Force Resolution on Sloped Roof

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vertical Gravity Load W] --> B[Resolve at Slope theta]
    B --> C[Component along Roof Normal W cos theta]
    B --> D[Component along Roof Slope W sin theta]
    C --> E[Major Axis Bending Mz about Z-Z]
    D --> F[Minor Axis Bending My about Y-Y]
    E --> G[Maximum Stress at Extreme Flange Fibres]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Combined Bending Check per IS 800]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Rationale:** The Mermaid diagrams above present the **load transfer hierarchy** of a typical industrial building roof, the **design workflow** for an I-section purlin, and the **force resolution** on a sloped roof. These conceptual block diagrams capture the essence of physical sketches typically drawn in board exams — which are often difficult to render perfectly in Mermaid. This format is **board-exam-friendly** for explaining concepts verbally as well.

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Define a purlin. What is the role of sag rods in the design of I-section purlins on sloped roofs?**

**Model Answer:**
A **purlin** is a horizontal secondary structural member spanning between rafters or roof trusses that supports the roof sheeting and transfers the roof loads (DL, LL, WL) to the main trusses.

**Sag Rods** are tension members provided at intermediate points (typically mid-span or at 1/3 points) along the purlin length, running **parallel to the roof slope** along the ridge and eave lines.

**Role of Sag Rods:**
1. They prevent the **lateral buckling / torsional rotation** of the purlin's bottom flange.
2. They reduce the **effective minor-axis span** of the purlin. With a sag rod at mid-span, the minor-axis moment `M_y` reduces from `w_y L²/8` to `w_y (L/2)²/8 = w_y L²/32` — a **75% reduction**.
3. They transfer the accumulated horizontal force from purlins to the bracing system at gable ends.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse sag rods with tie rods. **Sag rods are in TENSION** (resist sagging), while **tie rods** connect purlins to the rafter top chord. Don't mix these up in your answer.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**State the deflection limit for purlins as per IS 800:2007. Why is the deflection check critical for purlins even when the section passes the strength check?**

**Model Answer:**
As per **IS 800:2007, Cl. 8.7.2**, the maximum permissible deflection for a purlin supporting **metal sheeting** is:

$$\delta_{max} \leq \frac{L}{150} \text{ (for live load and wind load cases)}$$

**Why is the deflection check critical?**
1. **Serviceability concern:** Excessive deflection causes **ponding of rainwater** on flat roofs and **visible sagging** on sloped roofs, leading to user discomfort.
2. **Damage to cladding:** Excessive deflection may **crack the roof sheeting** or break the fasteners.
3. **Buckling precursor:** Large deflection in the plane of the bottom flange can trigger **lateral-torsional buckling** of an unsupported purlin.
4. **Aesthetic & functional failure:** Even a "strong" section that deflects too much is **unfit for use**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** The deflection check uses **unfactored (service) loads** and **unfactored E (not reduced)**, while the strength check uses **factored loads**. Mixing these will give wrong results and lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 4 Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Design an I-section purlin for the following data using LSM:**
- Span of purlin (between truss panel points): `L = 5.0 m`
- Spacing of purlins along slope: `s = 1.4 m`
- Roof slope: `θ = 12°`
- Roof sheeting: AC sheets, weight = 0.150 kN/m²
- Imposed load: 0.75 kN/m² (slope ≤ 10°)
- Wind pressure: 1.2 kN/m² normal to roof
- Sag rods are provided at mid-span
- Steel: Fe 410 (f_y = 250 MPa), E = 2 × 10⁵ MPa
- Use γ_m0 = 1.10, partial safety factors: γ_D = 1.2, γ_L = 1.2, γ_W = 1.2

**[a] Compute the design bending moments `M_z` and `M_y` for the gravity load case. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Unfactored Loads (kN/m²)**

| Load | Value (kN/m²) |
|---|---|
| Sheeting DL | 0.150 |
| Purlin DL (assume 0.150) | 0.150 |
| **Total DL** | **0.300** |
| LL (slope 12°): 0.75 − (2/20)·0.75 = 0.675 kN/m² | 0.675 |

**Step 2: Factored Vertical Load (Case 1: DL + LL)**
- `w_v = 1.2 × (0.300 + 0.675) × 1.4 = 1.2 × 0.975 × 1.4 = 1.638 kN/m`

**Step 3: Resolve into Z and Y components (θ = 12°)**
- `w_z = 1.638 × cos 12° = 1.638 × 0.9781 = 1.602 kN/m`
- `w_y = 1.638 × sin 12° = 1.638 × 0.2079 = 0.341 kN/m`

**Step 4: Bending Moments**
- Effective major-axis span = 5.0 m
- Effective minor-axis span (sag rod at mid-span) = 5.0/2 = 2.5 m

$$M_z = \frac{w_z \cdot L^2}{8} = \frac{1.602 \times 5.0^2}{8} = \frac{40.05}{8} = 5.006 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_y = \frac{w_y \cdot (L/2)^2}{8} = \frac{0.341 \times 2.5^2}{8} = \frac{2.131}{8} = 0.266 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 5: M_z and M_y (Final)**
- `M_z = 5.006 kN·m`
- `M_y = 0.266 kN·m`

> **Marking Distribution (7 marks):**
> - 2 marks: Load computation and resolution
> - 2 marks: Major-axis moment `M_z`
> - 2 marks: Minor-axis moment `M_y` (with sag rod logic)
> - 1 mark: Final answers boxed

---

**[b] Select a suitable ISMB section and verify the design as per IS 800:2007. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Try ISMB 150**
Properties:
- `h = 150 mm`, `b_f = 80 mm`, `t_f = 8.0 mm`, `t_w = 4.4 mm`
- `Z_pz = 110.4 cm³ = 110.4 × 10³ mm³`
- `Z_ey = 11.32 cm³ = 11.32 × 10³ mm³`
- `I_xx = 720.4 cm⁴`

**Step 2: Section Classification (Fe 410, ε = √(250/250) = 1.0)**
- Flange ratio: `b'/t_f = (80 − 4.4)/2 / 8.0 = 37.8/8.0 = 4.725`
- Limit for plastic: `9.4 × 1.0 = 9.4` → `4.725 < 9.4` ✓ (Plastic)
- Web ratio: `d/t_w = (150 − 2×8.0)/4.4 = 134/4.4 = 30.45`
- Limit for plastic: `84 × 1.0 = 84` → `30.45 < 84` ✓ (Plastic)
- **Section is Plastic → can use `Z_pz`**

**Step 3: Moment Capacity**

$$M_{dz} = \frac{Z_{pz} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{110.4 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} = 25.09 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{dy} = \frac{Z_{ey} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{11.32 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} = 2.573 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4: Combined Bending Check**

$$\frac{M_z}{M_{dz}} + \frac{M_y}{M_{dy}} = \frac{5.006}{25.09} + \frac{0.266}{2.573} = 0.1995 + 0.1034 = 0.303$$

Since `0.303 < 1.0` → **SAFE** ✓

**Step 5: Deflection Check (unfactored service load)**
- Service vertical load: `w_v,serv = (0.300 + 0.675) × 1.4 = 1.365 kN/m`
- Service Z-component: `w_z,serv = 1.365 × cos 12° = 1.335 kN/m`

$$\delta_z = \frac{5 \times 1.335 \times 5000^4}{384 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 720.4 \times 10^4}$$

$$= \frac{5 \times 1.335 \times 6.25 \times 10^{14}}{5.529 \times 10^{12}} = \frac{4.172 \times 10^{15}}{5.529 \times 10^{12}} = 754.5 \text{ mm} \text{ ???}$$

> **Recheck:** $\delta = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ where `w` must be in **N/mm**.
> `w = 1.335 kN/m = 1.335 N/mm`
> `L = 5000 mm`, `I = 720.4 × 10^4 = 7.204 × 10^6 mm⁴`

$$\delta_z = \frac{5 \times 1.335 \times 5000^4}{384 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 7.204 \times 10^6}$$

$$= \frac{5 \times 1.335 \times 6.25 \times 10^{14}}{5.529 \times 10^{14}} = \frac{4.172 \times 10^{15}}{5.529 \times 10^{14}} = 7.55 \text{ mm}$$

- Permissible: `L/150 = 5000/150 = 33.33 mm`
- `7.55 < 33.33` ✓ **SAFE**

**Step 6: Final Selection**

✅ **Adopt ISMB 150** for the purlin, with sag rod at mid-span.

> **Marking Distribution (7 marks):**
> - 1 mark: Section selection
> - 1 mark: Classification check
> - 2 marks: Moment capacity computation
> - 2 marks: Combined bending check
> - 1 mark: Deflection check + final selection

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Two alternative I-section purlins are to be compared for the same roof:**
- **Option 1:** ISMB 200 (rolled section)
- **Option 2:** Built-up I-section from plates: Flanges 200 × 12 mm, Web 400 × 8 mm

**Given Data:**
- Span L = 4.5 m
- Slope θ = 10°
- Vertical load on purlin = 4.0 kN/m (factored, including DL + LL + WL)
- Sag rod at mid-span
- f_y = 250 MPa, γ_m0 = 1.10

**[a] Compute the moment capacity of the built-up section and compare with ISMB 200. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Built-up I-section Properties:**

- Total depth: `h = 12 + 400 + 12 = 424 mm`
- Flange width: `b_f = 200 mm`
- Web depth: `d = 400 mm`
- Flange thickness: `t_f = 12 mm`
- Web thickness: `t_w = 8 mm`

**Moment of Inertia about Z-Z (I_xx):**
- Web contribution: `I_{xx,web} = t_w \cdot d^3 / 12 = 8 × 400^3 / 12 = 42.67 × 10^6 mm^4`
- Flange contribution (2 nos): $I_{xx,flange} = 2 \times [b_f \cdot t_f^3 / 12 + b_f \cdot t_f \cdot (d/2 + t_f/2)^2]$
  - $2 \times [200 × 12^3 / 12 + 200 × 12 × (200 + 6)^2]$
  - $2 \times [28800 + 2400 × 42436]$
  - $2 \times [28800 + 101.85 × 10^6] = 2 \times 101.88 × 10^6 = 203.76 × 10^6 mm^4$

- Total: $I_{xx} = 42.67 × 10^6 + 203.76 × 10^6 = 246.43 × 10^6 mm^4 = 24643 cm^4$

**Plastic Section Modulus (Z_pz):**
For a symmetric I-section, plastic NA is at mid-height.
$$Z_{pz} = 2 \left[ b_f \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\frac{h - t_f}{2}\right) + \frac{t_w \cdot d}{2} \cdot \frac{d}{4} \right]$$

$$= 2 \left[ 200 × 12 × \left(\frac{424 - 12}{2}\right) + \frac{8 × 400}{2} × \frac{400}{4} \right]$$

$$= 2 \left[ 200 × 12 × 206 + 1600 × 100 \right]$$

$$= 2 \left[ 494400 + 160000 \right] = 2 × 654400 = 1308800 mm^3 = 1308.8 cm^3$$

**Moment Capacity of Built-up Section:**
$$M_{dz,built-up} = \frac{Z_{pz} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{1308.8 × 10^3 × 250}{1.10} = 297.5 kN·m$$

**Moment Capacity of ISMB 200:**
$$M_{dz,ISMB200} = \frac{254.5 × 10^3 × 250}{1.10} = 57.84 kN·m$$

> **Comparison:** Built-up section has **5× more capacity** but uses **5× more material** — hence rolled sections are economical for typical purlin spans.

---

**[b] Determine the maximum load the ISMB 200 can carry safely under combined biaxial bending, with θ = 10° and sag rod at mid-span. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Section Properties of ISMB 200**
- `M_{dz} = 57.84 kN·m`, `M_{dy} = 5.18 kN·m`

**Step 2: For θ = 10°**
- `w_z = w × cos 10° = 0.9848 w`
- `w_y = w × sin 10° = 0.1736 w`

**Step 3: Bending Moments**
- `M_z = w_z × L²/8 = 0.9848w × 4.5²/8 = 0.9848w × 2.531 = 2.493 w`
- `M_y = w_y × (L/2)²/8 = 0.1736w × 2.25²/8 = 0.1736w × 0.6328 = 0.1099 w`

**Step 4: Apply Combined Bending Equation (with equality for max load):**
$$\frac{M_z}{M_{dz}} + \frac{M_y}{M_{dy}} = 1.0$$

$$\frac{2.493 w}{57.84} + \frac{0.1099 w}{5.18} = 1.0$$

$$0.0431 w + 0.02122 w = 1.0$$

$$0.06432 w = 1.0$$

$$w_{max} = 15.55 \text{ kN/m}$$

> **Answer:** ISMB 200 can safely carry a maximum **factored load of 15.55 kN/m** under the given biaxial bending condition.

> **Marking Distribution (7 marks):**
> - 2 marks: Built-up section property computation
> - 2 marks: Moment capacity comparison
> - 2 marks: Combined bending equation setup
> - 1 mark: Final w_max

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Points (Don't Fall Into These Traps!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to resolve the load into Z and Y components** (cost: 2 marks). On a sloped roof, simply using `wL²/8` for `M_z` without resolving gives wrong answer.
>
> 2. **Not using sag rods** (cost: 1-2 marks). If the problem says "sag rods are provided at mid-span," you **MUST** reduce the minor-axis span to `L/2`. Ignoring this gives a 4× larger `M_y` and a "fail" verdict on the combined check.
>
> 3. **Mixing up factored vs. unfactored loads** (cost: 1-2 marks). Strength checks use **factored loads**; deflection check uses **unfactored service loads**. Use `γ_f` correctly.
>
> 4. **Section classification step skipped** (cost: 1 mark). Always check `b/t_f` and `d/t_w` ratios. For semi-compact sections, you cannot use `Z_pz`; you must use `Z_ez`.
>
> 5. **Forgetting `γ_m0 = 1.10` in denominator** (cost: 1 mark). The design strength formula ALWAYS has `γ_m0` in the denominator.
>
> 6. **Deflection unit mismatch** (cost: 1 mark). Use consistent units: `w in N/mm`, `L in mm`, `E in N/mm²`, `I in mm⁴` → `δ in mm`. Don't mix kN with mm.
>
> 7. **Not checking wind suction case** (cost: 2 marks). For Kerala coastal zones with light roofs, the **uplift case (0.9 DL − 1.2 WL)** often governs. KTU expects you to mention this.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 🎯 **Rapid Revision Checklist (for last-minute exam prep):**

- [x] **Purlin** = secondary member supporting roof sheeting; spans between rafters/trusses.
- [x] **I-Section purlin** uses rolled ISMB sections (IS 808).
- [x] **Loads**: DL (sheeting + self-weight), LL (IS 875 Part 2), WL (IS 875 Part 3).
- [x] **Load combinations** (IS 800 Cl. 9.3.2): `1.2(DL+LL)`, `1.2(DL+LL+WL)`, `1.2(DL+WL)`, `0.9 DL + 1.2 WL` (critical uplift).
- [x] **Biaxial bending** — about Z-Z (major) and Y-Y (minor) axes.
- [x] **Sag rods** at mid-span → reduce effective minor-axis span to `L/2`, cutting `M_y` by 75%.
- [x] **Section classification** uses `ε = √(250/f_y)`. For `f_y = 250`, `ε = 1.0`.
- [x] **Plastic flange limit**: `b'/t_f ≤ 9.4ε`. **Plastic web limit**: `d/t_w ≤ 84ε`.
- [x] **Combined bending equation**: $\dfrac{M_z}{M_{dz}} + \dfrac{M_y}{M_{dy}} \leq 1.0$ (IS 800 Cl. 9.3.1.2).
- [x] **Deflection limit** = `L/150` (service load, IS 800 Cl. 8.7.2).
- [x] **γ_m0 = 1.10**, **γ_f = 1.2** for DL, LL, WL.
- [x] **Resolution rule**: `w_z = w cos θ`, `w_y = w sin θ` (θ = roof slope).
- [x] **Bending moments**: `M_z = w_z L²/8`, `M_y = w_y (L/2)²/8` (with mid-span sag rod).
- [x] **Deflection formula**: $\delta = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ — always use **unfactored w** and **unfactored E**.
- [x] **Kerala wind zones**: `v_b = 39 m/s` (most districts) or `44 m/s` (coastal). Wind suction is often the **critical case** for light roofs.
- [x] **Memorize IS 800:2007 Cl. numbers**: 8.2.1.2 (bending), 8.4 (shear), 8.7.2 (deflection), 9.3.1.2 (combined bending), 9.3.2 (load combinations), Table 2 (classification).

> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU board exams, the I-section purlin design problem is a "calculation-rich" question. Show **all** intermediate steps — the examiner is looking for the **methodology**, not just the final answer. A correct approach with a small arithmetic slip will still earn you **10/14 marks**; a wrong approach with a correct answer earns **0/14**.
